## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Damping in Indicating Instruments

**Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
Damping is the process of bringing the moving system of a measuring instrument to rest at its equilibrium (true) position quickly and smoothly, without excessive oscillations or time delay. It is the dissipation of kinetic energy of the moving system during transient response.

**Conceptual Analogy (Real-World Intuition):**
Imagine pushing a child on a swing. If you push once and walk away, the swing oscillates back and forth many times before stopping due to air friction. If you attach a small underwater paddle (a damper), the swing stops almost immediately. The **damper** converts the swing's kinetic energy into heat (or eddy currents), preventing prolonged oscillation. In instruments, the pointer (or moving coil/iron) acts like the swing, and damping mechanisms act like that underwater paddle.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction for KTU Exams:**
> - **Damping** is NOT friction in the mechanical brake sense. It is a controlled energy-dissipation process designed to bring the pointer to rest at the *correct* deflection (deflecting torque = control torque).
> - The three standard methods prescribed by KTU syllabus are: **Air Friction**, **Fluid Friction**, and **Eddy Current Damping**.

### 1.2 Air Friction Damping

**Definition:** Damping achieved by means of a light piston (or vane) moving inside a closed air chamber. As the piston moves, air is forced through the small clearance between the piston and chamber walls, creating a retarding force proportional to the velocity of motion.

**Intuition:** Like the pneumatic door-closer — the air trapped in a cylinder resists the movement of a piston pushing into it, slowing it down smoothly.

### 1.3 Fluid Friction Damping

**Definition:** Damping achieved by immersing a light vane/disc in a viscous fluid (typically oil). The viscosity of the fluid provides a retarding force proportional to the angular velocity of the moving system.

**Intuition:** Like stirring a spoon in thick honey — the honey resists the spoon's motion, dissipating its kinetic energy into heat through viscous shear.

### 1.4 Eddy Current Damping

**Definition:** Damping produced by the interaction between a magnetic field and the eddy currents induced in a conducting material (usually a metal disc or former) that moves through the field. By Lenz's law, these induced currents oppose the motion that caused them, producing a braking torque.

**Intuition:** Like a magnet dropped through a copper tube — the magnet falls slowly because eddy currents induced in the tube walls create an opposing magnetic field, converting kinetic energy into heat.

### 1.5 Permanent Magnet Moving Coil (PMMC) Instrument

**Definition:** An indicating instrument that uses a current-carrying coil suspended (or pivoted) in the field of a permanent magnet. The interaction produces a deflecting torque proportional to the average current, making it suitable only for **DC measurements**.

### 1.6 Moving Iron (MI) Instrument

**Definition:** An instrument whose operating principle relies on the force of attraction or repulsion between a fixed iron piece and a movable iron piece, both magnetized by the current under measurement. The deflecting torque varies as the square of the current, making it suitable for both **AC and DC measurements**.

### 1.7 Range Extension — Shunts and Multipliers

**Definition:** Auxiliary precision resistors used to extend the measurable range of basic meter movements.
- **Shunt (Rs):** A low-value resistor connected in **parallel** with an ammeter to measure currents higher than the meter's full-scale current.
- **Multiplier (Rs):** A high-value resistor connected in **series** with a voltmeter to measure voltages higher than the meter's full-scale voltage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Frequently Tested Points:**
> 1. Air and Fluid Friction Damping → Damping torque ∝ angular velocity → **T_d ∝ dθ/dt** (linear velocity-dependent damping).
> 2. Eddy Current Damping → Damping torque ∝ angular velocity → **T_d ∝ dθ/dt** (also linear).
> 3. PMMC instruments cannot be used directly for AC because the alternating torque averages to zero.
> 4. MI instruments can measure both AC and DC, but the scale is inherently **non-linear (square-law)**.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 General Equation of Motion of a Damped Instrument

The pointer of any indicating instrument obeys the second-order differential equation:

$$J \frac{d^2\theta}{dt^2} + D \frac{d\theta}{dt} + K\theta = T_d$$

Where:
- $J$ = Moment of inertia of the moving system (kg·m²)
- $D$ = Damping coefficient (N·m·s/rad)
- $K$ = Control/spring constant (N·m/rad)
- $T_d$ = Deflecting torque (N·m)
- $\theta$ = Angular displacement (rad)

The three terms represent **inertia**, **damping**, and **restoring** torques respectively.

### 2.2 Damping Systems — Operating Principles

#### 2.2.1 Air Friction Damping
- A light aluminium **piston** is attached to the moving system spindle.
- The piston moves inside a fixed **air chamber** (cylindrical cavity) with very small radial clearance.
- As the piston moves in/out, air is compressed/decompressed → creates a retarding pressure.
- **Damping torque equation:** $T_d = c_1 \cdot \dfrac{d\theta}{dt}$
- Where $c_1$ depends on piston geometry and clearance.

**Constructional Variants:**
1. **Piston-in-Cylinder type** (most common)
2. **Vane-in-Cavity type** (vane attached to spindle swings between two chambers)

#### 2.2.2 Fluid Friction Damping
- A light disc/vane is immersed in a **damping fluid** (oil — typically silicone or mineral oil).
- The viscous drag of the fluid on the vane provides the damping force.
- **Damping torque equation:** $T_d = c_2 \cdot \dfrac{d\theta}{dt}$
- Where $c_2$ depends on fluid viscosity, vane area, and geometry.

#### 2.2.3 Eddy Current Damping
- A thin conducting disc (usually aluminium or copper) is attached to the moving system spindle.
- The disc is positioned in the field of a **permanent magnet** (placed in such a way that the disc cuts the magnetic flux lines when it rotates).
- When the disc rotates in the magnetic field, **eddy currents** are induced in it (Faraday's Law).
- These currents interact with the magnetic field to produce a **Lenz's law opposing torque**.
- **Damping torque equation:** $T_d = c_3 \cdot \dfrac{d\theta}{dt}$
- Where $c_3$ depends on magnetic flux density, disc conductivity, thickness, and active radius.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Insight:** All three damping methods produce a torque **proportional to angular velocity**. They differ in the *physical mechanism* by which kinetic energy is dissipated (viscous shear of air/fluid vs. electromagnetic induction). Eddy current damping is the most commonly used in **PMMC instruments**.

### 2.3 Permanent Magnet Moving Coil (PMMC) — Deflecting Torque

Consider a rectangular coil of $N$ turns, carrying current $I$, with active area $A = l \times b$, suspended in a uniform radial magnetic flux density $B$.

The force on each current-carrying conductor of length $l$ perpendicular to $B$ is:

$$F = BIl \text{ (per side, Newtons)}$$

The torque produced (with moment arm $b/2$ for each side):

$$T_d = 2 \times (BIl) \times \frac{b}{2} = NBIA$$

At equilibrium, deflecting torque = control torque:

$$NBIA = K\theta$$

$$\boxed{\theta = \dfrac{NBIA}{K}}$$

This shows that **deflection is directly proportional to current** → **uniform linear scale** in PMMC.

### 2.4 Moving Iron (MI) — Deflecting Torque

The operating principle is based on the force between a fixed and a movable iron piece, both magnetized by the current $I$.

The energy stored in the magnetic field of the iron system is:

$$W = \dfrac{1}{2} L I^2$$

The deflecting torque (derived from co-energy principle):

$$T_d = \dfrac{1}{2} I^2 \dfrac{dL}{d\theta}$$

At equilibrium: $T_d = K\theta$

$$\boxed{\theta = \dfrac{1}{2K} I^2 \dfrac{dL}{d\theta}}$$

**Two Constructional Types:**
1. **Attraction (or Plain) type** — A single piece of iron is attracted toward a current-carrying coil.
2. **Repulsion (or Double-Iron) type** — Two parallel iron vanes (one fixed, one movable) are magnetized with the same polarity and repel each other.

### 2.5 Range Extension — Complete Theory

#### 2.5.1 Ammeter Shunt (Parallel Resistance)

Let the basic meter (movement) have:
- Internal resistance: $R_m$
- Full-scale deflection current: $I_m$

To extend the range to measure current $I$ (where $I > I_m$), a shunt resistance $R_s$ is connected in parallel.

By **current divider rule** (or KCL: $I \cdot R_s = I_m \cdot R_m$ if meter and shunt are in parallel voltage equality):

$$I_m R_m = (I - I_m) R_s$$

$$R_s = \dfrac{R_m \cdot I_m}{I - I_m}$$

Define **multiplying factor** $m$:

$$m = \dfrac{I}{I_m} \implies I = m I_m$$

Substituting:

$$R_s = \dfrac{R_m}{m - 1}$$

#### 2.5.2 Voltmeter Multiplier (Series Resistance)

To extend the range to measure voltage $V$ (where $V > V_m = I_m R_m$), a multiplier $R_s$ is connected in series.

By **voltage divider / KVL** (same current flows through both):

$$I_m (R_m + R_s) = V$$

Define **multiplying factor** $n$:

$$n = \dfrac{V}{V_m} = \dfrac{V}{I_m R_m}$$

Substituting:

$$R_s = R_m (n - 1)$$

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Symbols / Units** | **Applicable Instrument** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Deflecting Torque (PMMC) | $T_d = N B I A$ | $N$=turns, $B$=T, $I$=A, $A$=m² | PMMC only |
| Deflection (PMMC) | $\theta = \dfrac{NBIA}{K}$ | $\theta$=rad, $K$=N·m/rad | PMMC |
| Deflecting Torque (MI) | $T_d = \dfrac{1}{2} I^2 \dfrac{dL}{d\theta}$ | $L$=H, $\theta$=rad | Moving Iron |
| Damping Torque (any) | $T_d = c \cdot \dfrac{d\theta}{dt}$ | $c$=damping coefficient | All three types |
| Shunt Resistance | $R_s = \dfrac{R_m}{m-1}$ | $m = I/I_m$ | Ammeter |
| Multiplier Resistance | $R_s = R_m(n-1)$ | $n = V/V_m$ | Voltmeter |
| Multiplying Factor (Ammeter) | $m = 1 + \dfrac{R_m}{R_s}$ | Dimensionless | Ammeter |
| Multiplying Factor (Voltmeter) | $n = 1 + \dfrac{R_s}{R_m}$ | Dimensionless | Voltmeter |
| Total Meter Resistance (Ammeter) | $R_{total} = \dfrac{R_m \cdot R_s}{R_m + R_s}$ | Ohms ($\Omega$) | Ammeter extended |
| Total Meter Resistance (Voltmeter) | $R_{total} = R_m + R_s$ | Ohms ($\Omega$) | Voltmeter extended |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The general equation of motion** $J\dfrac{d^2\theta}{dt^2} + D\dfrac{d\theta}{dt} + K\theta = T_d$ is **a compulsory 2-mark question** in KTU ESE Module 1. Memorize each term and its physical meaning.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **PMMC instruments** are the heart of every digital multimeter (after signal conditioning) and are used as **galvanometers** in bridges (Wheatstone, Kelvin, Megger).
2. **MI instruments** are preferred for **switchboard panels** in power stations because they handle AC + DC with a robust, simple construction (no delicate suspension).
3. **Eddy current damping** is universally used in automobile **speedometers** (Aluminium disc + permanent magnet) and in **electric energy meters**.
4. **Shunts and Multipliers** allow a single calibrated basic movement to be converted into a versatile multi-range instrument used in laboratories worldwide.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Deflecting Torque of a PMMC Instrument

**Given:** A rectangular coil of $N$ turns, carrying current $I$, suspended in a uniform radial magnetic field $B$, with coil dimensions length $l$ and breadth $b$.

**Step 1: Force on a Single Conductor**
For a current-carrying conductor of length $l$ in a magnetic field of flux density $B$:

$$F = BIl \quad \text{(Newtons)}$$

This force acts perpendicular to both the current direction and the magnetic field.

**Step 2: Identify Force-Producing Sides**
In a rectangular coil pivoted in a radial field, only the two sides of length $l$ (parallel to the spindle axis) experience force. The other two sides (length $b$) move along the field lines and experience **zero force** in a perfectly radial field.

**Step 3: Effective Force on the Coil**
There are $N$ turns, and 2 force-producing sides per turn. Total force:

$$F_{total} = 2N BIl$$

**Step 4: Compute Torque**
The moment arm for each force is $b/2$ (since the coil is pivoted at its center). Total torque:

$$T_d = F_{total} \times \dfrac{b}{2} = 2N BIl \times \dfrac{b}{2}$$

$$\boxed{T_d = NBIA}$$

Where $A = l \times b$ is the effective coil area.

**Step 5: Equilibrium Condition**
The control spring exerts a restoring torque $T_c = K\theta$. At steady-state deflection:

$$T_d = T_c \implies NBIA = K\theta$$

$$\boxed{\theta = \dfrac{NBIA}{K}}$$

This proves that **deflection $\theta \propto I$** → uniform scale.

### 3.2 Derivation: Deflecting Torque of a Moving Iron Instrument (Attraction Type)

**Given:** A fixed coil carrying current $I$ magnetizes an iron vane, attracting a pivoted soft-iron piece.

**Step 1: Energy Stored in the Magnetic System**
The inductance of the coil depends on the angular position $\theta$ of the movable iron piece. The magnetic energy stored is:

$$W = \dfrac{1}{2} L(\theta) I^2$$

**Step 2: Apply Co-Energy Principle (Work-Energy Theorem)**
The mechanical work done by the magnetic system as the iron moves by $d\theta$ equals the change in stored magnetic energy. Therefore, the deflecting torque is:

$$T_d = \dfrac{\partial W}{\partial \theta} \bigg\vert_{I=\text{const}} = \dfrac{1}{2} I^2 \dfrac{dL}{d\theta}$$

**Step 3: Repulsion Type — Modification**
For repulsion type, two vanes are magnetized, both moving in $\theta$. The effective change in inductance is the difference:

$$T_d = \dfrac{1}{2} I^2 \left( \dfrac{dL_1}{d\theta} - \dfrac{dL_2}{d\theta} \right)$$

**Step 4: Equilibrium Condition**

$$T_d = K\theta \implies \theta = \dfrac{1}{2K} I^2 \dfrac{dL}{d\theta}$$

**Key Conclusion:** Since $\theta \propto I^2$, the scale is **square-law** (crowded at the low end, spread out at the high end). For AC, $I$ is RMS, so the instrument reads **RMS values directly**.

### 3.3 Derivation: Damping Torque for Eddy Current Damping

**Given:** A conducting disc of radius $r$, thickness $t$, conductivity $\sigma$, rotating with angular velocity $\omega = d\theta/dt$ in a uniform magnetic field $B$ over a small pole-face area $A_p$.

**Step 1: EMF Induced in a Radial Strip**
Consider a small radial strip at radius $x$ from the center. The linear velocity of the strip is $v = x\omega$. The EMF induced per unit length (in the strip) is $B v = Bx\omega$.

For a strip of length $dx$ moving perpendicular to $B$ over a path $r$:

$$e_{strip} = Bx\omega \cdot dx$$

**Step 2: Induced Eddy Current**
The resistance of the strip depends on the active length in the field. Assuming the path length $l_p$ is in the field region:

$$i_{eddy} = \dfrac{Bx\omega \cdot dx}{\rho \cdot \dfrac{l_p}{t \cdot dx}} = \dfrac{Bx\omega \cdot t \cdot dx^2}{\rho \cdot l_p}$$

Where $\rho$ is the resistivity of the disc material.

**Step 3: Force on the Strip**
The force on the current-carrying strip in the magnetic field:

$$dF = B \cdot i_{eddy} \cdot l_p = B \cdot \dfrac{Bx\omega \cdot t \cdot dx^2}{\rho \cdot l_p} \cdot l_p$$

$$dF = \dfrac{B^2 x \omega t}{\rho} dx^2$$

**Step 4: Damping Force by Integration**
Integrating from $0$ to $r$ (the disc radius):

$$F_{damping} = \int_0^r \dfrac{B^2 t \omega}{\rho} x^2 \cdot dx \cdot (\text{path correction factor})$$

After full integration over both radial and circumferential paths in the active pole area, the final damping torque takes the form:

$$\boxed{T_d = k_e B^2 \omega = k_e B^2 \dfrac{d\theta}{dt}}$$

Where $k_e$ is a constant depending on disc geometry, conductivity, and pole area.

**Engineering Implication:** To increase damping, we can increase $B$ (use stronger magnets) or use a disc of higher conductivity (e.g., copper instead of aluminium — but copper is heavier, so aluminium is preferred for inertia-sensitive meters).

### 3.4 Derivation: Shunt Resistance for Ammeter Range Extension

**Given:** A basic meter movement with $R_m = 100 \;\Omega$ and $I_m = 1 \text{ mA}$ (full-scale). Required range: $I = 1 \text{ A}$.

**Step 1: Apply KCL at the Junction**
The total current $I$ splits into $I_m$ through the meter and $I_s$ through the shunt:

$$I = I_m + I_s \implies I_s = I - I_m$$

**Step 2: Apply KVL (Equal Voltage Across Parallel Combination)**
Since the meter and shunt are in parallel, the voltage across them is identical:

$$V_m = V_s \implies I_m R_m = I_s R_s$$

**Step 3: Solve for $R_s$**

$$R_s = \dfrac{I_m R_m}{I_s} = \dfrac{I_m R_m}{I - I_m}$$

**Step 4: Introduce Multiplying Factor $m$**

$$m = \dfrac{I}{I_m} \implies I = m I_m \implies I - I_m = (m-1) I_m$$

$$R_s = \dfrac{I_m R_m}{(m-1) I_m} = \boxed{\dfrac{R_m}{m-1}}$$

**Step 5: Numerical Verification**
For $I_m = 1 \text{ mA}$, $R_m = 100 \;\Omega$, $I = 1 \text{ A} = 1000 \text{ mA}$:

$$m = \dfrac{1000}{1} = 1000$$

$$R_s = \dfrac{100}{999} \approx 0.1001 \;\Omega$$

**Sanity Check:** $R_s$ is very small (mΩ range for high-range ammeters) — consistent with physics, since most of the high current must bypass the delicate meter.

### 3.5 Derivation: Multiplier Resistance for Voltmeter Range Extension

**Given:** Same basic meter with $R_m = 100 \;\Omega$ and $I_m = 1 \text{ mA}$. Required voltage range: $V = 10 \text{ V}$.

**Step 1: Full-Scale Voltage of the Bare Meter**

$$V_m = I_m R_m = (1 \text{ mA})(100 \;\Omega) = 100 \text{ mV}$$

**Step 2: Apply KVL — Series Connection**
The multiplier $R_s$ and meter $R_m$ are in series, so the same current $I_m$ flows through both. Total voltage:

$$V = I_m (R_m + R_s)$$

**Step 3: Solve for $R_s$**

$$R_s = \dfrac{V}{I_m} - R_m = \dfrac{V - V_m}{I_m}$$

**Step 4: Introduce Multiplying Factor $n$**

$$n = \dfrac{V}{V_m} \implies V = n V_m = n I_m R_m$$

$$R_s = n I_m R_m / I_m - R_m = R_m (n - 1)$$

$$\boxed{R_s = R_m(n-1)}$$

**Step 5: Numerical Verification**
For $V = 10 \text{ V}$, $V_m = 0.1 \text{ V}$:

$$n = \dfrac{10}{0.1} = 100$$

$$R_s = 100 \times (100 - 1) = 9900 \;\Omega = 9.9 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Total resistance seen by source:** $R_{total} = R_m + R_s = 100 + 9900 = 10{,}000 \;\Omega = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$.

**Sanity Check:** $R_s$ is much larger than $R_m$ for high-range voltmeters — consistent with physics, since the multiplier must "absorb" most of the voltage to protect the meter.

### 3.6 Worked-Out KTU-Style Numerical Examples

#### **Problem 1: PMMC Full-Scale Current Calculation**

A PMMC instrument has coil dimensions $l = 12 \text{ mm}$ and $b = 10 \text{ mm}$, with 100 turns. The flux density is $B = 0.15 \text{ T}$, and the spring constant is $K = 2 \times 10^{-6} \text{ N·m/rad}$. Find the current required for full-scale deflection of 90°.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute coil area:

$$A = l \times b = 0.012 \times 0.010 = 1.2 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2:** Use $\theta = \dfrac{NBIA}{K}$ and solve for $I$:

$$I = \dfrac{K\theta}{NBA}$$

**Step 3:** Convert $\theta$ to radians: $\theta = 90° = \dfrac{\pi}{2} = 1.5708 \text{ rad}$.

**Step 4:** Substitute:

$$I = \dfrac{(2 \times 10^{-6})(1.5708)}{(100)(0.15)(1.2 \times 10^{-4})}$$

$$I = \dfrac{3.1416 \times 10^{-6}}{1.8 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$I = 1.745 \times 10^{-3} \text{ A} \approx 1.75 \text{ mA}$$

#### **Problem 2: Multi-Range Ammeter Design**

A meter movement with $I_m = 50 \;\mu\text{A}$ and $R_m = 800 \;\Omega$ is to be used as a multi-range ammeter with ranges $1 \text{ A}$, $5 \text{ A}$, and $10 \text{ A}$. Find the shunt resistances using the **Ayrton (Universal) Shunt** method.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute the **universal shunt** (a single resistor with multiple taps). The basic equation is:

$$R_{sh} = \dfrac{R_m + R_{sh}}{(m+1)} \quad \text{... universal shunt formula}$$

For a 3-range instrument, the universal shunt resistance total is calculated first.

**Step 2:** For the highest range ($I = 10 \text{ A}$):

$$m_1 = \dfrac{10}{50 \times 10^{-6}} = 200{,}000$$

$$R_{sh(\text{total})} = \dfrac{R_m}{m_1 - 1} = \dfrac{800}{199{,}999} \approx 4 \text{ m}\Omega$$

**Step 3:** For $I = 5 \text{ A}$: $m_2 = 100{,}000$

$$R_{sh(\text{from tap to A2})} = \dfrac{R_m + R_{sh(total)}}{m_2 + 1} \cdot \text{(rearranging)}$$

This gives individual section resistances for the Ayrton shunt — students are expected to apply KCL/KVL at each tap node.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Pattern:** For a 14-mark Part B question, expect 1 numerical problem on **shunt design** (5–7 marks) and 1 on **multiplier design** (5–7 marks). Always show units and intermediate KCL/KVL steps.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — PMMC Instrument

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input DC Current I] --> B[Current Carrying Coil N turns]
    B --> C[Permanent Magnet produces B]
    C --> D[Deflecting Torque Td equals NBIA]
    D --> E[Spindle rotates by angle theta]
    E --> F[Control Spring exerts K theta]
    F --> G{Equilibrium Td equals Tc}
    G -->|Yes| H[Pointer indicates value]
    G -->|No| E
    E --> I[Aluminium Former cuts B field]
    I --> J[Eddy Currents Induced]
    J --> K[Damping Torque opposes motion]
    K --> E
```

### 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — Moving Iron Instrument (Repulsion Type)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Current I AC or DC] --> B[Fixed Coil Magnetizes Iron Vanes]
    B --> C[Fixed Vane and Movable Vane both magnetized]
    C --> D[Repulsion Force between vanes]
    D --> E[Deflecting Torque Td equals 0.5 I squared dL by dtheta]
    E --> F[Spindle rotates by angle theta]
    F --> G[Control Spring exerts K theta]
    G --> H{Equilibrium Td equals Tc}
    H -->|Yes| I[Pointer indicates value]
    H -->|No| F
    F --> J[Air Dashpot Piston resists motion]
    J --> K[Air Friction Damping Torque]
    K --> F
```

### 4.3 Range Extension — Shunt and Multiplier Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph AMMETER["Ammeter Range Extension using Shunt"]
        A1[Source Current I] --> A2{Junction Node}
        A2 -->|Current I minus Im| A3[Shunt Resistor Rs in parallel]
        A2 -->|Current Im| A4[Meter Movement Rm in parallel]
        A3 --> A5[Common Return Node]
        A4 --> A5
    end

    subgraph VOLTMETER["Voltmeter Range Extension using Multiplier"]
        V1[Source Voltage V positive terminal] --> V2[Multiplier Resistor Rs in series]
        V2 --> V3[Meter Movement Rm in series]
        V3 --> V4[Source Voltage V negative terminal]
    end

    noteA[Key Formula Rs equals Rm divided by m minus 1] -.-> AMMETER
    noteV[Key Formula Rs equals Rm times n minus 1] -.-> VOLTMETER
```

### 4.4 Damping Mechanism Selection — Decision Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Instrument Requires Damping] --> Q1{Operating Principle?}
    Q1 -->|PMMC Galvanometer| Q2{Moving System Mass?}
    Q1 -->|Moving Iron Panel Meter| MI[Air Dashpot Piston in Chamber]
    Q1 -->|Wattmeter Induction Type| IND[Eddy Current Disc in Magnetic Field]

    Q2 -->|Light Coil| EDDY[Eddy Current Damping using Aluminium Former]
    Q2 -->|Heavy Moving Iron| AIR[Air Friction Damping using Vane in Cavity]

    MI --> UseCase1[Used for Robust Industrial Switchboard Meters]
    EDDY --> UseCase2[Used for Sensitive Lab Galvanometers and D Arsonval Meters]
    IND --> UseCase3[Used in Energy Meters and Power Meters]
    AIR --> UseCase4[Used in Ruggedised Industrial Analog Meters]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define damping. Differentiate between air friction damping and eddy current damping in indicating instruments.*

**Model Answer:**

**Definition of Damping:**
Damping is the process of dissipating the kinetic energy of the moving system of a measuring instrument to bring it to rest at its equilibrium (true deflected) position quickly and smoothly, without prolonged oscillations.

**Differences (Tabular Form for Examiner-Friendly Marking):**

| **Aspect** | **Air Friction Damping** | **Eddy Current Damping** |
|---|---|---|
| Mechanism | Viscous drag of air on a piston/vane | Induced eddy currents in a conductor disc |
| Energy Dissipated As | Heat (friction of air) | Heat ($I^2R$ losses in eddy current path) |
| Construction | Piston + air chamber | Conducting disc + permanent magnet |
| Dependence on $B$ | Independent of magnetic field | Strongly dependent on $B$ |
| Used In | MI instruments, some wattmeters | PMMC, energy meters, speedometers |
| Effect of External Field | None | Can affect damping if field varies |

**(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for differentiation table)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Why is the scale of a moving iron instrument non-uniform while that of a PMMC instrument is uniform? Justify mathematically.*

**Model Answer:**

**PMMC Instrument:**
The deflecting torque is $T_d = NBIA$, and at equilibrium $T_d = K\theta$. Therefore:

$$\theta = \dfrac{NBIA}{K} \implies \theta \propto I$$

Deflection is **directly proportional to current** → **uniform (linear) scale**.

**Moving Iron Instrument:**
The deflecting torque is $T_d = \dfrac{1}{2} I^2 \dfrac{dL}{d\theta}$. At equilibrium:

$$\theta = \dfrac{1}{2K} I^2 \dfrac{dL}{d\theta} \implies \theta \propto I^2$$

Deflection is **proportional to the square of the current** → **square-law scale**, which is inherently non-uniform (compressed at low currents, expanded at high currents).

**(3 marks: 1 for PMMC derivation + 1 for MI derivation + 1 for conclusion)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice Format)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Damping and PMMC Focus*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**(a)** Explain with neat block diagram, the construction and working of a **Permanent Magnet Moving Coil (PMMC) instrument**. Derive the equation for deflecting torque. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A PMMC instrument has a coil of 100 turns, dimensions $15 \text{ mm} \times 12 \text{ mm}$, suspended in a uniform radial magnetic field of $0.2 \text{ T}$. The control spring constant is $2.5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ N·m/rad}$. Calculate:
(i) The current required for full-scale deflection of 100°.
(ii) The deflecting torque at this current. *(7 marks)*

---

### **Model Solution to Question A:**

**Part (a) — Construction, Working, and Derivation (7 marks):**

**Construction (3 marks):**
A PMMC instrument consists of:
1. **Permanent Magnet:** A high-grade Alnico or rare-earth magnet producing a strong, uniform radial field $B$ across the air gap.
2. **Moving Coil:** A rectangular coil of $N$ turns of fine copper wire, wound on a light aluminium former, pivoted in jewel bearings.
3. **Pole Pieces:** Soft iron pole pieces shaped to provide a radial field, ensuring the coil sides always move perpendicular to $B$.
4. **Control System:** Phosphor-bronze spiral springs that provide the restoring torque.
5. **Pointer and Scale:** A lightweight pointer attached to the spindle, moving over a uniformly graduated scale.
6. **Damping System:** The aluminium former itself, by virtue of eddy currents induced in it during motion, provides damping.

**Working (2 marks):**
When current $I$ flows through the coil, a force $F = BIl$ acts on each vertical side. This produces a deflecting torque $T_d = NBIA$ that rotates the coil. The control spring provides an opposing torque $K\theta$. The pointer comes to rest when $T_d = T_c$.

**Derivation (2 marks):**
As derived in Section 3.1:

$$T_d = NBIA = K\theta \implies \theta = \dfrac{NBIA}{K}$$

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $N = 100$ turns
- $l = 15 \text{ mm} = 0.015 \text{ m}$
- $b = 12 \text{ mm} = 0.012 \text{ m}$
- $B = 0.2 \text{ T}$
- $K = 2.5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ N·m/rad}$
- $\theta_{FS} = 100°$

**Step (i): Compute Current for Full-Scale Deflection (4 marks)**

**Step 1: Convert $\theta$ to radians.**

$$\theta = 100° \times \dfrac{\pi}{180°} = \dfrac{100\pi}{180} = \dfrac{5\pi}{9} \approx 1.7453 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 2: Compute coil area.**

$$A = l \times b = 0.015 \times 0.012 = 1.8 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3: Apply $\theta = \dfrac{NBIA}{K}$ and solve for $I$.**

$$I = \dfrac{K\theta}{NBA}$$

**Step 4: Substitute values.**

$$I = \dfrac{(2.5 \times 10^{-6})(1.7453)}{(100)(0.2)(1.8 \times 10^{-4})}$$

$$I = \dfrac{4.363 \times 10^{-6}}{3.6 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$\boxed{I_{FS} = 1.212 \times 10^{-3} \text{ A} \approx 1.21 \text{ mA}}$$

**Step (ii): Compute Deflecting Torque (3 marks)**

$$T_d = NBIA = (100)(0.2)(1.212 \times 10^{-3})(1.8 \times 10^{-4})$$

$$\boxed{T_d = 4.363 \times 10^{-6} \text{ N·m} \approx 4.36 \;\mu\text{N·m}}$$

**Cross-check:** $T_d = K\theta = (2.5 \times 10^{-6})(1.7453) = 4.36 \times 10^{-6}$ N·m ✓

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Moving Iron and Range Extension Focus*

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]**

**(a)** Explain the **principle of operation of Moving Iron (MI) instruments** with two types of construction. Derive the expression for deflecting torque. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A basic PMMC meter has internal resistance $R_m = 500 \;\Omega$ and full-scale current $I_m = 100 \;\mu\text{A}$. Design:
(i) A **DC voltmeter** to read up to $0–50 \text{ V}$.
(ii) A **DC ammeter** to read up to $0–10 \text{ A}$.

Calculate the required multiplier and shunt resistance in each case. *(7 marks)*

---

### **Model Solution to Question B:**

**Part (a) — MI Instrument Principle and Derivation (7 marks):**

**Principle (2 marks):**
A moving iron instrument operates on the principle that a soft iron piece, when placed inside a current-carrying coil, gets magnetized. The force of attraction or repulsion between the magnetized iron pieces produces a deflecting torque proportional to the square of the current, enabling measurement of both AC (RMS) and DC.

**Two Types of Construction (3 marks):**

1. **Attraction Type (or Plain Type):**
   - A single soft-iron vane (movable) is mounted on the spindle inside a coil.
   - When current flows, the coil's magnetic field magnetizes the vane, which is **attracted toward the stronger field** region (toward the coil's center).
   - This causes spindle rotation and pointer deflection.

2. **Repulsion Type (or Double-Iron Type):**
   - Two iron vanes are placed inside the coil: one fixed to the coil frame, the other attached to the spindle.
   - When current flows, **both vanes are magnetized in the same direction** (e.g., both become North poles on the same side).
   - Like poles repel → movable vane is pushed away from the fixed vane → spindle rotates.

**Derivation (2 marks):**
As derived in Section 3.2:

$$T_d = \dfrac{1}{2} I^2 \dfrac{dL}{d\theta} = K\theta$$

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Design (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $R_m = 500 \;\Omega$
- $I_m = 100 \;\mu\text{A} = 100 \times 10^{-6} \text{ A}$

---

**Part (i): DC Voltmeter Design — Range 0–50 V (3.5 marks)**

**Step 1: Compute Full-Scale Voltage of Bare Meter.**

$$V_m = I_m R_m = (100 \times 10^{-6})(500) = 0.05 \text{ V} = 50 \text{ mV}$$

**Step 2: Compute Multiplying Factor $n$.**

$$n = \dfrac{V}{V_m} = \dfrac{50}{0.05} = 1000$$

**Step 3: Compute Multiplier Resistance.**

$$R_s = R_m(n-1) = 500 \times (1000 - 1)$$

$$\boxed{R_s = 500 \times 999 = 499{,}500 \;\Omega \approx 499.5 \text{ k}\Omega}$$

**Step 4: Verify Total Resistance.**

$$R_{total} = R_m + R_s = 500 + 499{,}500 = 500{,}000 \;\Omega = 500 \text{ k}\Omega$$

Check: $V = I_m \times R_{total} = (100 \times 10^{-6})(500{,}000) = 50 \text{ V}$ ✓

---

**Part (ii): DC Ammeter Design — Range 0–10 A (3.5 marks)**

**Step 1: Compute Multiplying Factor $m$.**

$$m = \dfrac{I}{I_m} = \dfrac{10}{100 \times 10^{-6}} = 100{,}000$$

**Step 2: Compute Shunt Resistance.**

$$R_s = \dfrac{R_m}{m-1} = \dfrac{500}{100{,}000 - 1} = \dfrac{500}{99{,}999}$$

$$\boxed{R_s \approx 5.0005 \times 10^{-3} \;\Omega \approx 5 \text{ m}\Omega}$$

**Step 3: Verify.**

$$I_s = I - I_m = 10 - 0.0001 \approx 10 \text{ A}$$

$$V_{shunt} = I_s R_s = (10)(5 \times 10^{-3}) = 0.05 \text{ V}$$

$$V_{meter} = I_m R_m = (100 \times 10^{-6})(500) = 0.05 \text{ V} \;\checkmark$$

The voltages are equal → KCL satisfied → design validated.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Unit Conversion Error (–2 marks typical):** Forgetting to convert **mm to m** and **degrees to radians** in PMMC numericals. Always state units in *every* intermediate step.
> 2. **Confusing Shunt and Multiplier Formulas (–3 marks typical):** Some students use $R_s = R_m(m-1)$ for ammeters (WRONG — that is the voltmeter formula). The correct ammeter formula is $R_s = \dfrac{R_m}{m-1}$. **Memory aid:** Shunt is "small resistance" → $R_s$ is in denominator; Multiplier is "big resistance" → $R_s$ is in numerator.
> 3. **Forgetting to Define $m$ and $n$ (–1 mark):** KTU expects you to explicitly state "$m = I/I_m$ is the multiplying factor" before using it in the formula.
> 4. **Not Mentioning the Use of Aluminium Former (–2 marks):** In PMMC theory questions, students often forget that the aluminium former serves as both the coil support AND the eddy-current damping disc.
> 5. **MI Instrument Deflecting Torque Mistake (–2 marks):** Writing $T_d = I^2 dL/d\theta$ instead of $T_d = \frac{1}{2} I^2 dL/d\theta$. The factor $\frac{1}{2}$ is mandatory and is checked.
> 6. **Damping Torque Direction (–1 mark):** Damping torque always **opposes** motion. In free-body diagrams, it must be drawn opposite to $d\theta/dt$.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Damping** ensures the pointer reaches its true position without prolonged oscillations. Damping torque $\propto d\theta/dt$ for all three methods.
- **Air friction damping** uses piston-in-chamber or vane-in-cavity. Independent of magnetic field. Common in MI instruments.
- **Fluid friction damping** uses oil-immersed vane. Most efficient damping but adds inertia and friction errors.
- **Eddy current damping** uses a conducting disc (often the PMMC former itself) in a permanent magnet field. Most elegant, no mechanical contact, no friction wear. Damping torque $T_d = k_e B^2 \omega$.
- **PMMC:** Linear scale ($\theta \propto I$), DC only, highest accuracy (class 0.1 to 1.0).
- **Moving Iron:** Square-law scale ($\theta \propto I^2$), AC + DC, robust, lower accuracy (class 1.0 to 2.5).
- **Deflecting torque equations:**
  - PMMC: $T_d = NBIA$
  - MI: $T_d = \dfrac{1}{2} I^2 \dfrac{dL}{d\theta}$
- **Shunt (Ammeter):** $R_s = \dfrac{R_m}{m-1}$, connected in **parallel**. Very small resistance (mΩ).
- **Multiplier (Voltmeter):** $R_s = R_m(n-1)$, connected in **series**. Very large resistance (kΩ to MΩ).
- **Universal Shunt (Ayrton Shunt):** A single resistor with multiple taps, used in multi-range ammeters to avoid errors due to switch contact resistance.
- **General Equation of Motion:** $J\dfrac{d^2\theta}{dt^2} + D\dfrac{d\theta}{dt} + K\theta = T_d$ — *must* be memorized for KTU.
- **Conversion factors:** $1° = \pi/180 \text{ rad}$; $1 \text{ mA} = 10^{-3} \text{ A}$; $1 \mu\text{A} = 10^{-6} \text{ A}$; $1 \text{ k}\Omega = 10^3 \;\Omega$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Magnetic Measurement - Flux Meter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET205 - MEASUREMENTS AND INSTRUMENTATION (ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Magnetic Measurement - Flux Meter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Flux Meter (Grassot Flux Meter):** A flux meter is a precision electromagnetic instrument used to measure the total change in magnetic flux ($\Delta \Phi$) linked with a measuring (search) coil. The Grassot Flux Meter is a *specialized* moving-coil ballistic galvanometer in which the controlling (restoring) torque is deliberately reduced to **near zero**, allowing the deflection of the moving coil to remain stationary after the transient flux change, thus acting as a direct integrating device for magnetic flux.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the flux meter is classified under **Ballistic Methods of Magnetic Measurement**, where it is the principal integrating instrument for measuring flux density (B), magnetizing field strength (H), and the B–H loop parameters of ferromagnetic materials.

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — "The See-Saw That Remembers"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine a perfectly balanced see-saw in a playground. If you give it a small push, it rotates to a new position and *stays there* — it does **not** return to its original position. The total angular movement depends only on the *total push* (impulse) and not on *how fast* you pushed. A flux meter behaves exactly like this see-saw for magnetic flux:
> - The "push" is the **impulse of induced current** (caused by changing flux).
> - The "rotation" is the **deflection of the moving coil**.
> - The "no return" behavior comes from the **negligible spring restoring torque**.

So, when flux changes rapidly, the coil moves once and then *freezes* in a new position, giving a steady reading proportional to the total flux change $\Delta \Phi$. The operator simply *reads* the deflection on a calibrated scale.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Magnitude / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Change in Flux | $\Delta \Phi$ | Weber (Wb) |
| Flux Density | B | Tesla (W/m²) or Gauss ($1 \text{ T} = 10^4 \text{ G}$) |
| Magnetizing Force | H | Ampere-turns/meter (At/m) or Oersted |
| Permeability of free space | $\mu_0$ | $4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m |
| Search coil turns | N | Number (dimensionless) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Construction of the Grassot Flux Meter

The flux meter is essentially a moving-coil instrument with **specific design modifications** tailored for flux integration:

1. **Moving Coil:** Wound with a **large number of turns** of fine copper wire wound on a non-metallic (ivory or wooden) former to minimize eddy current losses.
2. **Suspension System:** The coil is suspended by a **single filament of silk or phosphor-bronze strip with negligible torsional constant ($C \to 0$)**. The absence of control springs removes restoring torque.
3. **Damping:** Damping is purely **electromagnetic (air friction)**. The coil is wound on a light aluminum frame moving in a strong magnetic field produced by permanent magnets. No external damping resistance is used because damping must be independent of the external circuit resistance.
4. **Pointer & Scale:** A long, lightweight pointer is attached to the coil moving over a calibrated scale. Mirror-and-scale arrangements are used to avoid parallax in precise measurements.
5. **External Circuit:** A **search coil** (also called a *test coil* or *exploring coil*) of known area ($A$) and number of turns ($N$) is connected directly to the flux meter terminals through a low-resistance link.

## 2.2 Working Principle — Why a "Frozen" Deflection?

When the flux linked with the search coil changes (e.g., when the search coil is removed from a magnetic field, or when the field is switched off), an **impulse of current** flows through the flux meter coil.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Because the restoring torque is **zero**, once the impulse is delivered, there is **no torque to bring the coil back**. The coil stays at its deflected position until it is manually reset (or a reverse current is passed).

The deflection is therefore a **time-integral** of the induced EMF (a flux integrator), exactly like a ballistic galvanometer, but with $C \approx 0$, so the deflection becomes directly proportional to the change in flux.

## 2.3 Operational Steps for Measurement

- **Step 1:** Connect a calibrated search coil ($N$ turns, area $A$) to the flux meter terminals.
- **Step 2:** Place the search coil in the unknown magnetic field with its plane perpendicular to the field direction.
- **Step 3:** Read the initial position of the flux meter pointer (set to zero).
- **Step 4:** Suddenly remove the search coil from the field, **or** reverse the field, **or** rotate the coil through 90° or 180°.
- **Step 5:** Read the final steady deflection $\theta$.
- **Step 6:** Apply the calibration constant to convert $\theta$ into $\Delta \Phi$ and then compute $B = \Delta \Phi / (NA)$.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Induced EMF in search coil | $e = -N \dfrac{d\Phi}{dt}$ | Lenz's Law |
| 2 | Instantaneous current | $i = \dfrac{e}{R} = -\dfrac{N}{R} \dfrac{d\Phi}{dt}$ | R = total circuit resistance |
| 3 | Deflection (general, with spring) | $\theta = \dfrac{B_a \cdot a \cdot N \cdot \Delta \Phi}{R \cdot C}$ | $B_a$ = flux density in flux meter air gap, $C$ = spring constant |
| 4 | Deflection (Grassot flux meter, $C \to 0$) | $\theta = \dfrac{B_a \cdot a \cdot N \cdot \Delta \Phi}{R \cdot 0} \to \text{const} \cdot \Delta \Phi$ | The denominator limit indicates that $C$ must be very small; R is then the controlling parameter |
| 5 | Practical flux meter equation | $\Delta \Phi = K \cdot \theta$ | $K$ = flux-meter constant (Wb/division) |
| 6 | Magnetic flux density | $B = \dfrac{K \cdot \theta}{N \cdot A}$ | SI: Tesla |
| 7 | Magnetizing field strength | $H = \dfrac{B}{\mu_0 \mu_r}$ | For air core: $H = B/\mu_0$ |
| 8 | Calibration constant (using standard mutual inductor) | $K = \dfrac{M \cdot \Delta I}{\theta_{cal}}$ | $M$ = known mutual inductance, $\Delta I$ = known current change |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical observation for the KTU exam:** The deflection $\theta$ depends on the **total change in flux** $\Delta \Phi$ and is **independent of the time taken** for the flux change, provided the change is **rapid** (so that coil inertia is negligible during the change but damping acts only after).

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

Flux meters are indispensable in:
- Testing **permanent magnets** (residual flux, coercive force).
- Plotting **B–H curves and hysteresis loops** of transformer steel, silicon iron, and ferrites.
- **Quality control** in the manufacture of electrical machines, relays, and magnetic components.
- **Earth's field mapping**, geomagnetic surveys, and calibration of magnetometers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Flux Meter Equation

We begin from the fundamental electromagnetic induction equation and apply a force-balance on the moving coil, integrating over the duration of the flux change.

**Step 1 — Induced EMF in the search coil:**
When the flux $\Phi$ linking the search coil of $N$ turns changes, the induced EMF is given by Faraday's Law.

$$
e = -N \frac{d\Phi}{dt}
$$

The negative sign denotes Lenz's law; for magnitude considerations we drop the sign in subsequent steps.

**Step 2 — Current flowing in the closed circuit (search coil + flux meter coil):**
The total resistance of the closed circuit is $R$ (which is the algebraic sum of the search coil resistance $R_s$, the flux meter coil resistance $R_g$, and the lead resistance $R_l$).

$$
i = \frac{e}{R} = \frac{N}{R} \cdot \frac{d\Phi}{dt}
$$

**Step 3 — Deflecting torque on the moving coil of the flux meter at any instant:**
The flux meter has its own magnetic field in the air gap with flux density $B_a$, and the moving coil has an effective area $a$ (single turn of the moving coil). The instantaneous torque on the moving coil carrying current $i$ in field $B_a$ is:

$$
T_d = B_a \cdot a \cdot i
$$

**Step 4 — Equation of motion of the moving coil:**
Let the angular acceleration be $\ddot{\theta}$, angular velocity be $\dot{\theta}$, and the controlling torque coefficient be $C$. Including the damping torque $D\dot{\theta}$ (viscous damping), the equation of motion is:

$$
J \ddot{\theta} + D \dot{\theta} + C \theta = B_a \cdot a \cdot i
$$

where $J$ is the moment of inertia of the moving system.

**Step 5 — Integrate over the time of flux change:**
Integrate both sides with respect to time, from $t = 0$ (just before flux change) to $t = t_1$ (just after the flux change, when motion has effectively ceased and the pointer is at the maximum deflection).

> **Why integrate?** Because the flux changes rapidly, the term $J\ddot{\theta}$ and $D\dot{\theta}$ are non-zero **only** during the very short interval of change. After the change, $\dot{\theta} = 0$ and $\ddot{\theta} = 0$, so the right-hand side integral from $0$ to $t_1$ must equal the change in the left-hand side.

$$
\int_0^{t_1} J \ddot{\theta} \, dt + \int_0^{t_1} D \dot{\theta} \, dt + \int_0^{t_1} C \theta \, dt = \int_0^{t_1} B_a \cdot a \cdot i \, dt
$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate each term on the left side:**
- The first term equals $J(\dot{\theta}_1 - \dot{\theta}_0)$. Since velocity is zero both before ($0$) and after ($0$), this term vanishes.
- The second term: $\int_0^{t_1} D \dot{\theta} \, dt = D \int_0^{\theta_{max}} d\theta = D \theta_{max}$. The damping is **important during the overshoot and return to rest**. For an underdamped, dead-beat ballistic galvanometer, $D\theta_{max}$ reduces the deflection slightly. The Grassot flux meter is designed to be slightly underdamped such that this integral effectively cancels the impulse (this is why the *frozen* deflection is slightly less than the ideal).
- The third term: during the rapid change, $\theta$ has not yet increased appreciably, so $\int_0^{t_1} C \theta \, dt \approx 0$.

**Step 7 — Right-hand side evaluation:**

$$
\int_0^{t_1} B_a \cdot a \cdot i \, dt = B_a \cdot a \cdot \int_0^{t_1} \frac{N}{R} \frac{d\Phi}{dt} \, dt = \frac{B_a \cdot a \cdot N}{R} \cdot \Delta \Phi
$$

**Step 8 — Final relation for a ballistic galvanometer (with restoring spring):**
Substituting:

$$
D \theta = \frac{B_a \cdot a \cdot N \cdot \Delta \Phi}{R}
$$

$$
\theta = \frac{B_a \cdot a \cdot N \cdot \Delta \Phi}{D \cdot R}
$$

For a standard ballistic galvanometer, $D$ is the **damping constant** (not the spring constant) and is calibrated to include all electromagnetic damping from the external resistance $R$.

**Step 9 — Specialization to Grassot Flux Meter ($C \to 0$):**
In a flux meter, the **spring constant $C$ is essentially zero**. This means there is no controlling torque to bring the coil back. Therefore, the coil will remain at the deflected position $\theta$ after the transient, and $\theta$ is the *peak* deflection reached.

The equation simplifies to:

$$
\theta = \frac{B_a \cdot a \cdot N \cdot \Delta \Phi}{R \cdot C_{eff}} \quad \text{where } C_{eff} \to 0
$$

To make the deflection finite and measurable, the Grassot flux meter is designed such that $C$ is **very small but non-zero**, and the coil is gently returned to zero by a *reverse* current pulse from the operator (or a manual push). The equation in **operational form** is:

$$
\Delta \Phi = \frac{R \cdot C}{B_a \cdot a \cdot N} \cdot \theta = K \cdot \theta
$$

where $K = \dfrac{R \cdot C}{B_a \cdot a \cdot N}$ is the **flux-meter constant** (in Weber per division), obtained by **calibration** using a standard mutual inductor or a known solenoid.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparison Table for KTU

| Feature | Ballistic Galvanometer | Grassot Flux Meter |
|---|---|---|
| Spring constant $C$ | Significant | Almost zero |
| Restoring torque | Strong (returns to zero) | Negligible (stays deflected) |
| Reading mode | Returns to zero automatically | Must be read after damping settles |
| Damping | Electromagnetic (depends on R) | Air friction / aluminum frame |
| Deflection $\propto$ | $\Delta Q$ (charge) | $\Delta \Phi$ (flux change) |
| Reset mechanism | None needed | Manual reset (or reverse current) |
| Sensitivity | High | Very high (suitable for tiny flux changes) |
| Typical use | Capacitance, inductance, flux | Magnetic flux and B–H curves |

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A Grassot flux meter has a constant of $K = 50 \times 10^{-6}$ Wb/division. A search coil of 200 turns and cross-sectional area of $2 \text{ cm}^2$ is used. The flux meter gives a deflection of 80 divisions when the search coil is withdrawn from a magnetic field. Find the flux density.

**Step 1 — Convert the area:**

$$
A = 2 \text{ cm}^2 = 2 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the change in flux:**

$$
\Delta \Phi = K \cdot \theta = (50 \times 10^{-6}) \times 80 = 4000 \times 10^{-6} = 4 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Wb}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the flux density using $\Delta \Phi = B \cdot N \cdot A$:**

$$
B = \frac{\Delta \Phi}{N \cdot A} = \frac{4 \times 10^{-3}}{200 \times 2 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{4 \times 10^{-3}}{4 \times 10^{-2}} = 0.1 \text{ T}
$$

**Final Answer:** $B = 0.1 \text{ T} = 1 \text{ kG}$ (since 1 T = 10⁴ G).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Flux Meter Measurement Setup

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Unknown Magnetic Field Region] -->|Flux Phi threads the coil| B[Search Coil: N turns, area A]
    B -->|Induced EMF e| C[Connecting Leads]
    C --> D[Grassot Flux Meter Moving Coil]
    D -->|Deflection theta| E[Calibrated Scale]
    D --> F[Damping: Air friction on aluminium frame]
    G[Permanent Magnet provides B_a in air gap] --> D
    H[Calibration Source: Standard Mutual Inductor] -.->|Provides known dPhi| D
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Operational Flow of a Flux Meter Measurement

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: Connect search coil to flux meter] --> S2[Step 2: Place search coil in field, plane perpendicular to B]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Set pointer to zero mechanically]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Suddenly remove coil OR reverse field OR rotate 90 degrees]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Wait for pointer to settle]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Read final steady deflection theta]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: Compute Delta Phi = K times theta]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8: Compute B = Delta Phi divided by N times A]
    S8 --> S9[Step 9: Compute H from B using material properties]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Schematic — Equivalent Circuit of the Flux Meter

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SC[Search Coil: EMF source e = minus N dPhi dt] --> R1[Lead Resistance R_l]
    R1 --> R2[Search coil resistance R_s]
    R2 --> FM[Flux meter moving coil resistance R_g]
    FM --> R1
    FM --> IND[Induced current i flows and produces torque]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Visualization Insight:** The total circuit resistance $R = R_l + R_s + R_g$ is the controlling parameter of the flux meter. For accurate integration, $R$ must be kept **constant** between calibration and measurement, because $K$ depends on $R$. Always use the same leads, the same switch positions, and the same temperature conditions during calibration and actual measurement.

## 4.4 Functional Architecture of the Flux Meter Instrument

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT[Input Stage]
        I1[Search coil in magnetic field]
    end
    subgraph TRANSDUCER[Transduction Stage]
        T1[Induced EMF in search coil]
        T2[Current impulse in closed circuit]
    end
    subgraph MECHANICAL[Mechanical Integration Stage]
        M1[Moving coil experiences impulse torque]
        M2[Coil rotates by angle theta proportional to dPhi]
        M3[Pointer attached to coil indicates theta on scale]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT[Output Stage]
        O1[Steady deflection read on calibrated scale]
        O2[Converted to Delta Phi using constant K]
        O3[Converted to B and H]
    end
    I1 --> T1 --> T2 --> M1 --> M2 --> M3 --> O1 --> O2 --> O3
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**Q: Define a Grassot Flux Meter. Why is the controlling torque in a flux meter made negligibly small?**

**Model Answer:**

A **Grassot Flux Meter** is a moving-coil instrument, used for the measurement of magnetic flux, in which the controlling (restoring) torque is kept **almost zero** so that the deflection of the moving system becomes a true integral of the induced EMF, and hence directly proportional to the change of flux linked with the search coil.

The controlling torque is made negligibly small because:

1. If $C \to 0$, then the term $C \int \theta \, dt$ in the equation of motion vanishes, and the deflection becomes dependent *only* on the total charge (or total flux change), not on the instantaneous position of the coil.
2. With $C \to 0$, the coil, after deflection, has no torque to return it to zero; it **stays at the maximum deflection**, allowing the operator to read it at leisure.
3. This converts the moving-coil instrument into an **integrating device** (flux integrator), analogous to integrating a current to get charge.

### Question 2 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**Q: Write the equation for the flux meter deflection. Define the flux meter constant and explain how it is determined.**

**Model Answer:**

The fundamental flux meter equation is:

$$
\Delta \Phi = K \cdot \theta
$$

where:
- $\Delta \Phi$ = change in magnetic flux (in Wb),
- $\theta$ = steady-state deflection of the moving coil (in divisions or radians),
- $K$ = **flux meter constant** (in Wb/division), given by:

$$
K = \frac{R \cdot C}{B_a \cdot a \cdot N_{cal}}
$$

**Determination of $K$ (Calibration):**
The constant $K$ is determined experimentally using a **standard mutual inductor** of known mutual inductance $M$.

1. A known current change $\Delta I$ is sent through the primary of the standard mutual inductor.
2. The induced EMF in the secondary equals $M \cdot \Delta I$ over the brief switching interval, producing a change in flux linkage $\Delta \Phi = M \cdot \Delta I$ in the secondary.
3. This change in flux is connected to the flux meter, producing a deflection $\theta_{cal}$.
4. The constant is then:

$$
K = \frac{M \cdot \Delta I}{\theta_{cal}}
$$

The calibration must be done with the **same circuit resistance $R$** as used in actual measurements.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**Q: A Grassot flux meter is used to measure the flux density in the air gap of a magnetic circuit. With a search coil of 50 turns and area of $5 \text{ cm}^2$, a deflection of 60 divisions is obtained. The flux meter constant is $40 \times 10^{-6}$ Wb/division.**

**(a)** Derive the working equation of a Grassot flux meter, clearly stating all the assumptions made. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Compute the flux density in the air gap and the corresponding magnetizing force, assuming a non-magnetic (air) medium. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

Starting from Faraday's law, the induced EMF in the search coil is:

$$
e = -N \frac{d\Phi}{dt}
$$

The current in the closed circuit of total resistance $R$ is:

$$
i = \frac{e}{R} = \frac{N}{R} \frac{d\Phi}{dt}
$$

The instantaneous deflecting torque on the moving coil (area $a$, in flux density $B_a$) of the flux meter is:

$$
T_d = B_a \cdot a \cdot i
$$

The equation of motion of the moving system is:

$$
J \ddot{\theta} + D \dot{\theta} + C \theta = B_a \cdot a \cdot i
$$

Integrating over the short duration of the flux change (from $0$ to $t_1$), and noting that:
- $J \int_0^{t_1} \ddot{\theta} \, dt = J(\dot{\theta}_1 - \dot{\theta}_0) = 0$,
- $\int_0^{t_1} \theta \, dt \approx 0$ (since $\theta$ does not change appreciably during the rapid flux change),
- The damping term $D \int_0^{t_1} \dot{\theta} \, dt$ is absorbed into the constant,

we obtain:

$$
\theta = \frac{B_a \cdot a \cdot N \cdot \Delta \Phi}{R \cdot C_{eff}}
$$

For a Grassot flux meter, $C \to 0$, so practically:

$$
\Delta \Phi = K \cdot \theta, \quad \text{where } K = \frac{R \cdot C}{B_a \cdot a \cdot N}
$$

**Assumptions made:**
1. The flux change is **rapid** compared to the natural period of the coil.
2. The damping torque $D$ is small enough not to oppose the impulse significantly.
3. The total circuit resistance $R$ is constant.
4. The spring restoring torque is negligibly small.
5. No magnetic leakage or fringing in the search coil; flux is uniform over the search coil area.

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Step 1 — Convert area to SI units:**

$$
A = 5 \text{ cm}^2 = 5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the change in flux:**

$$
\Delta \Phi = K \cdot \theta = (40 \times 10^{-6}) \times 60 = 2400 \times 10^{-6} = 2.4 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Wb}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute flux density:**

$$
B = \frac{\Delta \Phi}{N \cdot A} = \frac{2.4 \times 10^{-3}}{50 \times 5 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{2.4 \times 10^{-3}}{2.5 \times 10^{-2}} = 0.096 \text{ T}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute magnetizing force for an air medium ($\mu_r = 1$):**

$$
H = \frac{B}{\mu_0} = \frac{0.096}{4\pi \times 10^{-7}} = \frac{0.096}{1.2566 \times 10^{-6}} = 7.64 \times 10^{4} \text{ A/m}
$$

**Final Answer:** $B = 0.096 \text{ T}$ and $H = 7.64 \times 10^{4} \text{ A/m}$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q: (a)** With a neat diagram, explain the construction and working of a Grassot Flux Meter. Discuss the role of damping in the instrument. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A flux meter has a constant of $K = 100 \times 10^{-6}$ Wb/division. It is connected to a search coil of 500 turns and area $2 \text{ cm}^2$. When the search coil is rotated through 90° in a uniform magnetic field, the flux meter gives a deflection of 50 divisions. Calculate the flux density of the field. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**Construction:** (Refer to Section 2.1 for the detailed construction description and to Section 4 for diagrams.)

The Grassot Flux Meter consists of:
1. A moving coil of fine insulated copper wire wound on a light non-metallic former.
2. The coil is suspended between the poles of a permanent magnet by a single silk thread (or a thin strip with negligible torsional control).
3. The coil moves in a strong radial magnetic field produced by a permanent magnet.
4. A long, lightweight pointer is attached to the moving coil, moving over a calibrated mirror-and-scale.
5. Damping is provided by air friction or by the eddy-current action in the aluminum frame of the coil.
6. Two terminals are provided on top for connecting the external search coil.

**Working:**
- A search coil of known area $A$ and turns $N$ is connected to the flux meter.
- The search coil is placed in the magnetic field with its plane perpendicular to the field direction.
- The search coil is suddenly removed from the field (or rotated through 90°). This produces a change in flux linkage $\Delta \Phi = N \cdot B \cdot A$.
- The induced EMF causes an impulse of current through the flux meter coil.
- The coil receives an impulse torque, and since the restoring torque is near zero, the coil moves to a new steady position proportional to $\Delta \Phi$.
- The pointer indicates the deflection $\theta$, and the flux density is computed as $B = K\theta/(NA)$.

**Role of damping:**
- Damping must be **critical (or slightly under-damped)** to ensure that the pointer comes to rest quickly *after* the impulse, without oscillating excessively.
- Damping is provided by **air friction or aluminum-frame eddy currents**, and it must be **independent of the external circuit resistance** so that the calibration constant does not change with different search coils.
- If damping is too high (overdamped), the pointer moves slowly and may not reach the true maximum before being braked.
- If damping is too low (underdamped), the pointer oscillates, and although the *first maximum* is the correct value, reading is difficult.

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Step 1 — Compute the change in flux from the flux meter constant:**

$$
\Delta \Phi = K \cdot \theta = (100 \times 10^{-6}) \times 50 = 5000 \times 10^{-6} = 5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Wb}
$$

**Step 2 — Relate $\Delta \Phi$ to the rotation:**
When a search coil of area $A$ and $N$ turns, initially perpendicular to $B$ (full flux linkage), is rotated through 90°, the flux linkage changes from $NBA$ to $0$. Therefore:

$$
\Delta \Phi_{linkage} = N \cdot B \cdot A
$$

(Note: $\Delta \Phi$ here refers to the *flux linkage change* experienced by the search coil, which equals $N \cdot B \cdot A$. The flux meter constant $K$ is defined such that the deflection is proportional to this linkage change, so $\Delta \Phi_{linkage} = N \cdot B \cdot A = K \cdot \theta$.)

**Step 3 — Compute the flux density $B$:**

$$
B = \frac{K \cdot \theta}{N \cdot A} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{500 \times 2 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{0.1} = 0.05 \text{ T}
$$

**Final Answer:** $B = 0.05 \text{ T} = 500 \text{ Gauss}$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (KTU Examiner's Perspective):**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor $N$:** Many students compute $B = K \theta / A$ and forget that $\Delta \Phi$ in the flux meter equation is the *flux linkage change* ($NBA$), not the bare flux change. **Always divide by $N \cdot A$ when computing $B$.**
> 2. **Unit conversion of area:** Search coil area is often given in $\text{cm}^2$ in KTU problems. Converting to $\text{m}^2$ (multiply by $10^{-4}$) is mandatory. A missing factor of $10^{-4}$ is the most common error.
> 3. **Rotation through 90° vs 180°:** When a coil is rotated through 90° starting from a position perpendicular to $B$, the flux linkage change is $NBA$. When rotated through 180°, the change is $2NBA$. Read the problem statement carefully.
> 4. **Sign convention for Lenz's law:** Always use magnitudes in numerical problems; the sign in the derivation is for understanding only.
> 5. **Calibration resistance mismatch:** The flux meter constant $K$ is valid only for the circuit resistance $R$ at which it was calibrated. Changing leads or search coils alters $R$, and hence $K$. Always re-calibrate if $R$ changes.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (High-Yield for KTU):**
> - A **Flux Meter** is a moving-coil ballistic galvanometer with **near-zero restoring torque**, used to measure **change in magnetic flux**.
> - Working equation: $\Delta \Phi = K \theta$, where $K$ is the **flux meter constant** (Wb/division).
> - Magnetic flux density: $B = K \theta / (N A)$.
> - For air gap: $H = B / \mu_0 = B / (4\pi \times 10^{-7}) \text{ A/m}$.
> - Damping is **air friction or aluminum-frame eddy current**; it is **independent of external resistance**.
> - Calibration is done with a **standard mutual inductor** using $\Delta \Phi = M \cdot \Delta I$.
> - Deflection depends on **total flux change** $\Delta \Phi$, not on the rate of change.
> - Search coil plane must be **perpendicular to $B$** for initial maximum linkage.
> - Rotation through 90° gives flux linkage change $NBA$; rotation through 180° gives $2NBA$.
> - **Grassot flux meter** is preferred over ballistic galvanometer for magnetic measurements because it gives a steady, readable deflection.
> - Practical formula (general ballistic galvanometer with spring): $\theta = \dfrac{B_a a N \Delta \Phi}{R C}$, and the Grassot limit corresponds to $C \to 0$.
> - The flux meter is an **integrating instrument** — it integrates the induced EMF to give the flux change.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
