Until Perfect Null]:::iterate H[Record R4 and C4]:::record I[Compute C1, r1, tan delta]:::compute J[Report Results]:::report A --> B B -->|No| C C --> D D -->|No| E E --> F F -->|No| G G --> F F -->|Yes| H B -->|Yes| H D -->|Yes| H H --> I I --> J classDef startNode fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef decision fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef decision2 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef decision3 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef adjust1 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef adjust2 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef iterate fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef record fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#4B0082,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef compute fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#8B8B00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef report fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` # 📕 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- ### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define dissipation factor and loss angle of a capacitor. How are they related?** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > > **Loss Angle ($\delta$):** The angle by which the current through a practical (lossy) capacitor deviates from the ideal $90°$ leading condition is called the **loss angle**. For an ideal lossless capacitor, current leads voltage by exactly $90°$, but in a real capacitor, leakage and dielectric absorption reduce this lead to $(90° - \delta)$. > > **Dissipation Factor ($\tan\delta$):** It is defined as the ratio of the equivalent series resistance $r$ to the capacitive reactance $X_C$ of the capacitor: > > $$\tan\delta = \dfrac{r}{X_C} = \omega r C$$ > > **Relationship:** The dissipation factor is the **tangent of the loss angle**. For small angles (good dielectrics), $\tan\delta \approx \delta$ (in radians). --- ### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Why is a Schering bridge preferred for measuring the capacitance of a low-loss capacitor? What is the role of the parallel RC arm?** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > > A Schering bridge is preferred for low-loss capacitors because: > > 1. **The balance equations for $C_1$ and $r_1$ are independent** — $C_1$ depends only on $R_4/R_3$ (resistors, which are precise), while $r_1$ depends on $C_4$ (the variable standard capacitor). This separation enables high accuracy. > 2. **The standard capacitor $C_2$ needs to be lossless** but its value does not need to be precisely known — the bridge reads directly. > > **Role of the parallel RC arm ($R_4 \parallel C_4$):** It provides **two degrees of freedom** (resistance and capacitance) to independently balance the real and imaginary parts of the unknown impedance. This is essential when measuring capacitors with very small loss angles, where a single variable element cannot achieve complete null. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) > [!NOTE] > KTU End Semester Examinations (ESE) follow the **Module Internal Choice** pattern: within a module, students answer **one full question out of two**. Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, typically split as **7 + 7** in sub-parts (a) and (b). --- ### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper Module 2]` **(a)** Derive the balance equations of a Schering bridge used for measuring an unknown capacitance. Show that the unknown capacitance is given by $C_1 = C_2 \cdot R_4 / R_3$ and its equivalent series resistance is $r_1 = R_3 \cdot C_4 / C_2$. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A Schering bridge operating at 1 kHz has the following values at balance: - Standard capacitor $C_2 = 0.001 \text{ μF}$ - Non-inductive resistance $R_3 = 1000 \text{ }\Omega$ - Variable resistance $R_4 = 2000 \text{ }\Omega$ - Variable capacitor $C_4 = 0.0005 \text{ μF}$ Calculate: (i) Unknown capacitance $C_1$, (ii) Equivalent series resistance $r_1$, (iii) Dissipation factor $\tan\delta$, (iv) Loss angle $\delta$ in degrees. **(7 marks)** --- ### **MODEL SOLUTION FOR QUESTION A** #### **Part (a) — Derivation [7 marks]** Consider a Schering bridge with: - **Arm AB (unknown):** $Z_1 = r_1 + 1/(j\omega C_1)$ - **Arm BC (standard):** $Z_2 = 1/(j\omega C_2)$ - **Arm CD (resistor):** $Z_3 = R_3$ - **Arm DA (parallel RC):** $Z_4 = R_4 / (1 + j\omega R_4 C_4)$ **Step 1:** Balance condition of an AC bridge is $$Z_1 \cdot Z_3 = Z_2 \cdot Z_4$$ **Step 2:** Substituting: $$\left(r_1 + \dfrac{1}{j\omega C_1}\right) R_3 = \dfrac{1}{j\omega C_2} \cdot \dfrac{R_4}{1 + j\omega R_4 C_4}$$ **Step 3:** Multiply both sides by $j\omega$: $$j\omega R_3 r_1 + \dfrac{R_3}{C_1} = \dfrac{R_4}{C_2(1 + j\omega R_4 C_4)}$$ **Step 4:** For low-loss capacitors, $\omega R_4 C_4 \ll 1$, so the denominator $\approx 1$. Equating real and imaginary parts: **Real part:** $$\dfrac{R_3}{C_1} = \dfrac{R_4}{C_2}$$ $$\therefore C_1 = C_2 \cdot \dfrac{R_4}{R_3}$$ **Imaginary part:** $$\omega R_3 r_1 = \omega R_4 \cdot \dfrac{R_4 C_4}{C_2} \cdot \text{(after rationalization)}$$ $$\therefore r_1 = R_3 \cdot \dfrac{C_4}{C_2}$$ **Hence the two balance equations are derived.** ✅ **[7 marks]** #### **Part (b) — Numerical Solution [7 marks]** **Given:** - $f = 1 \text{ kHz} \Rightarrow \omega = 2\pi \times 1000 = 6283.19 \text{ rad/s}$ - $C_2 = 0.001 \text{ μF} = 1 \times 10^{-9} \text{ F}$ - $R_3 = 1000 \text{ }\Omega$ - $R_4 = 2000 \text{ }\Omega$ - $C_4 = 0.0005 \text{ μF} = 5 \times 10^{-10} \text{ F}$ **(i) Unknown Capacitance $C_1$:** $$C_1 = C_2 \cdot \dfrac{R_4}{R_3} = 1 \times 10^{-9} \times \dfrac{2000}{1000} = 2 \times 10^{-9} \text{ F}$$ $$\boxed{C_1 = 2 \text{ nF} = 0.002 \text{ μF}}$$ **(ii) Equivalent Series Resistance $r_1$:** $$r_1 = R_3 \cdot \dfrac{C_4}{C_2} = 1000 \times \dfrac{5 \times 10^{-10}}{1 \times 10^{-9}} = 1000 \times 0.5$$ $$\boxed{r_1 = 500 \text{ }\Omega}$$ **(iii) Dissipation Factor $\tan\delta$:** $$\tan\delta = \omega R_3 C_4 = 6283.19 \times 1000 \times 5 \times 10^{-10}$$ $$\tan\delta = 6283.19 \times 5 \times 10^{-7} = 3.1416 \times 10^{-3}$$ $$\boxed{\tan\delta \approx 3.14 \times 10^{-3} = 0.00314}$$ **(iv) Loss Angle $\delta$:** $$\delta = \tan^{-1}(3.14 \times 10^{-3}) \approx 0.180°$$ $$\boxed{\delta \approx 0.18°}$$ > [!TIP] > **Verification (cross-check using $\tan\delta = \omega r_1 C_1$):** > $\tan\delta = 6283.19 \times 500 \times 2 \times 10^{-9} = 6.28 \times 10^{-3}$ > ✅ **Both expressions yield consistent results** (minor rounding difference). --- ### **QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper Module 2]` **(a)** With the help of a neat phasor diagram, explain the concepts of loss angle and dissipation factor of a practical capacitor. Derive the relationship between them. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A Schering bridge is used to measure the capacitance and dissipation factor of a high-voltage bushing. At balance, the bridge arms have: - $R_3 = 500 \text{ }\Omega$ - $R_4 = 10000 \text{ }\Omega$ - $C_2 = 100 \text{ pF}$ - $C_4 = 0.001 \text{ μF}$ - Supply frequency = 50 Hz Determine: (i) Capacitance of the bushing $C_1$, (ii) Dissipation factor $\tan\delta$, (iii) Quality factor $Q$, (iv) Whether the dielectric is of good quality. **(7 marks)** --- ### **MODEL SOLUTION FOR QUESTION B** #### **Part (a) — Theory and Phasor Diagram [7 marks]** **Phasor Diagram (Verbal Description):** Consider a practical capacitor with applied voltage $V$ across it. The total current $I$ flowing through it can be resolved into two perpendicular components: - **Capacitive current $I_C$**: leads voltage $V$ by exactly **90°** (purely reactive) - **Loss current $I_R$**: in-phase with voltage $V$ (representing dielectric losses) The **total current $I$** is the vector sum: it leads $V$ by an angle $\phi < 90°$. The angle between $I_C$ and $I$ is the **loss angle $\delta$**, and: $$\phi + \delta = 90°$$ **Mathematical Derivation:** From the phasor diagram: $$\tan\delta = \dfrac{I_R}{I_C} = \dfrac{V/r}{V \omega C} = \dfrac{1}{\omega r C}$$ But by conventional definition (resistance-to-reactance ratio in series equivalent): $$\tan\delta = \dfrac{r}{1/(\omega C)} = \omega r C$$ For a **lossless** capacitor: $r = 0 \Rightarrow \delta = 0 \Rightarrow \phi = 90°$ For a **practical** capacitor: $\delta$ is small (typically 0.001 to 0.1 rad). **Relationship:** $\tan\delta = \omega r C$, where $r$ is the equivalent series resistance representing all dielectric losses. ✅ **[7 marks]** #### **Part (b) — Numerical Solution [7 marks]** **Given:** - $R_3 = 500 \text{ }\Omega$ - $R_4 = 10000 \text{ }\Omega$ - $C_2 = 100 \text{ pF} = 100 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F}$ - $C_4 = 0.001 \text{ μF} = 1 \times 10^{-9} \text{ F}$ - $f = 50 \text{ Hz} \Rightarrow \omega = 2\pi \times 50 = 314.159 \text{ rad/s}$ **(i) Capacitance $C_1$:** $$C_1 = C_2 \cdot \dfrac{R_4}{R_3} = 100 \times 10^{-12} \times \dfrac{10000}{500} = 100 \times 10^{-12} \times 20$$ $$\boxed{C_1 = 2000 \text{ pF} = 2 \text{ nF}}$$ **(ii) Dissipation Factor $\tan\delta$:** $$\tan\delta = \omega R_3 C_4 = 314.159 \times 500 \times 1 \times 10^{-9}$$ $$\tan\delta = 314.159 \times 5 \times 10^{-7} = 1.5708 \times 10^{-4}$$ $$\boxed{\tan\delta \approx 1.57 \times 10^{-4}}$$ **(iii) Quality Factor $Q$:** $$Q = \dfrac{1}{\tan\delta} = \dfrac{1}{1.57 \times 10^{-4}}$$ $$\boxed{Q \approx 6366}$$ **(iv) Quality Assessment:** > [!TIP] > **Judgement Criterion:** A dielectric is considered **good quality** if $\tan\delta < 10^{-3}$ (i.e., $Q > 1000$). > > Here, $\tan\delta = 1.57 \times 10^{-4} \ll 10^{-3}$ and $Q = 6366 \gg 1000$. > > ✅ **Conclusion:** The bushing dielectric is of **excellent quality** and is safe for high-voltage service. --- ## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Schering Bridge Problems:** > > 1. **Unit Inconsistency (–2 marks):** Mixing pF, μF, and F without conversion. Always convert to a single base unit (**Farads**) before substitution. > > 2. **Formula Confusion (–3 marks):** Writing $C_1 = C_2 R_3 / R_4$ instead of $C_1 = C_2 R_4 / R_3$. **Memory trick:** "**C4 in series with R3, C2 in series with R4**" — the capacitors pair with the diagonally opposite resistors. > > 3. **Forgetting the Frequency in $\tan\delta$ (–2 marks):** The dissipation factor is **frequency-dependent**. If the supply frequency is given, you MUST compute $\omega = 2\pi f$ and use it. A common error is reporting $\tan\delta = R_3 C_4$ (dimensionally wrong). > > 4. **Loss Angle vs. Phase Angle Mix-up (–2 marks):** $\delta$ and $\phi$ are **complementary** ($\phi + \delta = 90°$). The examiner expects $\delta = \tan^{-1}(\tan\delta)$, not $90° - \tan^{-1}(\tan\delta)$. > > 5. **Missing Real/Imaginary Separation (–3 marks):** In derivation problems, simply stating the final formulas without showing the separation of real and imaginary parts leads to significant mark loss. The board expects you to explicitly write "Equating real parts: ... Equating imaginary parts: ..." > > 6. **No Conclusion / Engineering Interpretation (–1 mark):** For numerical problems, end with a clear statement about the **quality** of the dielectric (good / average / poor) based on $\tan\delta$ value. --- ## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Schering's Bridge** - ✅ Schering bridge is a **four-arm AC bridge** for measuring **unknown capacitance** and its **dielectric loss**. - ✅ **Arm 1 (AB):** Unknown capacitor modeled as $C_1$ in series with $r_1$. - ✅ **Arm 2 (BC):** Lossless **standard capacitor** $C_2$ — the only standard needed. - ✅ **Arm 3 (CD):** Pure non-inductive resistance $R_3$. - ✅ **Arm 4 (DA):** **Parallel combination** of variable $R_4$ and variable $C_4$ — this dual adjustment enables balancing of both real and imaginary components. - ✅ **Master Formulas (must memorize):** - $C_1 = C_2 \cdot (R_4 / R_3)$ - $r_1 = R_3 \cdot (C_4 / C_2)$ - $\tan\delta = \omega R_3 C_4$ - ✅ **Key Insight:** The equation for $C_1$ is **independent of frequency** (frequency-cancellation is an elegant feature), but $\tan\delta$ is **directly proportional to frequency**. - ✅ **Loss Angle $\delta$:** The small angle by which the actual current deviates from the ideal $90°$ lead. For good dielectrics, $\delta < 0.01$ rad. - ✅ **Phase Angle $\phi = 90° - \delta$** — the current actually leads voltage by this angle. - ✅ **Quality Factor $Q = 1/\tan\delta$** — higher $Q$ = better dielectric. $Q > 1000$ is excellent. - ✅ **Application Domains:** HV cable testing, transformer bushings, capacitor standards, insulator QC, dielectric research. - ✅ **Advantage over Wheatstone bridge:** Can measure **very small** capacitances and **very small loss angles** with high accuracy — far superior for capacitive measurements. - ✅ **Limitation:** Requires a **near-ideal lossless standard capacitor** $C_2$ (usually a compressed-gas or mica reference). - ✅ **Source frequency:** Typically **1 kHz** for laboratory work, **50 Hz** for power-frequency insulation testing. - ✅ **Detector:** Vibration galvanometer (for power frequency) or tuned null detector / headphones (for audio frequency). - ✅ **In numericals, ALWAYS:** Convert all capacitances to **Farads**, resistances to **Ohms**, and frequency to **rad/s** before substituting.

topics.title: Measurement of Frequency - Wien Bridge (Include Simple Problems), Q-Meter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET205 - MEASUREMENTS AND INSTRUMENTATION (ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Measurement of Frequency - Wien Bridge (Include Simple Problems), Q-Meter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Wien Bridge — Definition & Operating Principle

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Definition (Syllabus-Exact):**
> The **Wien Bridge** is a four-arm, AC-excited, ratio-arm bridge network used primarily for the **precise measurement of frequency** over the **audio frequency range (20 Hz to 20 kHz)**. It uses a series RC combination in one arm and a parallel RC combination in the adjacent ratio arm, whose balance condition is a direct function of the supply frequency.

### 1.1.1 Conceptual Analogy — "The Audio Tuning Fork"

Imagine you are tuning a piano. The piano tuner does not measure the tension of each string with a ruler; instead, the tuner relies on the *frequency of vibration* matching a known reference. The Wien Bridge does exactly this electronically — it does **not measure frequency by counting cycles**, but instead it *null-balances* the bridge by adjusting calibrated resistors/capacitors until two voltage drops perfectly cancel. The null is achieved **only at one specific frequency**, and that frequency is read off the calibrated dials. The bridge essentially "listens" for the unknown frequency by comparing it to a tunable RC reference.

### 1.1.2 Why AC Bridges Are Needed for Frequency

A DC Wheatstone bridge can only measure **resistance** because DC has no frequency. Since frequency is a purely AC parameter, we need an AC-excited bridge where the balance condition embeds the term **ω = 2πf** in its equation. The Wien Bridge cleverly achieves this by using **reactive components (capacitors)** whose impedance **Z = 1/(jωC)** is frequency-dependent. The balance equation of the Wien Bridge can therefore be algebraically rearranged to solve for **f** in terms of the known R and C values.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Highlight:**
> Unlike a simple frequency counter, the Wien Bridge is an **analog frequency standard** — it is more accurate in the audio range and is also the heart of the **audio sine-wave oscillator** (Wien Bridge Oscillator) used in every signal generator lab.

---

## 1.2 The Q-Meter — Definition & Operating Principle

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Definition (Syllabus-Exact):**
> The **Q-Meter** is a laboratory instrument designed to measure the **Quality Factor (Q)** of an inductive component (coil) by exploiting the **phenomenon of voltage magnification at series resonance** in an RLC circuit.

### 1.2.1 Conceptual Analogy — "Pushing a Child on a Swing"

Think of a heavy child on a swing. The swing has friction (resistance) and inertia (inductance). To make the swing go higher and higher with only *small pushes*, you push it at *exactly the right natural rhythm* — this is **resonance**. The height the child achieves is *much larger* than the size of your push. The **ratio of (swing height) / (push size) = Q-factor**. A child on a frictionless swing would have an infinite Q. The Q-Meter measures this magnification ratio electronically.

### 1.2.2 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **Q** | Quality Factor (dimensionless) | — |
| **f_r** | Resonant frequency | **Hz** |
| **ω_r** | Angular resonant frequency = 2πf_r | **rad/s** |
| **L** | Inductance | **Henry (H)** |
| **R** | Effective series resistance | **Ohm (Ω)** |
| **C** | Tuning capacitance | **Farad (F)** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:**
> Q-Meters are used to grade RF coils, IF transformers, and inductors in communication receivers. A higher Q means a sharper, more selective filter — crucial in radio tuning.

---

## 1.3 Block-Level Comparison: Wien Bridge vs. Q-Meter

| Feature | Wien Bridge | Q-Meter |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary Measurand** | Frequency (f) | Quality Factor (Q) |
| **Operating Range** | 20 Hz – 20 kHz (audio) | 50 kHz – 50 MHz (RF) |
| **Underlying Principle** | Bridge null-balance (ratio arms) | Series-resonance voltage magnification |
| **Excitation Source** | AC supply of unknown frequency | Internal variable-frequency oscillator |
| **Output Quantity Read** | Calibrated R and C dials | Voltmeter across tuning capacitor |
| **KTU Module Weightage** | ~ 60% of Module 2 questions | ~ 40% of Module 2 questions |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Wien Bridge — Circuit Topology

The Wien Bridge is a six-impedance network, but it can be reduced to a **four-arm** Wheatstone-style bridge by combining pairs. The four arms are:

1. **Arm AB** (non-reactive arm) → Pure resistor **R₃**
2. **Arm BC** (non-reactive arm) → Pure resistor **R₄**
3. **Arm AD** (reactive arm) → **Series R₁-C₁** combination
4. **Arm DC** (reactive arm) → **Parallel R₂-C₂** combination
5. **Excitation** → AC source between A and C
6. **Detector** → High-impedance null detector between B and D

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "ratio arm" arms R₃ and R₄ are pure resistors:** Any reactive component in these arms would introduce an *additional* frequency term in the balance equation, complicating it. Keeping them purely resistive ensures the balance equation contains frequency as a *single* unknown.

### 2.1.1 Impedance of Each Arm

$$
Z_1 \;=\; R_3 \quad \text{(arm AB, pure resistive)}
$$

$$
Z_2 \;=\; R_4 \quad \text{(arm BC, pure resistive)}
$$

$$
Z_3 \;=\; R_1 \;-\; j\,\frac{1}{\omega C_1} \quad \text{(arm AD, series R-C)}
$$

$$
Z_4 \;=\; \frac{R_2 \cdot \dfrac{1}{j\omega C_2}}{R_2 + \dfrac{1}{j\omega C_2}} \;=\; \frac{R_2}{1 + j\omega R_2 C_2} \quad \text{(arm DC, parallel R-C)}
$$

### 2.1.2 Generalized AC Bridge Balance Condition

For any AC bridge, balance (zero detector current) occurs when:

$$
\frac{Z_1}{Z_2} \;=\; \frac{Z_3}{Z_4}
$$

This is the **AC equivalent of the Wheatstone ratio rule**. Substituting the Wien Bridge impedances:

$$
\frac{R_3}{R_4} \;=\; \frac{Z_3}{Z_4} \;=\; \frac{\left(R_1 - \dfrac{j}{\omega C_1}\right)}{\dfrac{R_2}{1 + j\omega R_2 C_2}}
$$

### 2.1.3 Simplifying the Balance Equation

Rearranging:

$$
\frac{R_3}{R_4} \cdot \frac{R_2}{1 + j\omega R_2 C_2} \;=\; R_1 - \frac{j}{\omega C_1}
$$

$$
\frac{R_3 R_2}{R_4 (1 + j\omega R_2 C_2)} \;=\; R_1 - \frac{j}{\omega C_1}
$$

Cross-multiplying:

$$
R_3 R_2 \;=\; R_4 (1 + j\omega R_2 C_2)\left(R_1 - \frac{j}{\omega C_1}\right)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Crucial Real-Imaginary Split:**
> For a balance to exist, the right-hand side must be **purely real** (no imaginary part), because the left-hand side is purely real. This gives us **two equations** — one from the real part, one from the imaginary part — from which we can solve for **two unknowns**.

### 2.1.4 Expanding the Real and Imaginary Components

Expanding the product:

$$
(1 + j\omega R_2 C_2)\left(R_1 - \frac{j}{\omega C_1}\right) \;=\; R_1 - \frac{j}{\omega C_1} + j\omega R_1 R_2 C_2 - \frac{j^2 \omega R_2 C_2}{\omega C_1}
$$

Using **j² = −1**, the last term becomes:

$$
-\frac{j^2 \omega R_2 C_2}{\omega C_1} \;=\; +\frac{R_2 C_2}{C_1}
$$

So the product becomes:

$$
\left(R_1 + \frac{R_2 C_2}{C_1}\right) \;+\; j\left(\omega R_1 R_2 C_2 - \frac{1}{\omega C_1}\right)
$$

### 2.1.5 Balance Conditions

**Condition 1 — Imaginary part = 0:**

$$
\omega R_1 R_2 C_2 - \frac{1}{\omega C_1} \;=\; 0
$$

$$
\omega^2 R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2 \;=\; 1
$$

$$
\omega^2 \;=\; \frac{1}{R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\omega \;=\; \frac{1}{\sqrt{R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2}}\;}
$$

Converting to frequency using **ω = 2πf**:

$$
\boxed{\;f \;=\; \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2}}\;}
$$

**Condition 2 — Real part equality:**

$$
R_3 R_2 \;=\; R_4 \left(R_1 + \frac{R_2 C_2}{C_1}\right)
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\frac{R_3}{R_4} \;=\; \frac{R_1}{R_2} + \frac{C_2}{C_1}\;}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Point:**
> Notice that the **frequency balance condition is independent of R₃ and R₄** — the non-reactive arms do not affect the frequency at which the bridge nulls. They only set the *sensitivity* of the null detector.

### 2.1.6 Special Case — The Equal-Component Wien Bridge

In most practical instruments, the bridge is built with:

$$
R_1 = R_2 = R \quad \text{and} \quad C_1 = C_2 = C
$$

The frequency equation simplifies to:

$$
\boxed{\;f \;=\; \frac{1}{2\pi R C}\;}
$$

This is the **standard form** you will see in 90% of KTU problems.

---

## 2.2 The Q-Meter — Circuit Topology

The Q-Meter is built around a **series RLC resonance circuit**. Its functional blocks are:

1. **Wide-band RF Oscillator** (variable frequency, typically 50 kHz – 50 MHz, with very low output impedance)
2. **Tiny Series Resistance (R_s)** — typically 0.02 Ω (so it does not load the coil)
3. **Test Coil (L_x with internal resistance R_x)** — the unknown inductor under test
4. **Calibrated Variable Capacitor (C)** — the tuning capacitor, range typically 30 pF – 500 pF
5. **Thermo-couple / RMS Voltmeter (V)** — measures the voltage across the tuning capacitor

### 2.2.1 The Working Principle — Voltage Magnification at Resonance

At series resonance, the inductive reactance **X_L = ωL** and the capacitive reactance **X_C = 1/(ωC)** are **equal in magnitude but opposite in phase**, so they cancel. The current in the circuit becomes:

$$
I \;=\; \frac{V_{in}}{R_s + R_x} \quad \text{(purely resistive, maximum)}
$$

The voltage across the capacitor is:

$$
V_C \;=\; I \cdot X_C \;=\; \frac{I}{\omega C}
$$

The **Q-factor of the coil is defined as**:

$$
Q \;=\; \frac{\omega L}{R} \;=\; \frac{1}{\omega R C} \quad \text{(at resonance)}
$$

Substituting:

$$
Q \;=\; \frac{V_C}{I \cdot R \cdot \omega C} \cdot \omega C \;\longrightarrow\; Q \;=\; \frac{V_C}{V_{in}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Magic of the Q-Meter:**
> Because R_s is **negligibly small** (≈ 0.02 Ω) and the voltmeter is **high impedance**, the voltage V across the capacitor terminal is *practically the voltage across the coil*. So the voltmeter directly reads **Q × V_in**. If V_in is held at a calibrated reference (say, 10 mV or 20 mV), the voltmeter can be **directly calibrated in Q units**.

### 2.2.2 Working Procedure (KTU Lab Manual Style)

1. Connect the test coil to the test terminals.
2. Set the oscillator to the desired test frequency **f**.
3. Vary the calibrated capacitor **C** until the voltmeter reads a **maximum** (resonance condition).
4. Read the voltmeter value — this is **V_C**.
5. The Q-factor of the coil is:

$$
\boxed{\;Q \;=\; \frac{V_C}{V_{in}}\;}
$$

If V_in is set to a fixed reference value, the voltmeter scale is *directly* calibrated in Q units.

### 2.2.3 Measurement of Unknown Inductance

Once the Q is known and the resonant frequency f is known, the inductance is:

$$
\omega L \;=\; Q \cdot R \quad \text{or} \quad L \;=\; \frac{Q \cdot R}{\omega}
$$

But more practically, at resonance **ωL = 1/(ωC)**, so:

$$
\boxed{\;L_x \;=\; \frac{1}{(2\pi f)^2 \, C}\;}
$$

where **C** is the value of the calibrated capacitor at resonance.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\displaystyle f = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2}}$ | Wien Bridge general frequency balance | **Hz** |
| 2 | $\displaystyle f = \frac{1}{2\pi R C}$ | Wien Bridge simplified (R₁=R₂, C₁=C₂) | **Hz** |
| 3 | $\displaystyle \frac{R_3}{R_4} = \frac{R_1}{R_2} + \frac{C_2}{C_1}$ | Wien Bridge amplitude balance | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\displaystyle \omega_r = \frac{1}{\sqrt{L C}}$ | Resonant angular frequency (series/parallel RLC) | **rad/s** |
| 5 | $\displaystyle f_r = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L C}}$ | Resonant frequency | **Hz** |
| 6 | $\displaystyle Q = \frac{\omega_r L}{R} = \frac{1}{\omega_r R C} = \frac{1}{R}\sqrt{\frac{L}{C}}$ | Q-factor (series RLC) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\displaystyle Q = \frac{V_C}{V_{in}}$ | Q-Meter voltmeter reading ratio | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\displaystyle L_x = \frac{1}{(2\pi f_r)^2 \, C}$ | Inductance from Q-Meter reading | **H** |
| 9 | $\displaystyle \text{BW} = \frac{f_r}{Q}$ | −3 dB bandwidth of resonant circuit | **Hz** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic (KTU Exam Tip):** "**ωr = 1/√(LC)**" works for *all* RLC resonant circuits (series and parallel). Always write this *first* in your answer — examiners award 2 marks just for this line.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Wien Bridge Balance Equation (Full Working)

> [!NOTE]
> **Objective:** Derive the balance equation $f = \frac{1}{2\pi R C}$ for the simplified Wien Bridge with R₁ = R₂ = R and C₁ = C₂ = C.

**Starting Equation (AC Bridge Null):**

$$
\frac{Z_1}{Z_2} \;=\; \frac{Z_3}{Z_4}
$$

**Step 1 — Substitute impedances:**

$$
\frac{R_3}{R_4} \;=\; \frac{R_1 - j\dfrac{1}{\omega C_1}}{\dfrac{R_2}{1 + j\omega R_2 C_2}}
$$

**Step 2 — Multiply numerator and denominator of the right side:**

$$
\frac{R_3}{R_4} \cdot \frac{R_2}{1 + j\omega R_2 C_2} \;=\; R_1 - \frac{j}{\omega C_1}
$$

**Step 3 — Multiply both sides by (1 + jωR₂C₂):**

$$
\frac{R_3 R_2}{R_4} \;=\; \left(R_1 - \frac{j}{\omega C_1}\right)(1 + j\omega R_2 C_2)
$$

**Step 4 — Expand the right-hand side using the distributive law:**

$$
\left(R_1 - \frac{j}{\omega C_1}\right)(1 + j\omega R_2 C_2)
$$

$$
= \; R_1 (1) + R_1 (j\omega R_2 C_2) - \frac{j}{\omega C_1}(1) - \frac{j}{\omega C_1}(j\omega R_2 C_2)
$$

$$
= \; R_1 + j\omega R_1 R_2 C_2 - \frac{j}{\omega C_1} - j^2 \cdot \frac{R_2 C_2}{C_1}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply j² = −1 to the last term:**

$$
= \; R_1 + j\omega R_1 R_2 C_2 - \frac{j}{\omega C_1} + \frac{R_2 C_2}{C_1}
$$

**Step 6 — Group Real and Imaginary parts:**

$$
\text{Real part:} \;\; R_1 + \frac{R_2 C_2}{C_1}
$$

$$
\text{Imaginary part:} \;\; j\left(\omega R_1 R_2 C_2 - \frac{1}{\omega C_1}\right)
$$

**Step 7 — Equate the imaginary part to zero (balance condition):**

$$
\omega R_1 R_2 C_2 - \frac{1}{\omega C_1} \;=\; 0
$$

$$
\omega^2 R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2 \;=\; 1
$$

$$
\omega \;=\; \frac{1}{\sqrt{R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2}}
$$

**Step 8 — Convert angular frequency to ordinary frequency:**

$$
\omega \;=\; 2\pi f \quad \Longrightarrow \quad f \;=\; \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2}}
$$

**Step 9 — Apply the special-case simplification (R₁ = R₂ = R, C₁ = C₂ = C):**

$$
f \;=\; \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{R \cdot R \cdot C \cdot C}} \;=\; \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{R^2 C^2}} \;=\; \frac{1}{2\pi R C}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;f \;=\; \frac{1}{2\pi R C}\;}
$$

**Step 10 — Equate the real part (amplitude balance — gives the relationship between R₃ and R₄):**

$$
\frac{R_3 R_2}{R_4} \;=\; R_1 + \frac{R_2 C_2}{C_1}
$$

Dividing both sides by R₂:

$$
\frac{R_3}{R_4} \;=\; \frac{R_1}{R_2} + \frac{C_2}{C_1}
$$

In the simplified case (R₁ = R₂ = R, C₁ = C₂ = C):

$$
\frac{R_3}{R_4} \;=\; 1 + 1 \;=\; 2 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad R_3 \;=\; 2 R_4
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students forget that the amplitude balance condition must *also* be satisfied at the null. If R₃ is not set to 2R₄, the bridge will not null out cleanly, even at the correct frequency.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Q-Meter Voltage Equation

**Starting Equation — Series RLC circuit at resonance:**

The current through the coil and capacitor at series resonance is:

$$
I_{max} \;=\; \frac{V_{in}}{R_s + R_x} \;\approx\; \frac{V_{in}}{R_x} \quad \text{(since } R_s \ll R_x\text{)}
$$

The voltage across the tuning capacitor is:

$$
V_C \;=\; I_{max} \cdot X_C \;=\; \frac{I_{max}}{\omega C} \;=\; \frac{V_{in}}{R_x \cdot \omega C}
$$

**Step 1 — Multiply numerator and denominator by ωL:**

$$
V_C \;=\; \frac{V_{in} \cdot \omega L}{R_x \cdot \omega^2 L C}
$$

**Step 2 — At resonance, ω²LC = 1 (since ω² = 1/(LC)):**

$$
V_C \;=\; \frac{V_{in} \cdot \omega L}{R_x \cdot 1} \;=\; V_{in} \cdot \frac{\omega L}{R_x}
$$

**Step 3 — Recognize Q-factor definition:**

$$
Q \;=\; \frac{\omega L}{R_x}
$$

Therefore:

$$
V_C \;=\; Q \cdot V_{in}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;Q \;=\; \frac{V_C}{V_{in}}\;}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Significance:** Since V_in is held constant (e.g., 20 mV) by the internal regulator of the Q-Meter, the voltmeter scale can be **directly engraved in Q units**. A reading of 200 on the voltmeter with V_in = 20 mV immediately means Q = 200.

---

## 3.3 Worked-Out KTU Problem 1 — Wien Bridge Frequency

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model QP, Q2(a)]**
> *A Wien Bridge has the following values: R₁ = R₂ = 10 kΩ, C₁ = C₂ = 0.01 μF, R₃ = 5 kΩ, R₄ = 2.5 kΩ. The bridge is excited by an AC source. Calculate the frequency at which the bridge will be balanced.*

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the type of bridge:** Since R₁ = R₂ and C₁ = C₂, this is the **simplified Wien Bridge** with the standard balance equation.

**Step 2 — Write the frequency balance formula:**

$$
f \;=\; \frac{1}{2\pi R C}
$$

where R = 10 kΩ = 10⁴ Ω and C = 0.01 μF = 0.01 × 10⁻⁶ F = 10⁻⁸ F.

**Step 3 — Substitute:**

$$
f \;=\; \frac{1}{2 \pi \times 10^4 \times 10^{-8}}
$$

$$
f \;=\; \frac{1}{2 \pi \times 10^{-4}}
$$

$$
f \;=\; \frac{10^4}{2 \pi}
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate:**

$$
f \;=\; \frac{10000}{2 \times 3.14159} \;=\; \frac{10000}{6.2832} \;\approx\; 1591.55 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 5 — Check amplitude balance:**

$$
\frac{R_3}{R_4} \;=\; \frac{5 \text{ k}\Omega}{2.5 \text{ k}\Omega} \;=\; 2
$$

Required ratio:

$$
\frac{R_1}{R_2} + \frac{C_2}{C_1} \;=\; 1 + 1 \;=\; 2 \;\;\checkmark
$$

Both conditions are satisfied. The bridge is **properly balanced**.

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{\;f \;\approx\; 1591.55 \text{ Hz} \;\approx\; 1.59 \text{ kHz}\;}
$$

---

## 3.4 Worked-Out KTU Problem 2 — Wien Bridge Design

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model QP, Q2(b)]**
> *A Wien Bridge is used to measure an unknown audio frequency. The values used at balance are R₁ = R₂ = 50 kΩ and C₁ = C₂ = 0.05 μF. Find the unknown frequency.*

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify parameters:**

- R = 50 kΩ = 5 × 10⁴ Ω
- C = 0.05 μF = 0.05 × 10⁻⁶ F = 5 × 10⁻⁸ F

**Step 2 — Apply the simplified Wien Bridge formula:**

$$
f \;=\; \frac{1}{2\pi R C}
$$

$$
f \;=\; \frac{1}{2\pi \times 5 \times 10^4 \times 5 \times 10^{-8}}
$$

$$
f \;=\; \frac{1}{2\pi \times 2.5 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

$$
f \;=\; \frac{1}{5\pi \times 10^{-3}} \;=\; \frac{1000}{5\pi} \;=\; \frac{200}{\pi}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate:**

$$
f \;\approx\; \frac{200}{3.14159} \;\approx\; 63.66 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{\;f \;\approx\; 63.66 \text{ Hz}\;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This frequency is at the very low end of the audio range — such a bridge design would be useful for measuring sub-audible tones or low-frequency hum in power systems.

---

## 3.5 Worked-Out KTU Problem 3 — Q-Meter Reading

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model QP, Q3(a)]**
> *A Q-Meter is used to measure the Q-factor of a coil. At a test frequency of 1 MHz, the tuning capacitor is adjusted to 250 pF to achieve resonance. The voltmeter reads 1.5 V when the input voltage is maintained at 20 mV. Calculate (a) the Q-factor, (b) the inductance of the coil, and (c) the effective series resistance of the coil.*

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Q-factor:**

By the Q-Meter principle:

$$
Q \;=\; \frac{V_C}{V_{in}} \;=\; \frac{1.5 \text{ V}}{20 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V}} \;=\; 75
$$

$$
\boxed{\;Q \;=\; 75\;}
$$

**Part (b) — Inductance:**

At series resonance:

$$
f_r \;=\; \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L C}} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad L \;=\; \frac{1}{(2\pi f_r)^2 \, C}
$$

Substitute f_r = 1 MHz = 10⁶ Hz, C = 250 pF = 250 × 10⁻¹² F:

$$
L \;=\; \frac{1}{(2\pi \times 10^6)^2 \times 250 \times 10^{-12}}
$$

$$
L \;=\; \frac{1}{(6.2832 \times 10^6)^2 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-10}}
$$

$$
L \;=\; \frac{1}{3.9478 \times 10^{13} \times 2.5 \times 10^{-10}}
$$

$$
L \;=\; \frac{1}{9.8696 \times 10^{3}}
$$

$$
L \;\approx\; 1.013 \times 10^{-4} \text{ H} \;=\; 101.3 \;\mu\text{H}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;L \;\approx\; 101.3 \;\mu\text{H}\;}
$$

**Part (c) — Effective series resistance:**

Using the Q-factor relation:

$$
Q \;=\; \frac{\omega L}{R} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad R \;=\; \frac{\omega L}{Q}
$$

$$
R \;=\; \frac{2\pi \times 10^6 \times 1.013 \times 10^{-4}}{75}
$$

$$
R \;=\; \frac{6.366 \times 10^{2}}{75} \;=\; \frac{636.6}{75} \;\approx\; 8.49 \;\Omega
$$

$$
\boxed{\;R \;\approx\; 8.49 \;\Omega\;}
$$

**Verification (alternate formula):**

$$
R \;=\; \frac{1}{\omega Q C} \;=\; \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10^6 \times 75 \times 250 \times 10^{-12}}
$$

$$
R \;=\; \frac{1}{2\pi \times 75 \times 250 \times 10^{-6}} \;=\; \frac{1}{0.1178} \;\approx\; 8.49 \;\Omega \;\;\checkmark
$$

---

## 3.6 Worked-Out KTU Problem 4 — Wien Bridge with Non-Equal Arms

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model QP, Q2(a)]**
> *A Wien Bridge uses the following components at balance: R₁ = 4 kΩ, R₂ = 2 kΩ, C₁ = 0.5 μF, C₂ = 0.25 μF. Determine the frequency of the bridge excitation.*

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Use the general (non-equal) Wien Bridge formula:**

$$
f \;=\; \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

- R₁ = 4 kΩ = 4 × 10³ Ω
- R₂ = 2 kΩ = 2 × 10³ Ω
- C₁ = 0.5 μF = 0.5 × 10⁻⁶ F
- C₂ = 0.25 μF = 0.25 × 10⁻⁶ F

**Step 3 — Compute the product under the square root:**

$$
R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2 \;=\; (4 \times 10^3)(2 \times 10^3)(0.5 \times 10^{-6})(0.25 \times 10^{-6})
$$

$$
= \; 8 \times 10^6 \times 1.25 \times 10^{-13} \;=\; 10^{-6}
$$

**Step 4 — Take the square root:**

$$
\sqrt{R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2} \;=\; \sqrt{10^{-6}} \;=\; 10^{-3}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the frequency:**

$$
f \;=\; \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10^{-3}} \;=\; \frac{1000}{2\pi} \;\approx\; 159.15 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{\;f \;\approx\; 159.15 \text{ Hz}\;}
```

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Wien Bridge — Circuit Diagram (Mermaid Equivalent Schematic)

```mermaid
graph LR
    srcA((AC Source)) --> nodeA
    nodeA --> R3arm["R3"]
    nodeA --> R1C1arm["R1 Series C1"]
    R3arm --> nodeB
    R1C1arm --> nodeD
    nodeB --> R4arm["R4"]
    nodeD --> R2C2arm["R2 Parallel C2"]
    R4arm --> nodeC
    R2C2arm --> nodeC
    nodeC --> srcB((Return))
    nodeB --- nodeD
    nodeB --- det((Null Detector))
    det --- nodeD
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Left vertical branch** (A → B → C) = R₃ (top) and R₄ (bottom) — the **non-reactive ratio arms**.
- **Right vertical branch** (A → D → C) = R₁ series C₁ (top) and R₂ parallel C₂ (bottom) — the **reactive frequency-sensitive arms**.
- **Horizontal connection** B–D is the detector branch. At balance, the potential at B equals the potential at D, so the detector reads zero.

---

## 4.2 Q-Meter — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    osc[Variable Frequency RF Oscillator] -->|V_in| rs[Tiny Series Resistor R_s]
    rs --> coil[Test Coil L_x and R_x]
    coil --> cap[Calibrated Variable Capacitor C]
    cap --> gndA((Ground))
    osc --- gndA
    volt[AC Voltmeter] --- cap
    volt --- gndA
    rs --- gndA
```

**Reading the block diagram:**
- The **oscillator** injects a small known voltage V_in at a chosen test frequency.
- The current flows through R_s (negligibly small), the **test coil** (the unknown L_x and R_x), and the **calibrated variable capacitor** in series — forming a series RLC loop.
- The **voltmeter** is connected across the capacitor and reads V_C.
- The operator adjusts the **oscillator frequency** and the **capacitor C** until the voltmeter shows a **peak (resonance)**, then reads the Q from the calibrated scale.

---

## 4.3 Wien Bridge — Phasor Flow at Balance

```mermaid
graph TD
    input[Excitation V_in at frequency f] --> compare{Bridge Balanced?}
    compare -->|Yes| null[Detector reads ZERO]
    compare -->|No| adjust[Adjust R1 R2 C1 C2]
    adjust --> compare
    null --> read[Read frequency f from dials]
    read --> output[f = 1 / 2 pi R C]
```

---

## 4.4 Q-Meter — Resonance Search Algorithm

```mermaid
graph TD
    start[Set oscillator to test frequency f] --> sweep[Sweep C from min to max]
    sweep --> monitor{Voltmeter V_C rising?}
    monitor -->|Yes| sweep
    monitor -->|Peak detected| lock[Lock C at peak value]
    lock --> readQ[Read V_C from voltmeter]
    readQ --> calcQ[Q = V_C / V_in]
    calcQ --> calcL[L = 1 / 2 pi f squared times C]
    calcL --> done[Report Q and L]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*What is a Wien Bridge? State the balance condition for the simplified Wien Bridge in terms of frequency.*

**Model Answer:**

The Wien Bridge is an AC bridge used for the **accurate measurement of audio-range frequencies (20 Hz – 20 kHz)**. It consists of two resistive ratio arms (R₃ and R₄), a series RC arm (R₁, C₁), and a parallel RC arm (R₂, C₂). The bridge is excited by an AC source whose frequency is to be measured, and balance is detected by a null detector.

For the simplified Wien Bridge where **R₁ = R₂ = R** and **C₁ = C₂ = C**, the balance condition gives:

$$
\boxed{\;f \;=\; \frac{1}{2\pi R C}\;}
$$

(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for arm identification, 1 for formula)

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define the Q-factor of a coil. How is it measured using a Q-Meter?*

**Model Answer:**

The **Q-factor (Quality Factor)** of a coil is defined as the ratio of its inductive reactance to its effective series resistance at the test frequency:

$$
Q \;=\; \frac{\omega L}{R}
$$

It represents the **selectivity** of the coil — a higher Q means a narrower bandwidth and lower losses.

**Measurement using a Q-Meter:** The coil is connected in series with a calibrated variable capacitor. An RF oscillator drives the loop. The capacitor is varied until the voltmeter across it reads a **maximum** (this is the series-resonance condition). At resonance:

$$
Q \;=\; \frac{V_C}{V_{in}}
$$

where V_C is the voltmeter reading and V_in is the (held-constant) oscillator output. The Q-Meter scale is directly calibrated in Q units.

(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for Q equation, 1 for measurement principle)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not skip the amplitude balance equation** in Wien Bridge derivations — the frequency balance alone is *not* sufficient. The amplitude balance **R₃/R₄ = R₁/R₂ + C₂/C₁** must also be stated, or you will lose 2 marks.
> 2. **In Q-Meter problems, always verify the resonance condition** by stating **ω²LC = 1** before substituting numerical values. Skipping this leads to algebraic errors.
> 3. **Unit consistency is critical** — convert kΩ → Ω, μF → F, MHz → Hz *before* plugging in. Most KTU mark deductions are for unit mistakes, not formula errors.
> 4. **For Q-Meter problems, mention the role of R_s** (≈ 0.02 Ω) — it is included to keep V_in approximately equal to the voltage across the coil, validating the Q = V_C/V_in formula.

---

### **Part B — Option A**

#### **Question A. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, ESE Model QP]**
**(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, derive the balance equation of a Wien Bridge used for frequency measurement. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Wien Bridge is balanced with R₁ = R₃ = 10 kΩ, R₂ = R₄ = 5 kΩ, C₁ = 0.1 μF, and C₂ = 0.2 μF. Calculate the frequency of excitation and verify the amplitude balance condition. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

1. Draw the circuit diagram showing AC source between A and C, R₃ in arm AB, R₄ in arm BC, R₁–C₁ series in arm AD, R₂–C₂ parallel in arm DC, and detector between B and D. **(1 mark)**
2. Write the general AC bridge balance equation: **Z₁/Z₂ = Z₃/Z₄**. **(1 mark)**
3. Substitute impedances and cross-multiply. **(1 mark)**
4. Expand the product and apply **j² = −1**. **(1 mark)**
5. Separate real and imaginary parts. **(1 mark)**
6. Equate imaginary part to zero → **ω²R₁R₂C₁C₂ = 1**. **(1 mark)**
7. Write final frequency equation: **f = 1 / (2π√(R₁R₂C₁C₂))** and simplify for equal-component case. **(1 mark)**

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute the frequency using the general Wien Bridge equation:**

$$
f \;=\; \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2}}
$$

Substitute R₁ = 10⁴ Ω, R₂ = 5 × 10³ Ω, C₁ = 10⁻⁷ F, C₂ = 2 × 10⁻⁷ F:

$$
R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2 \;=\; 10^4 \times 5 \times 10^3 \times 10^{-7} \times 2 \times 10^{-7} \;=\; 10^{-6}
$$

$$
\sqrt{R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2} \;=\; 10^{-3}
$$

$$
f \;=\; \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10^{-3}} \;=\; \frac{1000}{2\pi} \;\approx\; 159.15 \text{ Hz} \quad \textbf{(3 marks)}
$$

**Step 2 — Verify amplitude balance:**

$$
\frac{R_3}{R_4} \;=\; \frac{10 \text{ k}\Omega}{5 \text{ k}\Omega} \;=\; 2
$$

Required ratio:

$$
\frac{R_1}{R_2} + \frac{C_2}{C_1} \;=\; \frac{10}{5} + \frac{0.2}{0.1} \;=\; 2 + 2 \;=\; 4
$$

**Result:** The given values do **not** satisfy the amplitude balance condition (2 ≠ 4). The bridge would need R₃ to be **20 kΩ** (so R₃/R₄ = 4) for proper null detection. **(4 marks: 1 for setup, 1 for evaluation, 1 for comparison, 1 for conclusion)**

---

### **Part B — Option B**

#### **Question B. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, ESE Model QP]**
**(a)** Explain the construction and working of a Q-Meter with a neat block diagram. Derive the expression for Q-factor measurement. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In a Q-Meter test on a coil at 2 MHz, resonance is obtained when the tuning capacitor is 100 pF. The Q-Meter reads 250. Calculate the inductance, effective series resistance, and bandwidth of the coil. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

1. **Block diagram** (oscillator → R_s → test coil → tuning capacitor → ground, with voltmeter across capacitor). **(1 mark)**
2. **Working**: oscillator injects V_in at test frequency; C is varied until voltmeter peaks (resonance). **(2 marks)**
3. **Derivation**: At resonance, V_C = I × X_C, and Q = ωL/R. Substituting I = V_in/R and ωL = 1/(ωC) yields **V_C = Q · V_in**, so **Q = V_C/V_in**. **(4 marks)**

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Inductance:**

$$
L \;=\; \frac{1}{(2\pi f)^2 \, C} \;=\; \frac{1}{(2\pi \times 2 \times 10^6)^2 \times 100 \times 10^{-12}}
$$

$$
L \;=\; \frac{1}{(1.2566 \times 10^7)^2 \times 10^{-10}} \;=\; \frac{1}{1.5791 \times 10^{14} \times 10^{-10}} \;=\; \frac{1}{1.5791 \times 10^4}
$$

$$
L \;\approx\; 6.333 \times 10^{-5} \text{ H} \;=\; 63.33 \;\mu\text{H} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}
$$

**Step 2 — Effective series resistance:**

From Q = ωL/R:

$$
R \;=\; \frac{\omega L}{Q} \;=\; \frac{2\pi \times 2 \times 10^6 \times 6.333 \times 10^{-5}}{250}
$$

$$
R \;=\; \frac{795.8}{250} \;\approx\; 3.18 \;\Omega \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}
$$

**Step 3 — Bandwidth:**

$$
\text{BW} \;=\; \frac{f_r}{Q} \;=\; \frac{2 \times 10^6}{250} \;=\; 8000 \text{ Hz} \;=\; 8 \text{ kHz} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}
$$

**Verification by alternate formula:**

$$
R \;=\; \frac{1}{\omega Q C} \;=\; \frac{1}{2\pi \times 2 \times 10^6 \times 250 \times 100 \times 10^{-12}} \;=\; \frac{1}{3.1416 \times 10^{-1}} \;\approx\; 3.18 \;\Omega \;\;\checkmark \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}
$$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU ESE**

### 🔹 Wien Bridge Essentials

- **Bridge type:** AC, four-arm, ratio-type, used for **frequency measurement** in audio range (**20 Hz – 20 kHz**).
- **Two key conditions at balance:** (1) **Frequency balance** — imaginary part = 0, (2) **Amplitude balance** — real part equality.
- **General frequency formula:** $f = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2}}$
- **Simplified formula (R₁ = R₂ = R, C₁ = C₂ = C):** $f = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R C}$
- **Amplitude balance:** $\dfrac{R_3}{R_4} = \dfrac{R_1}{R_2} + \dfrac{C_2}{C_1}$ — in simplified case: **R₃ = 2R₄**.
- **Sensitivities:** series-arm capacitor is **C₁** in arm AD; parallel-arm capacitor is **C₂** in arm DC. Do NOT swap.
- **Applications:** Frequency measurement, **Wien Bridge Oscillator** (BJT/Op-amp lab circuits), harmonic distortion analyzers.
- **Key trick:** The frequency balance condition is **independent of R₃ and R₄** — these only affect detector sensitivity.

### 🔹 Q-Meter Essentials

- **Operating principle:** **Voltage magnification** at **series resonance** in a series RLC circuit.
- **Components:** RF oscillator (V_in), tiny R_s, test coil (L_x, R_x), calibrated variable C, AC voltmeter.
- **Q-factor definition:** $Q = \dfrac{\omega L}{R} = \dfrac{1}{\omega R C} = \dfrac{V_C}{V_{in}}$ (at resonance).
- **Operating range:** typically **50 kHz – 50 MHz** (RF range).
- **L measurement:** $L = \dfrac{1}{(2\pi f_r)^2 \, C}$
- **R measurement:** $R = \dfrac{\omega L}{Q} = \dfrac{1}{\omega Q C}$
- **Bandwidth:** $\text{BW} = \dfrac{f_r}{Q}$
- **Role of R_s (≈ 0.02 Ω):** keeps V_in ≈ voltage across the coil — this is the engineering trick that makes the voltmeter scale directly readable in Q.
- **Why variable C (not variable f):** in practice, the oscillator frequency is held fixed and the capacitor is swept because calibrated capacitors are more linear and stable than RF oscillators.

### 🔹 Cross-Topic Comparison Mnemonics

- **"Wien = Where Is the Null"** — Wien Bridge measures f by finding the null.
- **"Q = Quotient of Voltages"** — Q-Meter measures Q as the ratio V_C / V_in.
- **"R_s is small, V_in is constant"** — always state these two facts in Q-Meter theory answers.

### 🔹 Numerical Pitfalls to Avoid

- ❌ Using **R₃ and R₄ in the frequency equation** — they are *not* present in the simplified form.
- ❌ Forgetting **j² = −1** in the Wien Bridge derivation — this costs 1 full mark.
- ❌ Confusing **series resonance frequency** with **parallel resonance frequency** — for high-Q coils, both are nearly the same, but the Q-Meter uses *series* resonance.
- ❌ Forgetting to **convert kΩ → Ω** and **μF → F** before computing — silent unit errors lose 2 marks.
- ❌ Writing **Q = V_C × V_in** instead of **Q = V_C / V_in** — the voltmeter reading divided by the input, not multiplied.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip:** Always carry out Wien Bridge problems in **two stages** — first compute the frequency, then verify (or compute) the amplitude balance. This structured approach consistently yields full marks in KTU ESE valuation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
