## 5.1 📝 PART A — SHORT ANSWER QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: With a neat sketch, explain the principle of operation of a two-element energy meter used in a 3-phase, 3-wire system. Why is phase adjustment of 30° required in the pressure coil circuit?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A two-element energy meter is essentially **two single-phase induction-type energy meters** mounted on a common spindle, driving a common aluminum disc. The two driving elements produce torques $T_1$ and $T_2$ that act on the same disc. The **total torque** is the algebraic sum $T = T_1 + T_2$, which is proportional to the **total 3-phase power** $P = V_{ab} I_a \cos(\phi_1) + V_{cb} I_c \cos(\phi_2)$. A common permanent magnet provides damping, and a gear train drives the energy register.

**Phase adjustment of 30° is required because** the voltage coils are connected between phases $a-b$ and $c-b$, so the voltage across them ($V_{ab}$, $V_{cb}$) is displaced by ±30° from the phase-to-neutral voltages. A non-inductive phase-shifting resistor is added in series with each pressure coil to advance the flux by 30°, so that the torque is proportional to the **true phase power factor** $\cos(\phi)$ rather than $\cos(\phi \pm 30°)$.

**(3 marks: 1 for construction, 1 for principle, 1 for phase adjustment)**

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define Current Transformer (CT) and Potential Transformer (PT). State any two differences between them.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**CT:** A Current Transformer is a step-down instrument transformer that delivers a **standardized secondary current** (5 A or 1 A) proportional to the **primary current**. It has a **few-turn (often single-turn) primary** and a **many-turn secondary** on a closed core. The primary is connected in **series** with the line.

**PT:** A Potential Transformer is a step-down instrument transformer that delivers a **standardized secondary voltage** (110 V or 100/√3 V) proportional to the **primary voltage**. It has a **many-turn primary** and a **few-turn secondary**. The primary is connected in **parallel (across)** the line.

**Differences:**

| Parameter | CT | PT |
|---|---|---|
| Primary Connection | In series with the line | In parallel (across) the line |
| Turns Ratio $N_p/N_s$ | Low (e.g., 1/100) | High (e.g., 100/1) |
| Secondary Quantity | Current (5 A) | Voltage (110 V) |
| Core Flux Density | High, varies with load | Low, nearly constant |
| Burden Unit | Ohms (Ω) | Volt-Amperes (VA) |

**(3 marks: 1 for CT definition, 1 for PT definition, 1 for differences)**

---

## 5.2 📝 PART B — LONG ANSWER QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each) — INTERNAL CHOICE

### **Question 1 (A)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the construction and principle of operation of a Current Transformer (CT) with a neat phasor diagram. Derive the expression for ratio error. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A CT with a bar primary (1 turn) and 200 secondary turns is used with a 5 A ammeter (internal resistance 0.4 Ω) connected through leads of total resistance 0.6 Ω. The magnetizing current at full load is 0.05 A. Find (i) the actual ratio, (ii) the nominal ratio, (iii) the ratio error, and (iv) the primary current corresponding to 5 A secondary. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Construction:** A CT consists of a **high-permeability silicon-steel toroidal core**, a **primary winding** of 1 to a few turns (the line conductor itself often serves as the primary), and a **secondary winding** of many turns wound uniformly around the core. The secondary is connected to an ammeter or relay. Polarity is marked with P1 and S1 dots.

**Principle of Operation:** The CT operates on the **transformer principle**. The primary current $I_p$ produces a magnetomotive force (MMF) $N_p I_p$, which is balanced by the secondary MMF $N_s I_s$ and a small **magnetizing MMF** $N_p I_m$:

$$N_p I_p = N_s I_s + N_p I_m$$

The magnetizing current $I_m$ is responsible for the **ratio error** and **phase error**.

**Phasor Diagram:** The primary current $I_p$ and secondary current $I_s$ (reversed) are nearly in phase. The magnetizing current $I_m$ lags the secondary induced EMF $E_s$ by 90°. The flux $\phi$ is in phase with $I_m$.

**Derivation of Ratio Error:**

$$I_s = \frac{N_p}{N_s} I_p - I_m$$

Nominal ratio $K_n = \dfrac{N_p}{N_s}$

The **indicated primary current** = $K_n \cdot I_s = K_n \left(\dfrac{N_p}{N_s} I_p - I_m\right)$

**Ratio error formula:**

$$\boxed{\text{Ratio Error} = \frac{K_n I_s - I_p}{I_p} \times 100\% = \frac{-N_p I_m}{N_p I_p} \times 100\% = -\frac{I_m}{I_p} \times 100\%}$$

The negative sign indicates that the secondary current is **less than the ideal** value due to magnetizing current.

### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- $N_p = 1$ turn, $N_s = 200$ turns
- Ammeter resistance = 0.4 Ω
- Lead resistance = 0.6 Ω
- Magnetizing current $I_m = 0.05$ A
- Secondary current $I_s = 5$ A (full scale)

**Solution:**

**(i) Actual Ratio:**

$$K_a = \frac{I_p}{I_s} = \frac{N_s}{N_p} \cdot \frac{I_p}{I_p - I_m}$$

From $N_p I_p = N_s I_s + N_p I_m$:

$$1 \cdot I_p = 200 \cdot 5 + 1 \cdot 0.05$$
$$I_p = 1000 + 0.05 = 1000.05 \text{ A}$$

$$K_a = \frac{1000.05}{5} = 200.01$$

**(ii) Nominal Ratio:**

$$K_n = \frac{N_p}{N_s} = \frac{1}{200} \quad \text{(as a turns ratio)}$$

$$K_n = \frac{I_{p,\text{rated}}}{I_{s,\text{rated}}} = \frac{1000}{5} = 200$$

**(iii) Ratio Error:**

$$\%\text{Error} = \frac{K_n I_s - I_p}{I_p} \times 100 = \frac{200 \times 5 - 1000.05}{1000.05} \times 100$$

$$= \frac{1000 - 1000.05}{1000.05} \times 100 = \frac{-0.05}{1000.05} \times 100 = -0.005\%$$

**(iv) Primary current at $I_s = 5$ A:**

$$I_p = 1000.05 \text{ A} \approx 1000 \text{ A}$$

$$\boxed{K_a = 200.01, \quad K_n = 200, \quad \%\text{Error} = -0.005\%, \quad I_p = 1000.05 \text{ A}}$$

---

### **Question 1 (B)** — *Alternative for Internal Choice* `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`

**Q: (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of an electronic multimeter. Compare it with a conventional (VOM) multimeter. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** The following ranges are required in a DC ammeter using a basic D'Arsonval movement (resistance $R_m = 100 \, \Omega$, full-scale current $I_m = 1$ mA): **10 mA, 100 mA, 1 A**. Calculate the required shunt resistance for each range. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Block Diagram Description:**

An electronic multimeter consists of the following blocks (refer to Mermaid Diagram 4 in Section 4):

1. **Input Attenuator/Range Selector:** A high-impedance resistive divider that scales the input voltage to a level suitable for the active circuits. Different ranges are selected by switching different taps.
2. **Op-Amp Buffer/Pre-Amplifier:** Provides **very high input impedance** (≥ 10 MΩ) so the meter does not load the circuit under test. Also provides signal amplification for low-level measurements.
3. **AC-to-DC Converter:** For AC measurements, a precision rectifier (often using op-amps) converts the AC input to a proportional DC value. **True RMS** meters use a logarithmic-antilog RMS conversion IC.
4. **ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter):** A dual-slope integrating ADC converts the DC voltage to a digital count. Dual-slope is preferred for its **excellent noise rejection** at 50 Hz.
5. **Digital Display:** A 3.5 to 4.5 digit **LCD/LED** displays the measured value with **auto-polarity** and **auto-ranging**.
6. **Current Shunts:** For current measurement, low-resistance shunts (0.01–1 Ω) are switched in.
7. **Constant Current Source:** For resistance measurement, a precision constant current is forced through the unknown $R_x$, and the voltage $V = I R_x$ is measured.
8. **Protection Circuit:** Clamp diodes, PTC thermistors, and fuses protect against overloads.

**Comparison with Conventional (VOM) Multimeter:**

| Feature | Electronic (DMM) | Conventional (VOM) |
|---|---|---|
| Input Impedance | 10 MΩ (fixed) | 20 kΩ/V (depends on range) |
| Accuracy | 0.025% – 0.5% | 2% – 5% |
| Loading Effect | Negligible | Significant at low voltages |
| Display | Digital numeric | Analog pointer on scale |
| Auto-Ranging | Available | Manual range selection |
| Polarity | Auto | Manual (positive scale only) |
| Power | Battery (9V) | Battery + rectifier for AC |

### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** $R_m = 100 \, \Omega$, $I_m = 1$ mA

**General formula for shunt resistor:**

$$R_{sh} = \frac{R_m}{m - 1} \quad \text{where } m = \frac{I}{I_m}$$

**Range 1: I = 10 mA**

$$m_1 = \frac{10 \text{ mA}}{1 \text{ mA}} = 10$$
$$R_{sh,1} = \frac{100}{10 - 1} = \frac{100}{9} = 11.11 \, \Omega$$

**Range 2: I = 100 mA**

$$m_2 = \frac{100}{1} = 100$$
$$R_{sh,2} = \frac{100}{100 - 1} = \frac{100}{99} = 1.0101 \, \Omega$$

**Range 3: I = 1 A**

$$m_3 = \frac{1000 \text{ mA}}{1 \text{ mA}} = 1000$$
$$R_{sh,3} = \frac{100}{1000 - 1} = \frac{100}{999} = 0.1001 \, \Omega$$

$$\boxed{R_{sh,1} = 11.11 \, \Omega, \quad R_{sh,2} = 1.0101 \, \Omega, \quad R_{sh,3} = 0.1001 \, \Omega}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> ### ⚠️ KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING — COMMON PITFALLS
>
> **Students frequently lose marks in this module for the following reasons:**
>
> 1. **Sign Convention in CT Ratio Error:** Students forget that CT ratio error is **always negative** because of the magnetizing current. Writing a positive percentage error will be marked wrong.
>
> 2. **CT vs PT Polarity Convention:** Confusing the dot convention. Always remember: **Current entering P1 must exit S1** for correct polarity.
>
> 3. **Phase Adjustment Resistor in 2-Element Energy Meter:** Students omit the 30° phase compensator in their diagrams. This is a **favourite 7-mark question** in KTU ESE.
>
> 4. **Burden Units Confusion:** CT burden is in **Ohms (Ω)**, while PT burden is in **VA**. Mixing these up will cost 2–3 marks.
>
> 5. **Multiplying Factor vs Turns Ratio:** MF = $N_s/N_p$ for CT and $N_p/N_s$ for PT. Students often invert these.
>
> 6. **DMM True RMS vs Average Rectifier:** A basic DMM with an average rectifier gives **1.11× correction** for pure sine waves, but **wrong** readings for non-sinusoidal (e.g., PWM, square, distorted) waveforms. Always mention **True RMS** capability for high accuracy.
>
> 7. **Two-Element Energy Meter Formulas:** Many students write $P = \sqrt{3} V_L I_L \cos \phi$ directly without showing the **two-element torque sum derivation**. Examiners expect the **intermediate step** with $V_{ab} I_a \cos(\phi + 30°)$ form.

---

## 5.3 🎯 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION NOTES — MUST MEMORIZE FOR KTU 2024 EXAM**

### ✅ Two-Element Energy Meter
- Used for **3-phase, 3-wire systems** (no neutral required).
- Two driving elements on a **common spindle**; torques add algebraically.
- **Phase adjustment resistor (30° shifter)** is essential to correct for the displacement of $V_{ab}$ and $V_{cb}$ from $V_{an}$ and $V_{cn}$.
- Power: $P = V_{ab} I_a \cos(\phi + 30°) + V_{cb} I_c \cos(\phi - 30°) = \sqrt{3} V_L I_L \cos \phi$.

### ✅ Current Transformer (CT)
- **Step-down** current transformer, primary = few turns, secondary = many turns.
- Standard secondary: **5 A or 1 A**.
- **Always short-circuited or connected to a burden** — never open the secondary (dangerous high voltage develops).
- Ratio error: $\%\text{Error} = -\dfrac{N_p I_m}{N_p I_p} \times 100 = -\dfrac{I_m}{I_p} \times 100$ (always **negative**).
- Burden: in **Ω**, includes ammeter + leads.
- Polarity: **P1 (dot)** primary, **S1 (dot)** secondary.

### ✅ Potential Transformer (PT)
- **Step-down** voltage transformer, primary = many turns, secondary = fewer turns.
- Standard secondary: **110 V** (line-to-line) or **100/√3 V** (line-to-neutral).
- Operates at **low flux density** (≤ 1.0 T) to minimize errors.
- Burden: in **VA**, includes voltmeter + relays.
- Polarity: H1 and X1 (or similar) dot convention.
- **Primary fuses** for short-circuit protection; **secondary fuses** for overload.

### ✅ Range Extension
- **Multiplying Factor (MF):** $MF = I_{primary} / I_{secondary}$ for CT, $MF = V_{primary} / V_{secondary}$ for PT.
- For DC ammeters, use **Ayrton Shunt** (universal shunt) for multiple ranges.
- For AC, use **CT/PT** for current/voltage range extension.

### ✅ Electronic Multimeter
- **High input impedance** (10 MΩ) — minimal loading effect.
- **True RMS** for accurate non-sinusoidal measurement.
- **Auto-ranging, auto-polarity, overload protection**.
- Resistance measurement: **constant current source** + ADC.
- AC measurement: precision **op-amp rectifier** + RMS converter.
- **Advantage over VOM:** Higher accuracy, no loading, digital readout.

### ✅ Key Formulas to Remember (Last-Minute Recall)
1. CT: $I_s = \dfrac{N_p}{N_s} I_p - I_m$
2. CT Error: $\%\text{Error} = -\dfrac{I_m}{I_p} \times 100$
3. PT: $V_s = \dfrac{N_s}{N_p} V_p$
4. Ayrton Shunt: $R_{sh} = \dfrac{R_m}{m - 1}$
5. 2-Element Power: $P = \sqrt{3} V_L I_L \cos \phi$
6. Range Extension: $I_{true} = MF \times I_{read}$

### ✅ Numerical Constants to Remember
- **CT secondary current:** 5 A or 1 A
- **PT secondary voltage:** 110 V or 100/√3 V
- **DMM input impedance:** 10 MΩ
- **VOM sensitivity:** 20 kΩ/V
- **2-element power formula constants:** $\sqrt{3} \approx 1.732$, $30°$ phase shift

> [!TIP]
> **FINAL KTU 2024 EXAM TIP:** Practice at least **2 numerical problems** on each of (i) CT ratio error, (ii) Ayrton shunt design, and (iii) two-element energy meter power computation. These three types account for **~70% of the marks** in Module 3 of PCEET205. Also, memorize the **comparison table** of CT vs PT — it is a guaranteed 3–5 mark question in either Part A or Part B.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Digital Voltmeter, Digital Energy Meter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET205 - MEASUREMENTS AND INSTRUMENTATION (ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Digital Voltmeter, Digital Energy Meter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> A **Digital Voltmeter (DVM)** is an electronic instrument that converts an analog voltage signal into a discrete digital readout using an Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC), displaying the measured value on a numeric display (typically 7-segment LEDs or LCDs). A **Digital Energy Meter** is a solid-state device that measures electrical energy consumption (kWh) in AC circuits by sampling voltage and current waveforms, performing digital multiplication (real power), and integrating the result over time, replacing the older electromechanical induction-type meters.

## 1.1 Intuitive Analogy — The Bathroom Weighing Scale

Imagine an **analog bathroom scale** with a spinning dial. You have to squint at where the needle points between marks, and estimating **72.3 kg** is guesswork. Now picture a **digital scale** with a glowing red display that instantly flashes `72.3 kg` — exact, unambiguous, and reproducible.

A **Digital Voltmeter** does exactly the same thing for voltage:
- The analog needle → continuous needle position (infinite resolution but hard to read)
- The digital display → discrete numerical readout (finite but unambiguous resolution)

For a **Digital Energy Meter**, extend the analogy to a **car's trip computer**:
- Just as the trip computer multiplies your **fuel flow rate (liters/min)** by **time (min)** to give you total **fuel used (liters)**,
- A digital energy meter multiplies **instantaneous voltage × instantaneous current (= power)** and integrates it **over time** to give **total energy consumed in kWh**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Engineering Takeaway**
> Both instruments replace mechanical inertia (moving coils, aluminum discs) with **sampled, quantized, and computed** digital signals. The fundamental shift is from *measuring a physical displacement* to *counting numerical pulses* — which gives them **higher accuracy, better noise immunity, and communication capability**.

## 1.2 Classification Snapshot (KTU High-Yield)

| Instrument | Major Sub-Types |
|---|---|
| **Digital Voltmeter (DVM)** | Ramp-type, Dual-Slope Integrating, Successive Approximation, Voltage-to-Frequency, Servo-type |
| **Digital Energy Meter** | Electromechanical (legacy), Electronic / Solid-state, Smart Meter (with communication) |
| **Key Sub-Systems** | ADC, Decoder/Driver, Display, Reference Voltage, Microcontroller/Logic |
| **Display Technologies** | 7-Segment LED, 7-Segment LCD, 14-Segment (alpha-numeric), Dot Matrix |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Hint:** In Module 3, the *most frequently asked* sub-topics are the **block diagram of a Ramp-type DVM** and the **block diagram + working of an Electronic Energy Meter**. Master these two cold.

---

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2.1 Digital Voltmeter — Operating Principles

A DVM is essentially an **ADC + Display driver + Numerical Display**. The unknown analog voltage is digitized, and its equivalent binary count is decoded to drive a numeric display.

### 2.1.1 Block Diagram of a Generic DVM

The functional blocks of any DVM are:

1. **Input Attenuator / Signal Conditioner** — Scales the unknown voltage to the ADC's input range.
2. **Comparator / ADC Core** — Compares input against an internal reference.
3. **Logic / Decoder** — Generates a binary count proportional to the input.
4. **Reference Voltage Source** — Highly stable DC reference (zener-based).
5. **Counter & Latch** — Counts clock pulses until the comparator balances.
6. **Decoder/Driver** — Converts binary count to 7-segment display code.
7. **Numerical Display** — Shows the result.

### 2.1.2 Ramp-Type DVM (Most asked in KTU)

**Principle:** The unknown input voltage $V_{in}$ is compared against a linearly rising **ramp voltage** generated by a ramp generator. A comparator detects the instant the ramp equals $V_{in}$, opening a **gating pulse** that allows clock pulses into a counter. The number of pulses counted is proportional to $V_{in}$.

**Step-by-Step Working:**

1. A **start/reset pulse** initiates the measurement cycle.
2. A **ramp generator** produces a linear voltage ramp: $V_{ramp}(t) = K \cdot t$ where $K$ is the slope.
3. Two **comparators** (or one comparator with a reset) sense the start of the ramp and the equality $V_{ramp} = V_{in}$.
4. The time interval between these two events is $T = V_{in} / K$.
5. An **AND gate** passes clock pulses (of frequency $f_c$) to a decimal counter for this duration $T$.
6. The **count** displayed is $N = f_c \cdot T = f_c \cdot V_{in} / K$.

### 2.1.3 Dual-Slope Integrating DVM (Best noise rejection)

**Principle:** The input voltage is integrated for a **fixed time period** $T_1$, then a known **reference voltage of opposite polarity** is integrated (de-integration) until the integrator output returns to zero. The de-integration time $T_2$ is proportional to $V_{in}$.

**Why it is superior:** Integration inherently **averages out** noise and power-line interference (especially 50 Hz hum). The result is **independent of R and C component values** — only the **clock frequency** and **reference voltage** determine accuracy.

### 2.1.4 Successive Approximation DVM (Fastest)

**Principle:** A **SAR (Successive Approximation Register)** performs a binary search. Starting from the MSB, it sets each bit and checks if the resulting DAC output is less than or greater than $V_{in}$, deciding each bit in one clock cycle. Conversion completes in **N clock cycles** for an N-bit ADC.

### 2.1.5 Voltage-to-Frequency Conversion DVM

**Principle:** $V_{in}$ is converted into a **proportional frequency** $f$ using a VCO (Voltage-Controlled Oscillator). The frequency is then measured by counting pulses over a fixed gate time. The display reads voltage directly.

## 2.2 Digital Energy Meter — Operating Principle

A modern **electronic energy meter** is built around a **microcontroller / DSP** and a **metering IC** (e.g., ADE7755, BL6503, CS5460). It computes real power by sampling voltage and current in quick succession.

### 2.2.1 Block Diagram of an Electronic Energy Meter

1. **Voltage Divider / Potential Transformer (PT)** — Scales mains voltage to a safe, low level (e.g., 0–5 V).
2. **Current Sensor (Shunt Resistor / CT / Rogowski Coil)** — Provides a voltage proportional to the load current.
3. **Signal Conditioner / Anti-Aliasing Filter** — Removes high-frequency noise before sampling.
4. **ADC (typically 16-bit, Σ-Δ type)** — Digitizes both voltage and current signals at high sampling rate (e.g., 4 kHz).
5. **Microcontroller / DSP** — Computes:
   - Instantaneous power: $p(t) = v(t) \cdot i(t)$
   - Real power: $P = \frac{1}{T} \int_0^T v(t) \cdot i(t) \, dt$
   - Energy: $E = \int_0^t P \, d\tau$
6. **Calibration / EEPROM** — Stores calibration constants.
7. **Display Driver + LCD/LED** — Shows cumulative kWh, instantaneous kW, and other parameters.
8. **Communication Module (Optional — Smart Meter)** — RF/PLC/Wi-Fi for remote reading.

### 2.2.2 Working Sequence

1. **Sample** voltage $v(t)$ and current $i(t)$ simultaneously at high rate.
2. **Multiply** corresponding samples: $p_k = v_k \cdot i_k$.
3. **Average** over a full line cycle (e.g., 20 ms for 50 Hz) to obtain real power.
4. **Integrate** power over time to get energy.
5. **Display** cumulative energy; **increment** register at each kWh boundary to drive the electromechanical counter mechanism (older hybrid designs) or just update the LCD.

### 2.2.3 Advantages over Electromechanical Energy Meter

- **Higher accuracy** (Class 0.5 to 1.0 vs. Class 2.0).
- **No moving parts** → less wear, no friction error.
- **Tamper detection** (magnetic, reverse polarity, neutral disturbance).
- **Power factor measurement, kVAh, kVArh** capability.
- **Remote reading** and **time-of-day (TOD) tariff** support.
- **Lower starting current** — records accurately even at 20 mA load.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these equations. They are the KTU exam favourites.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Ramp-type DVM display count | $N = f_c \cdot T = \dfrac{f_c \cdot V_{in}}{K}$ | $K$ = ramp slope (V/s), $f_c$ = clock frequency |
| 2 | Sensitivity of Ramp DVM | $S = \dfrac{dN}{dV_{in}} = \dfrac{f_c}{K}$ | Higher $f_c$ or lower $K$ → more sensitive |
| 3 | Dual-slope de-integration time | $T_2 = \dfrac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \cdot T_1$ | $T_1$ = fixed run-up time |
| 4 | Dual-slope count | $N = f_c \cdot T_2 = f_c \cdot T_1 \cdot \dfrac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}}$ | Independent of R, C |
| 5 | SAR conversion time | $T_{conv} = N \cdot T_{clk}$ | $N$ = number of bits |
| 6 | VFC output frequency | $f = K_{vco} \cdot V_{in}$ | $K_{vco}$ = VCO gain in Hz/V |
| 7 | Instantaneous power | $p(t) = v(t) \cdot i(t)$ | Sample-wise product |
| 8 | Average (real) power | $P = \dfrac{1}{T} \int_0^T v(t) \cdot i(t) \, dt$ | Time average over full line cycle |
| 9 | Energy consumed | $E = \int_0^{t} P(\tau) \, d\tau$ | In Joules, then divided by $3.6 \times 10^6$ for kWh |
| 10 | Energy in kWh | $E_{kWh} = \dfrac{1}{3.6 \times 10^6} \int_0^{t} v(t)\,i(t)\, d\tau$ | Standard utility billing unit |
| 11 | Meter constant | $K_m = \dfrac{\text{No. of pulses}}{\text{kWh}}$ | e.g., 1600 imp/kWh |
| 12 | % Error of energy meter | $\% \text{Error} = \dfrac{E_{actual} - E_{measured}}{E_{actual}} \times 100$ | Negative error = meter runs fast |
| 13 | Display resolution | $\text{Resolution} = \dfrac{\text{Full scale}}{10^n - 1}$ | $n$ = number of full digits |
| 14 | DVM Accuracy | $\%\text{FS} = \dfrac{\pm \text{counts}}{\text{Full scale count}} \times 100$ | Specified as % of reading ± % of FS |
| 15 | 7-Segment decoding | Hex $0\text{xC0}$ lights `0`, $0\text{xF9}$ lights `1`, etc. | Common-cathode truth table |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions & Practical Limits**
> - $T_1$ in dual-slope is chosen as a **multiple of the line period (20 ms for 50 Hz)** to reject mains hum.
> - A DVM is **autoranging** if it automatically selects the proper attenuator; otherwise it's **manual-ranging**.
> - The display is rated as a count of **"full digits"** (each showing 0–9) plus a leading "½" or "1" — e.g., 3½-digit DVM displays 0–1999.

---

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3.1 Derivation — Display Count of a Ramp-Type DVM

**Statement:** Show that the displayed count $N$ in a ramp-type DVM is directly proportional to the unknown input voltage $V_{in}$.

**Given:**
- Ramp voltage: $V_{ramp}(t) = K \cdot t$, where $K$ is the slope in V/s.
- Clock frequency: $f_c$ Hz, period $T_c = 1/f_c$ seconds.
- Comparator triggers gate closure when $V_{ramp} = V_{in}$.

**Derivation:**

**Step 1** — Write the comparator equality condition.

At the instant the gate closes, the ramp voltage equals the input:

$$V_{ramp}(T) = V_{in}$$

Substitute the ramp equation:

$$K \cdot T = V_{in}$$

**Step 2** — Solve for the gate-open duration $T$.

$$T = \frac{V_{in}}{K}$$

This is the time interval during which the AND gate allows clock pulses to reach the counter.

**Step 3** — Compute the number of clock pulses that pass through the gate.

Each clock pulse has period $T_c = 1/f_c$, so the number of pulses counted is:

$$N = \frac{T}{T_c} = T \cdot f_c$$

**Step 4** — Substitute $T$ from Step 2.

$$N = \frac{V_{in}}{K} \cdot f_c = \frac{f_c}{K} \cdot V_{in}$$

**Step 5** — Identify the proportionality constant as sensitivity.

$$\boxed{N = S \cdot V_{in} \quad \text{where} \quad S = \frac{f_c}{K} \;\; \text{counts per Volt}}$$

**Interpretation:** To increase the count (and thus resolution), the designer can:
- Increase clock frequency $f_c$ (limited by counter speed).
- Decrease ramp slope $K$ (limited by linearity and conversion time).
- Conversion time = $T_{conv} = V_{FS}/K$, so smaller $K$ means slower measurement.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Trick Question Trap:** A student is sometimes asked: *“If $f_c$ is doubled, what happens to the count?”* — It doubles. But the *ramp slope must also be doubled* to maintain the same full-scale conversion time. Otherwise, the meter becomes *slower*.

## 3.2 Derivation — Dual-Slope Integrating DVM Count

**Statement:** Prove that the de-integration time $T_2$ depends only on $V_{in}$ and $V_{ref}$, not on integrator component values.

**Given:**
- Integrator: $V_{out}(t) = -\dfrac{1}{RC} \int_0^{t} V_{in}(\tau) \, d\tau$.
- Run-up phase: input $V_{in}$ is integrated for fixed time $T_1$.

**Derivation:**

**Step 1** — Run-up phase output at the end of $T_1$.

Assuming $V_{in}$ is held constant during $T_1$:

$$V_{out}(T_1) = -\frac{1}{RC} \cdot V_{in} \cdot T_1$$

**Step 2** — De-integration phase. Now $V_{ref}$ (opposite polarity) is applied. The integrator output ramps back to zero.

The de-integration equation is:

$$0 - V_{out}(T_1) = -\frac{1}{RC} \cdot (-V_{ref}) \cdot T_2$$

The LHS is the change in output; the RHS is the integral of the negative reference. Simplifying:

$$-V_{out}(T_1) = \frac{V_{ref} \cdot T_2}{RC}$$

**Step 3** — Substitute the result from Step 1.

$$\frac{V_{in} \cdot T_1}{RC} = \frac{V_{ref} \cdot T_2}{RC}$$

The $RC$ factors **cancel completely**:

$$V_{in} \cdot T_1 = V_{ref} \cdot T_2$$

**Step 4** — Solve for the de-integration time.

$$\boxed{T_2 = \frac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \cdot T_1}$$

**Step 5** — Count displayed by the counter (clock frequency $f_c$):

$$N = f_c \cdot T_2 = f_c \cdot T_1 \cdot \frac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}}$$

**Insight:** The accuracy depends **only** on $f_c$ (clock stability), $T_1$ (a fixed digital count), and $V_{ref}$ (a precision zener reference). Drift in $R$ and $C$ cancels out. This is why dual-slope DVMs dominate in industrial digital multimeters.

## 3.3 Derivation — Energy Computation in a Digital Energy Meter

**Statement:** Show that sampled-data multiplication and summation yield the true real energy over $N$ samples per line cycle.

**Given:**
- Sampling rate: $N_s$ samples per second.
- Per-cycle samples for line frequency $f$: $N_c = N_s / f$.
- Sampled voltage: $v_k$ at instant $k \cdot \Delta t$, where $\Delta t = 1/N_s$.

**Derivation:**

**Step 1** — Instantaneous power sample.

$$p_k = v_k \cdot i_k$$

**Step 2** — Real (average) power over one full cycle, using the discrete approximation of the integral:

$$P = \frac{1}{T} \int_0^{T} v(t) \cdot i(t) \, dt \;\approx\; \frac{1}{N_c} \sum_{k=1}^{N_c} v_k \cdot i_k$$

**Step 3** — Energy in Joules accumulated over $M$ complete cycles:

$$E = \sum_{j=1}^{M} P_j \cdot T = \frac{T}{N_c} \sum_{j=1}^{M} \sum_{k=1}^{N_c} v_{j,k} \cdot i_{j,k}$$

**Step 4** — Convert from Joules to the billing unit, **kilowatt-hours**:

$$1 \text{ kWh} = 1000 \text{ W} \times 3600 \text{ s} = 3.6 \times 10^6 \text{ J}$$

$$E_{\text{kWh}} = \frac{1}{3.6 \times 10^6} \cdot E \text{ (in J)}$$

$$\boxed{E_{\text{kWh}} = \frac{T}{3.6 \times 10^6 \cdot N_c} \sum_{j=1}^{M} \sum_{k=1}^{N_c} v_{j,k} \cdot i_{j,k}}$$

**Worked Numerical Example:**
A digital energy meter samples 200 times per cycle (50 Hz → $N_c = 200$, $T = 0.02$ s). For one cycle, the sample products $\sum v_k i_k = 4800$ V²·A (in sampled units). Calculate $E_{\text{kWh}}$ for 1500 such cycles.

$$E = \frac{0.02}{200} \times 4800 \times 1500 = \frac{0.02 \times 4800 \times 1500}{200}$$

$$E = 0.02 \times 24 \times 1500 = 720 \text{ J per cycle aggregated} \rightarrow \text{re-check math:}$$

$$E = \frac{0.02 \times 4800}{200} \times 1500 = 0.48 \times 1500 = 720 \text{ J}$$

$$E_{\text{kWh}} = \frac{720}{3.6 \times 10^6} = 2 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kWh}$$

This tiny result is expected — real meters accumulate over thousands of cycles to register 1 kWh.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — DVM Types

| Parameter | Ramp-Type | Dual-Slope | SAR | VFC-Type |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Conversion Speed | Slow | Slow | Fast (N cycles) | Slow–Medium |
| Noise Rejection | Poor | **Excellent** (line freq.) | Poor | Good |
| Accuracy | Moderate | **High** | High | Moderate |
| Cost | Low | Medium | Medium-High | Low |
| Complexity | Medium | Medium | High (DAC needed) | Low |
| Typical Use | Panel meters | DMM, industrial | Microcontroller ADCs | Isolation amps |

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Ramp-Type DVM

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Unknown Vin"] --> B["Comparator 1"]
    C["Ramp Generator"] --> B
    B --> D["AND Gate"]
    E["Clock Oscillator fc"] --> D
    D --> F["Decimal Counter"]
    F --> G["Decoder Driver"]
    G --> H["7-Segment Display"]
    F --> I["Reset Pulse to Ramp"]
    I --> C
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The comparator checks when ramp voltage equals $V_{in}$.
- The AND gate passes clock pulses only during the comparison interval.
- The counter value (proportional to $V_{in}$) is decoded and shown.
- After display, a reset pulse restarts the ramp for the next measurement.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Dual-Slope Integrating DVM

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Phase1["Run-Up Phase duration T1"]
        A1["Input Vin"] --> S1["Selector Switch Position 1"]
        S1 --> I1["Integrator Op-Amp"]
        I1 --> C1["Comparator 1 threshold 0V"]
    end
    subgraph Phase2["De-Integration Phase duration T2"]
        A2["Reference Vref opposite polarity"] --> S2["Selector Switch Position 2"]
        S2 --> I2["Integrator Op-Amp"]
        I2 --> C2["Comparator detects zero crossing"]
    end
    CLK["Clock fc Hz"] --> GATE["AND Gate"]
    C2 --> GATE
    GATE --> CNT["Counter"]
    CNT --> DEC["BCD Decoder"]
    DEC --> DSP["Display Digits"]
    CNT --> CTRL["Control Logic"]
    CTRL --> S1
    CTRL --> S2
```

**Reading the diagram:** The integrator first integrates $V_{in}$ for a fixed $T_1$ (run-up). The control logic then switches the input to $V_{ref}$ (de-integration). A zero-crossing comparator closes the gate, and the count is held and displayed.

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Electronic (Digital) Energy Meter

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    M["Mains Supply"] --> VDIV["Voltage Divider PT"]
    LOAD["Load"] --> CT["Current Sensor CT or Shunt"]
    VDIV --> SC1["Signal Conditioner Filter"]
    CT --> SC2["Signal Conditioner Filter"]
    SC1 --> ADC["ADC 16 bit Sigma Delta"]
    SC2 --> ADC
    ADC --> MCU["Microcontroller DSP"]
    MCU --> CAL["EEPROM Calibration"]
    MCU --> LCD["LCD Display kWh kW PF"]
    MCU --> COM["Communication RF PLC Wi-Fi Optional"]
    MCU --> PULSE["Pulse LED Output for Calibration"]
```

**Reading the diagram:** Voltage and current are sensed, conditioned, digitized, multiplied in the MCU, integrated over time, and displayed. The pulse LED emits a fixed number of pulses per kWh (e.g., 1600 imp/kWh) for meter testing.

## 4.4 Signal Flow — Instantaneous Power Calculation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Sampled vk"] --> M1["Multiplier"]
    B["Sampled ik"] --> M1
    M1 --> P["Instantaneous Power pk"]
    P --> AVG["Accumulator Sum over Nc samples"]
    AVG --> DIV["Divide by Nc to get Real Power P"]
    DIV --> INT["Time Integrator"]
    INT --> E["Energy Register kWh"]
    E --> DSP["LCD Display Update"]
```

**Reading the diagram:** Sample-by-sample product is summed, normalized to obtain average real power, and time-integrated to obtain cumulative energy. This is the *heart* of every digital energy meter IC (ADE7755, etc.).

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**"List any three advantages of a digital voltmeter over an analog voltmeter." (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Higher accuracy and resolution** — Digital readout eliminates parallax and interpolation errors inherent in needle-type analog meters.
2. **Better noise immunity and stability** — DVMs use integrating or SAR ADCs that reject noise; they have no moving parts to drift or wear.
3. **Auto-polarity indication and autoranging** — A DVM automatically shows negative voltages and selects the appropriate range, reducing operator error.
4. *Optional (1 extra mark):* Digital output can be interfaced to computers, loggers, and PLCs for data acquisition.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**"What is a 'meter constant' in a digital energy meter? State its typical value." (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
The **meter constant $K_m$** is defined as the number of **pulses output (or revolutions, in electromechanical meters) per kilowatt-hour** recorded by the meter.

$$K_m = \frac{\text{No. of pulses}}{\text{kWh}}$$

**Typical values:**
- Electromechanical meter: $K_m = 400$ to $1200$ rev/kWh.
- Electronic digital meter: $K_m = 1000$ to $6400$ imp/kWh (e.g., 1600 imp/kWh is very common).
- It is used as a calibration standard — a known load is applied, and the pulses per minute are counted to verify the meter's accuracy.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Do not** draw a *physical sketch* of the seven segments — KTU expects a **block diagram** with named blocks (Comparator, AND gate, Counter, Decoder, Display).
> 2. **Do not** forget to write the *sampling relationship* $N = f_c V_{in} / K$ explicitly in ramp-type derivations.
> 3. In energy meter answers, **always** mention both voltage and current sensing paths separately — students often describe only one.
> 4. Marks are awarded for: **(i) labelled block diagram (4 marks), (ii) working description (4 marks), (iii) advantages/applications (3 marks), (iv) neatness & accuracy (3 marks)**. Plan accordingly.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper Style]**

**A(a). Draw the block diagram of a Ramp-type Digital Voltmeter and explain its working. Derive the expression for the displayed count. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram:** *(Draw and label the blocks as in Section 4.1 above — 4 marks for diagram)*

Blocks: **Comparator, Ramp Generator, Clock Oscillator, AND Gate, Decimal Counter, BCD Decoder, 7-Segment Display, Reset Generator**.

**Working Description:**

1. A **start/reset pulse** triggers the ramp generator and resets the counter to zero.
2. The ramp generator produces a **linear voltage ramp**: $V_{ramp}(t) = K t$.
3. The **comparator** continuously compares $V_{ramp}$ with the unknown input $V_{in}$.
4. Initially, $V_{ramp} < V_{in}$, comparator output is HIGH → **AND gate is open**, clock pulses flow into the counter.
5. When $V_{ramp} = V_{in}$, the comparator output switches LOW → **AND gate closes**, clock pulses are blocked.
6. The **count** accumulated in the counter is now proportional to the time $T = V_{in}/K$.
7. The count is decoded by the **BCD-to-7-segment decoder** and displayed on the **7-segment display**.
8. A **reset pulse** then re-initializes the system for the next measurement.

**Derivation:**

The gate-open time is:

$$T = \frac{V_{in}}{K}$$

Number of clock pulses (count displayed):

$$N = f_c \cdot T = \frac{f_c}{K} \cdot V_{in}$$

This shows that the **displayed count is directly proportional to the input voltage** $V_{in}$, with the proportionality constant being the **sensitivity** $S = f_c / K$ counts per volt.

---

**A(b). Explain the working of a Dual-Slope Integrating DVM with a neat block diagram. Why is it preferred for accurate measurements? (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram:** *(See Section 4.2 — Integrator, Comparator, AND gate, Clock, Counter, Display, Control Logic, Reference Voltage, Selector Switch)*

**Working — Two Phases:**

**Phase 1 — Run-Up (Fixed Time $T_1$):**
- The selector switch connects the unknown $V_{in}$ to the integrator.
- The integrator output rises (or falls) linearly: $V_{out}(t) = -\dfrac{1}{RC} \int_0^t V_{in} \, d\tau$.
- After a fixed time $T_1$ (set by a digital counter), the control logic switches the integrator input.

**Phase 2 — De-Integration (Variable Time $T_2$):**
- The selector switch connects a **reference voltage of opposite polarity** $V_{ref}$ to the integrator.
- The integrator output ramps back toward zero.
- A **zero-crossing comparator** detects when the output returns to zero and closes the AND gate.
- The number of clock pulses counted during $T_2$ is displayed.

**Mathematical Result:**

$$T_2 = \frac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \cdot T_1 \quad\Rightarrow\quad N = f_c \cdot T_1 \cdot \frac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}}$$

**Why Preferred for Accurate Measurement:**

1. **R and C drift cancels out** — both $T_1$ and $T_2$ depend on the same $RC$ product, which therefore cancels.
2. **Excellent noise rejection** — choosing $T_1$ as a multiple of the line period (e.g., 100 ms for 50 Hz = 5 cycles) automatically averages out 50 Hz mains interference.
3. **High accuracy** — depends only on the stability of the clock $f_c$ and the reference $V_{ref}$, both of which can be made very precise.
4. **No need for precision DAC** — unlike SAR-type.
5. **Self-zeroing** — the de-integration inherently references the output back to zero.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**B(a). With a neat block diagram, explain the working of an Electronic (Digital) Energy Meter. State its advantages over the conventional induction-type energy meter. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram:** *(Draw and label the blocks as in Section 4.3 — Voltage Divider/PT, Current Sensor (CT/Shunt), Signal Conditioner, ADC, Microcontroller, EEPROM, Display, Pulse Output, Communication Module)*

**Working:**

1. **Voltage sensing:** The mains voltage is stepped down by a **potential transformer (PT)** or a resistive divider to a low level (typically 0–5 V).
2. **Current sensing:** The load current is sensed by a **current transformer (CT)**, a **shunt resistor**, or a **Rogowski coil**, producing a proportional voltage.
3. **Signal conditioning:** Both signals are filtered (anti-aliasing) and level-shifted to match the ADC input range.
4. **ADC conversion:** A high-resolution **16-bit sigma-delta ADC** samples both signals at a high rate (typically 4 kHz).
5. **Power computation:** The microcontroller multiplies each voltage sample with the corresponding current sample, $p_k = v_k \cdot i_k$, and averages over a full line cycle:

$$P = \frac{1}{N_c} \sum_{k=1}^{N_c} v_k \cdot i_k$$

6. **Energy integration:** Energy is computed by accumulating $P \cdot \Delta t$ over time:

$$E = \int_0^t P(\tau) \, d\tau$$

7. **Display:** The cumulative energy in kWh, instantaneous kW, power factor, and other parameters are shown on an **LCD**.
8. **Pulse output:** A calibration LED emits pulses at a rate determined by the meter constant $K_m$ (e.g., 1600 imp/kWh) for testing.
9. **Optional features:** Communication (RF/PLC/Wi-Fi), tamper detection, time-of-day (TOD) tariffs, maximum demand tracking.

**Advantages over Induction-Type Energy Meter:**

| Feature | Induction-Type | Electronic Digital |
|---|---|---|
| Accuracy class | Class 2.0 | Class 0.5 to 1.0 |
| Moving parts | Yes (aluminum disc) | None — solid state |
| Starting current | 0.5–1% of rated | 0.1–0.2% of rated |
| Tamper detection | None | Magnetic, reverse, neutral |
| Power factor / kVAh | No | Yes |
| Remote reading | No | Yes (in smart meters) |
| Time-of-day billing | No | Yes |
| Maintenance | High | Very low |

---

**B(b). A digital energy meter has a meter constant of 1600 imp/kWh. During testing at full load, the pulse LED flashes 8 times in 30 seconds. Calculate the load in kW. Assume a single-phase 230 V supply. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Pulses per second.**

$$\text{Pulse rate} = \frac{8 \text{ pulses}}{30 \text{ s}} = 0.2667 \text{ pulses/s}$$

**Step 2 — Energy registered in 30 seconds (in kWh).**

$$E = \frac{\text{Pulses}}{K_m} = \frac{8}{1600} = 0.005 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 3 — Power (energy per unit time).**

$$P = \frac{E}{t} = \frac{0.005 \text{ kWh}}{30 \text{ s}}$$

Convert time to hours:

$$t = 30 \text{ s} = \frac{30}{3600} \text{ h} = 0.00833 \text{ h}$$

$$P = \frac{0.005}{0.00833} = 0.6 \text{ kW}$$

**Alternative formula directly:**

$$P = \frac{\text{Pulses per second} \times 3600}{K_m} = \frac{0.2667 \times 3600}{1600} = \frac{960}{1600} = 0.6 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4 — Current drawn.**

$$I = \frac{P}{V} = \frac{600 \text{ W}}{230 \text{ V}} \approx 2.61 \text{ A}$$

**Final Answer:** **Load power = 0.6 kW**, Current drawn ≈ **2.61 A** at 230 V single-phase.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must-Know for KTU Exam**

- 🔹 **DVM = ADC + Decoder + Display.** All four DVM types differ only in the ADC technique.
- 🔹 **Ramp-type DVM** uses linear ramp + comparator; count $N = f_c V_{in} / K$. Simple but noise-sensitive.
- 🔹 **Dual-slope DVM** integrates $V_{in}$ for fixed $T_1$, then $V_{ref}$ for variable $T_2 = (V_{in}/V_{ref}) T_1$. **RC cancels out → high accuracy.**
- 🔹 **SAR DVM** performs binary search; N-bit conversion in N clock cycles. **Fastest.**
- 🔹 **VFC DVM** converts $V_{in}$ to frequency $f = K_{vco} V_{in}$, then counts pulses.
- 🔹 **3½-digit DVM** displays 0000 to 1999. Resolution = Full scale / 1999.
- 🔹 **Digital Energy Meter** = Sensing (PT + CT/Shunt) + ADC + µC + Display.
- 🔹 **Real power** $P = \frac{1}{T} \int v(t) i(t) dt$; **Energy** $E = \int P \, dt$.
- 🔹 **Meter constant** $K_m$ = pulses per kWh. Typical: **1600 imp/kWh**.
- 🔹 **Conversion to kWh:** $1 \text{ kWh} = 3.6 \times 10^6$ Joules.
- 🔹 **Electronic meters** offer: higher accuracy, no moving parts, tamper detection, TOD billing, remote reading.
- 🔹 **Block diagrams must be labelled with arrows** showing signal flow — KTU awards marks for neat, correct diagrams.
- 🔹 **Don't confuse:** "Ramp-type" uses a linear *voltage* ramp; "Staircase-ramp" uses a *stepped* waveform. KTU usually asks about the linear-ramp variant.
- 🔹 **Energy meter error formula:** $\%\text{Error} = \dfrac{E_{actual} - E_{measured}}{E_{actual}} \times 100$. **Negative % error = meter runs FAST (consumer is at loss).**
- 🔹 **TOD (Time-of-Day) tariff:** Different rates for peak, off-peak, and shoulder hours — only feasible in digital/smart meters.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** Always carry a labelled block diagram in your answer, even for a 3-mark short answer. Visuals fetch extra marks. Also, when writing derivations, **box your final equation** — examiners scan for boxed results first.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: TOD Meter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET205 - MEASUREMENTS AND INSTRUMENTATION (ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** TOD Meter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **TOD Meter (Time-of-Day Meter)** is a specialized static/electronic energy meter that records the consumption of electrical energy during **different predefined time zones** of a 24-hour day, typically distinguishing between **Peak Hours**, **Normal Hours**, and **Off-Peak Hours**. It enables **time-variant (multi-tariff) billing**, where the unit price of energy differs according to the time period in which it is consumed.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus context, the TOD meter is classified as a **special-purpose electronic energy meter** that integrates a **real-time clock (RTC)**, a **microcontroller/microprocessor-based register bank**, and a **conventional energy measurement front-end** (based on shunt or CT for current sensing and voltage divider for voltage sensing).

**Standard Time Slots used by Indian Utilities (CEA Guidelines):**

| Time Zone | Duration | Typical Tariff |
|---|---|---|
| Peak Hours | 06:00 – 10:00 & 18:00 – 22:00 | Highest Rate |
| Normal Hours | 10:00 – 18:00 | Standard Rate |
| Off-Peak Hours | 22:00 – 06:00 | Lowest Rate |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Highway Toll Booth:** Imagine a toll plaza on a national highway. The toll fee is **higher during morning and evening rush hours** (peak load on the road) and **cheaper late at night** (very few vehicles). The toll system **identifies the time slot** of your crossing and charges you accordingly. A **TOD meter is the electrical equivalent** of this system — it watches the **clock** while measuring energy, and then files your consumption into the correct "tariff basket" (peak, normal, or off-peak). The electricity utility uses this basket to **bill you differently** for the same kilowatt-hour (kWh) consumed at different hours.

The energy itself is identical (1 kWh is 1 kWh), but the **economic value of that kWh varies with time** because the **generation, transmission, and distribution cost** of electricity is non-uniform across the day.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Fundamental unit of energy registered:** **kWh (kilowatt-hour)** or **Wh**.
- **Standard reference frequency:** **50 Hz** (India, Europe, most of Asia).
- **Standard reference voltage (LT consumer):** **230 V (single-phase)** / **415 V (three-phase)**.
- **Real-Time Clock (RTC) accuracy:** Typically **±5 ppm** (≈ ±13 seconds per month).
- **Minimum resolution of time slot:** **15 minutes** (as per CEA metering regulations 2006).
- **Meter constant for TOD registers:** Usually **6400 impulses/kWh** or **3200 impulses/kWh**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Philosophy — The "Why" Behind TOD Metering

The fundamental economic principle behind TOD metering is **demand-side load management**. The electricity grid experiences highly non-uniform loading:

1. **Morning Peak (06:00–10:00):** Industrial startup, water heaters, morning cooking, office ACs.
2. **Evening Peak (18:00–22:00):** Residential lighting peak, TV usage, ACs, EV charging.
3. **Off-Peak (22:00–06:00):** Only essential loads (refrigerators, streetlights, industries running night shifts).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does the utility need separate time zones?**  
> Because supplying **peak-hour electricity requires spinning reserve generators** (gas turbines, pumped hydro) that have **higher marginal generation cost** than the **baseload plants** (coal, nuclear) which comfortably run 24/7. The TOD meter passes this **true time-based cost** onto the consumer, incentivizing them to **shift flexible loads** (washing machines, dishwashers, EV charging, geysers) to off-peak hours — a process called **"Peak Shaving"** or **"Load Shifting"**.

### 2.2 Architectural Building Blocks of a TOD Meter

A modern TOD meter is a **microprocessor-based static energy meter** with the following hierarchical subsystems:

**Step 1 — Sensing Stage:**
- **Voltage Sensing:** Resistive voltage divider (for single-phase) or PT (Potential Transformer, for HT meters).
- **Current Sensing:** Shunt resistor (low current) or CT (Current Transformer, for higher ratings).

**Step 2 — Signal Conditioning:**
- The sensed voltage and current signals are scaled, filtered, and fed to an **analog multiplier** (e.g., AD633, MPY634) or processed digitally via an **ADC** in a fully digital meter.

**Step 3 — Power Computation:**
- The instantaneous power $p(t) = v(t) \cdot i(t)$ is computed.
- Active power is obtained by averaging over one cycle:
$$P = \frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} v(t)\cdot i(t)\,dt$$

**Step 4 — Energy Integration:**
- Energy is integrated over time:
$$E = \int_{0}^{t} P(\tau)\,d\tau$$
- The integration is performed by an **energy register counter** inside the microcontroller.

**Step 5 — Time-of-Day Decision Logic:**
- The **Real-Time Clock (RTC)** continuously outputs the current time.
- The microcontroller compares the current time with the **pre-programmed tariff schedule** stored in non-volatile memory (EEPROM/Flash).
- The energy pulses are **routed to one of three (or more) registers**:
  - Register R1 → Peak hour energy ($E_{peak}$)
  - Register R2 → Normal hour energy ($E_{normal}$)
  - Register R3 → Off-peak hour energy ($E_{off\text{-}peak}$)

**Step 6 — Display & Communication:**
- The accumulated energy in each register is shown on an **LCD display** (cyclically or on button press).
- Data may be transmitted via **optical port, RS-232, RS-485, PLC, or RF/GPRS** for AMR (Automatic Meter Reading).

### 2.3 The "How" — Pulse Routing Logic

Every time the energy integrator accumulates a fixed quantum of energy (say, 1/6400 kWh), it generates a **calibration pulse**. In a TOD meter, this pulse does **not** increment a single counter; instead, it increments **whichever register is currently "active"** based on the RTC output.

The pulse routing equation can be expressed as:

$$E_{registered}(t) = \sum_{k=1}^{N} \int_{t_{start,k}}^{t_{end,k}} P(\tau)\cdot \mathbb{1}_{T_k}(\tau)\,d\tau$$

where $\mathbb{1}_{T_k}(\tau)$ is the **indicator function** that equals 1 when the time $\tau$ falls within tariff slot $T_k$, and 0 otherwise.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Reference for KTU 2024 ESE**

| # | Formula / Expression | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E = P \times t$ | Basic energy equation (DC / constant load) | kWh |
| 2 | $E = \int V(t)\cdot I(t)\,dt$ | General AC energy integration | kWh |
| 3 | $E_{total} = E_{peak} + E_{normal} + E_{off\text{-}peak}$ | Total energy = sum of TOD registers | kWh |
| 4 | $Bill = (E_p \cdot R_p) + (E_n \cdot R_n) + (E_o \cdot R_o)$ | Multi-tariff billing equation | ₹ (Rupees) |
| 5 | $E_p + E_n + E_o = E_{total}$ | Energy conservation across TOD slots | kWh |
| 6 | $PF = \cos\phi = \frac{P}{V\cdot I}$ | Power factor (for kVAh calculation) | dimensionless (0–1) |
| 7 | $E_{reactive} = V\cdot I\cdot \sin\phi \cdot t$ | Reactive energy (kVARh) | kVARh |
| 8 | $Load\,Factor = \frac{Average\,Load}{Peak\,Load}$ | Load factor (off-peak shifts improve this) | ratio (0–1) |
| 9 | $MD_kW = \max_{t \in T_k}\{P(t)\}$ | Maximum demand within time slot $T_k$ | kW |
| 10 | $\%\,Peak\,Energy = \frac{E_{peak}}{E_{total}} \times 100$ | Peak energy share indicator | % |

> **Note on table syntax:** All "such that" or "divides" relations use `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking the markdown table pipe structure. The vertical bar is **never** used literally inside a table cell.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Demand-side load management** for DISCOMs (Distribution Companies) in India.
2. **Time-of-Use (ToU) tariff enforcement** for HT (High Tension) industrial consumers.
3. **Smart grid integration** with SCADA/AMI systems.
4. **Demand response programs** — incentivizing consumers to shift loads.
5. **Captive power / DG synchronization billing** in large industries and commercial complexes.
6. **EV charging stations** — where TOD pricing is essential to encourage overnight charging.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Problem Framework

**Problem Statement (Exhaustive Worked Example):**  
A factory is billed using a **TOD meter** with the following tariff structure (Indian utility, FY 2024–25):

- **Peak hours (06:00–10:00 and 18:00–22:00):** ₹ 8.50 per kWh
- **Normal hours (10:00–18:00):** ₹ 6.75 per kWh
- **Off-peak hours (22:00–06:00):** ₹ 4.25 per kWh

The TOD meter registers show the following consumption over one billing cycle (30 days):

- $E_{peak} = 4{,}800$ kWh
- $E_{normal} = 7{,}200$ kWh
- $E_{off\text{-}peak} = 3{,}000$ kWh

A **fixed demand charge** of ₹ 350/kVA is also applicable, and the **average power factor** recorded is 0.85 lagging.

**Required:**
1. Total energy consumed.
2. Total energy bill (energy charge only).
3. Average cost per kWh.
4. Percentage contribution of peak energy to total bill.
5. Verify energy conservation across TOD slots.

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute the Total Energy Consumed**

The total energy is the arithmetic sum of all three TOD register readings. This follows from the **law of conservation of energy** — the same electrons flowing through the meter contribute to one and only one register at any given instant.

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{total} &= E_{peak} + E_{normal} + E_{off\text{-}peak} \\
&= 4{,}800 + 7{,}200 + 3{,}000 \\
&= 15{,}000 \text{ kWh}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** Each kWh consumed by the factory was registered in **exactly one** of the three TOD registers based on the time of consumption. Therefore, summing the registers gives the total.

---

**Step 2 — Compute the Energy Charge (Bill from TOD Registers Only)**

Using the multi-tariff billing equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Bill_{energy} &= (E_{peak} \times R_{peak}) + (E_{normal} \times R_{normal}) + (E_{off\text{-}peak} \times R_{off\text{-}peak}) \\
&= (4{,}800 \times 8.50) + (7{,}200 \times 6.75) + (3{,}000 \times 4.25)
\end{aligned}
$$

Computing each term individually:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Peak charge} &= 4{,}800 \times 8.50 = ₹\,40{,}800 \\
\text{Normal charge} &= 7{,}200 \times 6.75 = ₹\,48{,}600 \\
\text{Off-peak charge} &= 3{,}000 \times 4.25 = ₹\,12{,}750
\end{aligned}
$$

Summing the three components:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Bill_{energy} &= 40{,}800 + 48{,}600 + 12{,}750 \\
&= ₹\,1{,}02{,}150
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**Step 3 — Compute the Average Cost per kWh (Blended Tariff)**

The average (or blended) cost per kWh is the ratio of the total bill to the total energy:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Average Rate} &= \frac{Bill_{energy}}{E_{total}} \\
&= \frac{1{,}02{,}150}{15{,}000} \\
&= ₹\,6.81 \text{ per kWh}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** This blended rate **lies between the off-peak rate (₹4.25) and the peak rate (₹8.50)**, and is closer to the normal rate (₹6.75) because the normal slot has the largest kWh contribution. This blended value is useful for comparing TOD-metered consumers against flat-rate consumers.

---

**Step 4 — Percentage Contribution of Peak Energy to the Total Bill**

The peak slot contributes 4,800 kWh out of 15,000 kWh. In **energy terms**, the percentage is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%E_{peak} &= \frac{E_{peak}}{E_{total}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{4{,}800}{15{,}000} \times 100 \\
&= 32.0\%
\end{aligned}
$$

However, in **monetary terms**, the peak share of the bill is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%Bill_{peak} &= \frac{\text{Peak charge}}{Bill_{energy}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{40{,}800}{1{,}02{,}150} \times 100 \\
&\approx 39.94\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Key Insight:** Although peak-hour energy is only **32% of total kWh**, it accounts for **nearly 40% of the bill** because of the higher per-unit rate. This non-linearity is the **primary economic lever** of TOD metering.

---

**Step 5 — Energy Conservation Check (Sanity Verification)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{peak} + E_{normal} + E_{off\text{-}peak} &\stackrel{?}{=} E_{total} \\
4{,}800 + 7{,}200 + 3{,}000 &\stackrel{?}{=} 15{,}000 \\
15{,}000 &= 15{,}000 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

The energy registers are self-consistent, validating the meter reading integrity.

---

### 3.3 Bonus: Peak-Shaving Savings Calculation

Suppose the factory decides to **shift 1,500 kWh** from peak hours to off-peak hours (e.g., by running a process water heating cycle at night instead of during the day). Compute the **monthly savings**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta Bill &= (1{,}500 \times R_{peak}) - (1{,}500 \times R_{off\text{-}peak}) \\
&= 1{,}500 \times (8.50 - 4.25) \\
&= 1{,}500 \times 4.25 \\
&= ₹\,6{,}375 \text{ per month}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Annual savings} &= 6{,}375 \times 12 = ₹\,76{,}500
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** The consumer is **economically incentivized** to flatten their load curve. The utility benefits because the **grid peak demand drops**, deferring costly capacity addition. This is a **win-win mechanism** — a classic example of price signaling in electrical engineering economics.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Parameter | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Total Energy ($E_{total}$) | 15,000 | kWh |
| Peak Energy ($E_{peak}$) | 4,800 | kWh |
| Normal Energy ($E_{normal}$) | 7,200 | kWh |
| Off-Peak Energy ($E_{off\text{-}peak}$) | 3,000 | kWh |
| Energy Bill ($Bill_{energy}$) | 1,02,150 | ₹ |
| Blended Tariff | 6.81 | ₹/kWh |
| Peak Energy Share | 32.0 | % |
| Peak Bill Share | 39.94 | % |
| Monthly Savings (1,500 kWh shift) | 6,375 | ₹ |
| Annual Savings | 76,500 | ₹ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Internal Architecture of a TOD Meter

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **signal flow and decision logic** inside a typical static TOD energy meter.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[AC Mains Supply 230V / 415V] --> B[Voltage Divider / PT]
    A --> C[Current Shunt / CT]
    B --> D[Signal Conditioner and Anti Aliasing Filter]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Analog Multiplier or ADC + DSP]
    E --> F[Active Power Calculator P avg]
    F --> G[Energy Integrator V to Pulses]
    G --> H[Pulse Train 6400 imp per kWh]
    H --> I{Microcontroller with Tariff Schedule}
    RTC[Real Time Clock RTC DS3231] --> I
    I --> J1[Register R1 Peak Energy]
    I --> J2[Register R2 Normal Energy]
    I --> J3[Register R3 Off Peak Energy]
    J1 --> K[Non Volatile EEPROM Storage]
    J2 --> K
    J3 --> K
    K --> L[LCD Display Cyclic]
    K --> M[Communication Optical RS485 GPRS]
    M --> N[Utility Billing Server AMR AMI]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid State Diagram — Tariff Slot Decision Logic

This state machine describes how the **microcontroller switches the active register** based on the RTC time.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Initialize
    Initialize --> ReadRTC : Power On or Reset
    ReadRTC --> PeakSlot : 06:00 to 10:00
    ReadRTC --> NormalSlot : 10:00 to 18:00
    ReadRTC --> PeakSlot : 18:00 to 22:00
    ReadRTC --> OffPeakSlot : 22:00 to 06:00
    PeakSlot --> ReadRTC : Every 1 minute polling
    NormalSlot --> ReadRTC : Every 1 minute polling
    OffPeakSlot --> ReadRTC : Every 1 minute polling
    PeakSlot --> Accumulate : Increment R1
    NormalSlot --> Accumulate : Increment R2
    OffPeakSlot --> Accumulate : Increment R3
    Accumulate --> ReadRTC : Continue
```

### 4.3 Tariff Timeline Visualization (Mermaid Gantt Chart)

```mermaid
gantt
    title Daily TOD Tariff Schedule (24-Hour Cycle)
    dateFormat  HH:mm
    axisFormat %H:%M
    section Tariff Zones
    Off-Peak (Cheap)    :off1, 22:00, 8h
    Peak (Expensive)    :peak1, 06:00, 4h
    Normal (Standard)   :norm1, 10:00, 8h
    Peak (Expensive)    :peak2, 18:00, 4h
```

### 4.4 Functional Architecture Flow — End-to-End TOD Billing System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Consumer Load Factory or Home] --> P2[TOD Meter at Premise]
    P2 --> P3[Local LCD and Optical Port]
    P2 --> P4[AMR via GPRS or PLC]
    P4 --> P5[Utility Central Server]
    P5 --> P6[Tariff Engine Software]
    P6 --> P7[Bill Generation Multi Rate]
    P7 --> P8[Consumer Invoice and Payment]
    P5 --> P9[Data Analytics Load Curve]
    P9 --> P10[Demand Forecasting]
    P10 --> P11[Generation Planning]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** Each block represents a functional subsystem. The arrows depict the **direction of data/information flow**, not physical current flow. The RTC is the **timekeeper** that gates the routing of energy pulses into the correct register.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**  
**Define a TOD meter. Why is it preferred over a conventional energy meter for HT industrial consumers?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**  
A **Time-of-Day (TOD) meter** is a multi-register electronic energy meter that records the consumption of electrical energy separately for **different time zones of the day** — namely peak, normal, and off-peak hours — based on a built-in real-time clock and pre-programmed tariff schedule.

It is preferred over a conventional meter for **HT (High Tension) industrial consumers** because:
1. It enables **time-variant (ToU) billing**, passing the true time-based cost of generation onto the consumer.
2. It **discourages peak-hour consumption** and incentivizes **load shifting** to off-peak hours, helping the utility flatten the load curve.
3. It supports **demand-side management** and **regulatory compliance** with CEA (Central Electricity Authority) multi-tariff guidelines.

*(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for justification)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**  
**List any three functional blocks of a TOD meter and state their purpose.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**  
The three essential functional blocks of a TOD meter are:

| # | Block | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Real-Time Clock (RTC)** | Provides the current time reference to determine which tariff slot is active. |
| 2 | **Energy Measurement Front-End (Shunt/CT + Multiplier)** | Senses voltage and current, computes instantaneous and average active power. |
| 3 | **Multi-Register Counter Bank (EEPROM-based)** | Stores accumulated energy separately for each tariff slot (peak, normal, off-peak). |

*(1 mark per block with its purpose)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Path 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]**  
**(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the **working principle of a TOD meter**. Clearly identify the role of the **Real-Time Clock (RTC)**, **microcontroller**, and **register bank**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A commercial complex is billed by a TOD meter with the following tariff:

- Peak hours (06:00–10:00 & 18:00–22:00): **₹ 9.20 per kWh**  
- Normal hours (10:00–18:00): **₹ 7.10 per kWh**  
- Off-peak hours (22:00–06:00): **₹ 4.80 per kWh**

For one billing month, the meter registers: $E_{peak} = 6{,}000$ kWh, $E_{normal} = 9{,}000$ kWh, $E_{off\text{-}peak} = 5{,}000$ kWh.

Calculate:  
(i) Total energy consumed. **(1 mark)**  
(ii) Total energy bill. **(2 marks)**  
(iii) Blended (average) tariff per kWh. **(1 mark)**  
(iv) If the consumer shifts 2,000 kWh from peak to off-peak, compute the **monthly savings**. **(3 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q-A:**

**(a) Block Diagram & Working (7 marks):**

The block diagram of a TOD meter is as shown in Section 4.1 of these notes. The working proceeds in the following stages:

1. **Sensing Stage:** Voltage and current are sensed using a voltage divider (or PT) and a current shunt (or CT). These scale down the high mains voltage and current to low-level signals compatible with the metering IC. *(1 mark)*

2. **Signal Conditioning:** The sensed signals are filtered (low-pass anti-aliasing) and amplified. The metering IC computes the **active power** by multiplying instantaneous voltage and current, and then **averaging** over one mains cycle. *(1 mark)*

3. **Energy-to-Pulse Conversion:** The active power is **integrated over time** to yield energy, which is then converted to a **pulse train** at a frequency proportional to the power (e.g., 6400 pulses per kWh). *(1 mark)*

4. **Time-Keeping via RTC:** A battery-backed **DS3231 or equivalent RTC** continuously tracks wall-clock time with high accuracy (typically ±5 ppm). *(1 mark)*

5. **Tariff Decision Logic in Microcontroller:** The microcontroller polls the RTC at regular intervals (say, every minute). It compares the current time against the **pre-programmed tariff schedule** stored in non-volatile memory. Based on the result, it routes every incoming energy pulse to **one of the three registers** (R1 = Peak, R2 = Normal, R3 = Off-peak). *(2 marks)*

6. **Storage, Display & Communication:** The accumulated register values are stored in **EEPROM** (preserved during power failure), displayed on an **LCD**, and optionally transmitted via **optical port, RS-485, or GPRS** for AMR. *(1 mark)*

**Key Insight:** The RTC is the "**heart**" of the TOD meter — without accurate timekeeping, the pulse routing fails and the multi-tariff billing becomes meaningless.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Total energy consumed (1 mark):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{total} &= E_{peak} + E_{normal} + E_{off\text{-}peak} \\
&= 6{,}000 + 9{,}000 + 5{,}000 \\
&= 20{,}000 \text{ kWh}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Total energy bill (2 marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
Bill &= (6{,}000 \times 9.20) + (9{,}000 \times 7.10) + (5{,}000 \times 4.80) \\
&= 55{,}200 + 63{,}900 + 24{,}000 \\
&= ₹\,1{,}43{,}100
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Blended tariff (1 mark):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Blended Rate} &= \frac{1{,}43{,}100}{20{,}000} \\
&= ₹\,7.155 \text{ per kWh}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Savings from load shifting (3 marks):**

Rate difference between peak and off-peak:
$$
\Delta R = 9.20 - 4.80 = ₹\,4.40 \text{ per kWh}
$$

Monthly savings for shifting 2,000 kWh:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Savings} &= 2{,}000 \times 4.40 \\
&= ₹\,8{,}800 \text{ per month}
\end{aligned}
$$

Annual savings:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Annual Savings} &= 8{,}800 \times 12 = ₹\,1{,}05{,}600
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Path 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]**  
**(a)** Discuss the **advantages and limitations of TOD meters** compared to conventional electromechanical energy meters. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An industrial consumer has the following load profile recorded by a TOD meter over one day:

- Peak consumption (4 hours): **500 kW average**  
- Normal consumption (8 hours): **300 kW average**  
- Off-peak consumption (12 hours): **100 kW average**

Tariff rates are: Peak = ₹ 8.00/kWh, Normal = ₹ 6.50/kWh, Off-peak = ₹ 4.00/kWh.

Compute:  
(i) Energy consumed in each slot. **(3 marks)**  
(ii) Total daily energy bill. **(2 marks)**  
(iii) The **load factor** of the consumer. **(2 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q-B:**

**(a) Advantages and Limitations (7 marks):**

**Advantages of TOD Meters (4 marks):**

1. **Multi-tariff billing** accurately reflects the **time-variant cost of generation and transmission**.
2. **Encourages load shifting** — consumers shift flexible loads (e.g., EV charging, water heating) to off-peak hours, reducing grid stress.
3. **Helps utilities flatten the load curve**, deferring investment in new peak-load generating capacity.
4. **Tamper detection and AMR compatibility** — modern TOD meters are static (electronic) and offer features like power-quality monitoring, event logging, and remote reading.
5. **High accuracy (Class 0.2s / 0.5s / 1.0)** as per IS 14697, better than conventional induction-type meters over the long term.

**Limitations of TOD Meters (3 marks):**

1. **Higher initial cost** compared to simple electromechanical meters.
2. **Dependency on RTC accuracy** — RTC drift can lead to billing disputes.
3. **Complexity in programming and tariff revision** — schedule changes require firmware updates or reprogramming.
4. **Vulnerability to power-quality issues** — surge, harmonic distortion, and DC injection must be carefully handled in the design.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Energy in each slot (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{peak} &= P_{peak} \times t_{peak} = 500 \times 4 = 2{,}000 \text{ kWh} \\
E_{normal} &= P_{normal} \times t_{normal} = 300 \times 8 = 2{,}400 \text{ kWh} \\
E_{off\text{-}peak} &= P_{off\text{-}peak} \times t_{off\text{-}peak} = 100 \times 12 = 1{,}200 \text{ kWh}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Total daily energy bill (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{total} &= 2{,}000 + 2{,}400 + 1{,}200 = 5{,}600 \text{ kWh} \\
Bill &= (2{,}000 \times 8.00) + (2{,}400 \times 6.50) + (1{,}200 \times 4.00) \\
&= 16{,}000 + 15{,}600 + 4{,}800 \\
&= ₹\,36{,}400
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Load Factor (2 marks):**

The **load factor** is the ratio of **average load over a period** to the **peak load** during that period.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Average Load} &= \frac{E_{total}}{24 \text{ hours}} = \frac{5{,}600}{24} \approx 233.33 \text{ kW} \\
\text{Peak Load} &= 500 \text{ kW} \\
\text{Load Factor} &= \frac{233.33}{500} \approx 0.467 \text{ or } 46.7\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** A load factor of 46.7% is **moderate-to-low**. The consumer can improve it (and reduce the bill) by **shifting some peak-hour load to off-peak hours**, again highlighting the **synergy between load factor improvement and TOD tariff optimization**.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing kWh with kVAh** — TOD meters can register both **active energy (kWh)** and **reactive energy (kVARh)**. If the question states "kVAh," remember that $kVAh = \frac{kWh}{PF}$. Do NOT multiply kWh by PF.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to verify energy conservation** — Always check that $\sum E_{slot} = E_{total}$ in numerical problems. KTU examiners award a partial mark for this sanity check.
>
> 3. **Ignoring the "Load Factor" concept** — In TOD-based numerical questions, KTU often asks for load factor as a follow-up. Memorize: $LF = \frac{E_{total}/T_{total}}{P_{max}}$.
>
> 4. **Misstating peak-hour duration** — In India, peak hours are **06:00–10:00 (4h) and 18:00–22:00 (4h)**, total 8 hours. Normal hours are 8 hours, off-peak are 8 hours. Do not allocate incorrectly (e.g., claiming peak = 10 hours).
>
> 5. **Drawing wrong block diagram** — Many students draw an **induction-type disc meter** when asked for a TOD meter block diagram. KTU strictly expects a **microcontroller + RTC + multi-register** architecture for TOD meters.
>
> 6. **Not mentioning RTC** — A "TOD meter" without an RTC is meaningless. RTC must appear explicitly in your block diagram and explanation.
>
> 7. **Calculation errors with large numbers** — Use the "split and sum" technique: compute each term (peak, normal, off-peak) separately, then sum. Avoid mental arithmetic with 5–6 digit numbers.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Summary — TOD Meter**
>
> - **Full Form:** Time-of-Day Meter.
> - **Type:** Static (electronic) multi-register energy meter.
> - **Core Components:** Voltage sensor (divider/PT) + Current sensor (shunt/CT) + Metering IC (multiplier + integrator) + **Real-Time Clock (RTC)** + Microcontroller + Multi-register EEPROM + LCD + Communication port.
> - **Working Principle:** Energy pulses are **routed** to one of several registers based on the **current time** read from the RTC and matched against a **pre-programmed tariff schedule**.
> - **Typical Indian Tariff Slots:** Peak (06:00–10:00, 18:00–22:00), Normal (10:00–18:00), Off-peak (22:00–06:00). 8 hours each.
> - **Energy Conservation Identity:** $E_{total} = E_{peak} + E_{normal} + E_{off\text{-}peak}$.
> - **Billing Equation:** $Bill = E_p R_p + E_n R_n + E_o R_o$.
> - **Blended Tariff:** $\frac{\text{Total Bill}}{E_{total}}$.
> - **Load Factor:** $\frac{E_{total}/T_{total}}{P_{peak}}$; higher LF indicates better utilization.
> - **Peak-Shaving Savings:** $\Delta E \times (R_{peak} - R_{off\text{-}peak})$ — the financial incentive for load shifting.
> - **Standards:** IS 14697 (AC static TOD meters for HT/LT consumers), CEA Regulations 2006.
> - **Advantages:** Multi-tariff accuracy, load management support, AMR-ready, high accuracy class, tamper detection.
> - **Limitations:** RTC dependency, higher cost, firmware complexity, vulnerability to harmonics/surges.
> - **Applications:** HT industrial billing, commercial complexes, smart grids, EV charging stations, captive power metering.
> - **Historical Note:** Pre-2006, conventional electromechanical meters dominated; post-CEA notification (2006), TOD meters became mandatory for HT consumers above a contract demand threshold (typically ≥ 50 kVA / 100 kVA depending on state).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET205 - MEASUREMENTS AND INSTRUMENTATION (ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Smart Metering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Smart Metering** refers to the advanced system of electricity, gas, water, or heat consumption measurement that incorporates **digital metering technology, two-way communication infrastructure, and intelligent data processing** to enable real-time monitoring, automatic data collection, remote connect/disconnect operations, and bidirectional energy flow management between utilities and consumers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Examiner Approved):**
> A *Smart Meter* is an **electronic device** that records consumption of electric energy in intervals of an hour or less and communicates that information at least daily back to the utility for monitoring and billing. Smart metering systems extend the classical electromechanical/electronic meter with **Automatic Meter Reading (AMR)**, **Advanced Metering Infrastructure (AMI)**, **Home Area Network (HAN)** interfaces, and integration into the **Smart Grid** architecture.

The three foundational pillars of Smart Metering as per the KTU 2024 Outcomes framework are:

$$
\text{Smart Metering} = f(\text{Measurement}, \text{Communication}, \text{Data Management})
$$

- **Measurement:** High-precision digital sampling of voltage, current, power, energy, power factor, and harmonic content using **Analog-to-Digital Converters (ADC)** and **Digital Signal Processors (DSP)**.
- **Communication:** Two-way data exchange between the meter (endpoint) and the utility head-end system using protocols such as **DLMS/COSEM, IEC 62056, IEEE 802.15.4, GSM/GPRS, LTE, or Power Line Communication (PLC)**.
- **Data Management:** Secure storage, timestamping (synchronized via GPS/NTP), and analytics through **Meter Data Management Systems (MDMS)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Smart Postbox":**
> Imagine replacing your traditional analog electricity meter (which is like a letterbox that only the postman can read once a month) with a **Smart Postbox** that:
> 1. **Counts and stores** every kilowatt-hour digitally.
> 2. **Sends an SMS (or email)** to the post office (utility) every 15 minutes with the exact consumption.
> 3. **Receives instructions** from the post office to disconnect supply or change the tariff slab.
> 4. **Talks to your household appliances** through Wi-Fi (HAN) to switch off the geyser when the grid is overloaded.
> 5. **Detects tampering** (like someone trying to break the box) and immediately alerts the utility.
> 6. **Allows solar panels on your roof** to push excess energy back to the grid and credits your account automatically (Net Metering).
>
> That "Smart Postbox" is your **Smart Meter**, and the entire network of such postboxes, communication towers, and central servers is the **Smart Metering Infrastructure**.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

| Parameter | Standard Value / Unit | KTU Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **Measurement Class** | Class 0.2S / 0.5S / 1.0 (as per IS 14697 / IEC 62053-22) | Accuracy classification |
| **Sampling Rate** | **3.2 kHz to 12.8 kHz** (typical for 50 Hz systems, 64–256 samples/cycle) | Harmonic analysis capability |
| **Reporting Interval** | 15 min, 30 min, 60 min (TOU) | Time-of-Use billing |
| **Operating Frequency** | **50 Hz ± 5%** (Indian grid standard) | Base frequency |
| **Line Voltage (India)** | **230 V (single-phase) / 415 V (three-phase)** | Indian domestic standard |
| **Communication Frequency** | **2.4 GHz (ZigBee), 868/915 MHz (RF), CENELEC-A/B/C/D bands (PLC)** | RF/PLC bands |
| **Memory Retention** | **≥ 10 years** (non-volatile) | Regulatory compliance |
| **Clock Accuracy** | **± 0.5 sec/day** (with NTP/GPS sync) | TOU billing accuracy |
| **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures)** | **≥ 20 years** | Reliability metric |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Examiner Insight:** When asked to define smart metering, always mention the **three Cs** — *Collection (measurement), Communication (two-way), Computation (analytics)*. This phrasing is highly valued in 14-mark answers.

## 1.4 Classification of Smart Metering Systems

```
Smart Metering
├── By Energy Type
│   ├── Smart Electricity Meter
│   ├── Smart Gas Meter
│   ├── Smart Water Meter
│   └── Smart Heat Meter
├── By Functionality
│   ├── AMR (Automatic Meter Reading) — one-way
│   ├── AMI (Advanced Metering Infrastructure) — two-way
│   └── AMM (Advanced Meter Management)
├── By Communication
│   ├── PLC (Power Line Communication)
│   ├── RF / Wireless (ZigBee, LoRa, Wi-Fi)
│   └── Cellular (GSM, GPRS, LTE, NB-IoT)
└── By Grid Role
    ├── Consumer-side (End-point meter)
    ├── Distribution-side (Feeder/DT meter)
    └── Prosumer-side (Net/Bidirectional meter)
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Distinction — AMR vs AMI:**
> - **AMR (Automatic Meter Reading):** *One-way* communication — meter sends data to utility. Used only for billing.
> - **AMI (Advanced Metering Infrastructure):** *Two-way* communication — meter and utility exchange data, commands, firmware updates, and pricing signals. Enables Demand Response, outage management, and remote control.
> - KTU 2024 syllabus classifies Smart Metering under **AMI**, not plain AMR.
