# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part A carries 3 marks per question, 5 – 6 such questions per module, each expecting a 2-to-3 sentence crisp answer plus a labelled diagram.

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`

**Define a recorder. List the three principal types of recorders used in instrumentation systems.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **recorder** is an instrument that produces a permanent, continuous record of a measured electrical or transduced quantity so that the variation can be analysed later. According to the KTU 2024 syllabus, the three principal types are:

1. **Strip-chart recorder** — records a variable *vs. time* on a moving paper strip.
2. **X–Y recorder** — records one variable *vs. another variable* on a flat sheet, using two servo-driven axes.
3. **Magnetic tape recorder** — records the signal as magnetisation patterns on magnetic tape, allowing re-play and re-analysis.

(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for the three types, 1 mark for the distinguishing independent variable.)

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`

**Compare a galvanometric strip-chart recorder with a potentiometric strip-chart recorder in terms of accuracy and principle.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Galvanometric | Potentiometric |
|---|---|---|
| Principle | Direct deflection of a D'Arsonval coil by the signal current | Null-balance comparison against a precision reference slide-wire |
| Accuracy | ±2 % of full scale | ±0.1 % of full scale |
| Bandwidth | DC – 100 Hz | DC – 10 Hz (slower) |
| Input impedance | Low | High |

(1 mark each for principle, accuracy values, and bandwidth / input impedance contrast.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions

> **KTU 2024 Pattern (Module-Level Internal Choice, 14 marks):** Two questions are given; the student answers **one**. Each question has two sub-parts, usually **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**, often the first part being descriptive/principle and the second part being analytical/comparison/applications.

### Question A — 14 Marks (Choose Either A or B)

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain with a neat block diagram the working principle of an **X–Y recorder**. How does the servo loop keep the pen positioned correctly?

**(b) [7 marks]** List and explain any **four industrial / laboratory applications** of X–Y recorders, mentioning the type of curve traced in each case.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**(a) Working principle of X–Y recorder [7 marks]:**

An X–Y recorder is a two-axis, null-balance recording instrument that plots one electrical variable on the vertical (*Y*) axis as a function of another electrical variable on the horizontal (*X*) axis. Each axis works as a **closed-loop servo system**.

- The *X* input signal is applied to one input of a **comparator**; the *Y* input signal is applied to one input of a second comparator.
- The other input of each comparator is connected to the wiper of a **precision slide-wire potentiometer** that is mechanically coupled to the pen carriage.
- The comparator produces an **error voltage** *E* = (reference voltage) − (input signal).
- This error is amplified by a **chopper-stabilised DC amplifier** (chopper is used to convert DC error to AC for low-drift amplification, then synchronously demodulated).
- The amplified error drives a **two-phase servo motor**.
- The servo motor rotates a **lead screw**, which moves the pen carriage along the axis until the slide-wire wiper reaches the position where the error becomes zero. At this point the motor stops.
- Because both axes work simultaneously and continuously, the pen continuously adjusts its X and Y position so that *Y* is always equal to the Y-input and *X* is always equal to the X-input. The path of the pen therefore draws the curve **Y vs. X**.

(3 marks for the block-diagram explanation, 2 marks for the comparator and slide-wire null principle, 2 marks for the chopper amplifier + servo motor drive.)

**(b) Four applications of X–Y recorders [7 marks]:**

1. **Plotting V–I characteristics of diodes and transistors** — the X input is voltage, Y input is current; the pen directly draws the V–I curve including knee and breakdown regions.
2. **B–H (magnetisation) curves of ferromagnetic materials** — X = H (from field coil current), Y = B (from search-coil flux); the resulting closed loop is the hysteresis curve whose area gives the core loss per cycle.
3. **Pressure–Volume (P–V) loops in engines and biomedical lungs** — X = volume, Y = pressure; the area inside the loop is the work done per cycle.
4. **Calibration curves of transducers** — X = input mechanical quantity, Y = output voltage; produces a direct, highly accurate calibration graph for documentation.
5. **Frequency-response curves (Bode plots)** — X = log-frequency from a sweep generator, Y = output amplitude from a detector.
6. **Stress–strain curves in material testing** — X = strain (from extensometer), Y = stress (from load cell).

(2 marks for naming four applications clearly; 5 marks distributed for explaining *what curve is traced* in each case and *why the X–Y format is the natural choice*.)

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **working principle of a magnetic tape recorder in FM mode** with a block diagram. Why is FM preferred over direct recording for instrumentation?

**(b) [7 marks]** A magnetic tape moves past the record head at **75 mm/s**. The FM carrier is **27 kHz** with a frequency deviation of **±40 %** for a ±1 V input. Compute the **maximum modulating (input signal) frequency** that can be recorded using Carson's rule, and state the **wavelength of the magnetisation on tape** at the carrier centre frequency.

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**(a) FM magnetic tape recorder — principle [7 marks]:**

In FM magnetic recording, the **input signal voltage** is *not* applied directly to the record head. Instead, it is used to **frequency-modulate a high-frequency carrier** generated by a voltage-controlled oscillator (VCO). The carrier is then sent to the record head, which writes the frequency-modulated flux pattern on the tape. On playback, the inverse process recovers the original signal.

**Block diagram and operation:**

- **Input signal** *v(t)* enters the **voltage-controlled oscillator (VCO)**.
- The VCO produces a sinusoidal carrier whose instantaneous frequency is $f(t) = f_0 + \Delta f \cdot k \cdot v(t)$, where *f₀* = centre carrier frequency and *Δf* = peak deviation.
- The FM carrier is amplified and fed to the **record head**, which magnetises the tape with flux transitions spaced according to the instantaneous frequency.
- On playback, the **playback head** senses the flux changes; the small reproduced voltage is amplified and **limited** (limiter removes amplitude noise picked up from tape irregularities).
- A **frequency discriminator** (e.g., a pulse-counting discriminator or a Foster–Seeley discriminator) converts the FM signal back to a voltage proportional to the original modulating signal.
- A **low-pass filter** removes residual carrier and noise; the original *v(t)* emerges at the output.

**Why FM is preferred over direct recording for instrumentation:**

1. FM recording can handle **DC and very low frequencies** because the information is carried in *frequency*, not in *amplitude* of flux.
2. Amplitude variations due to tape irregularities, dropout, and head contact do not affect the recovered signal (the limiter removes them).
3. The **signal-to-noise ratio is improved** by a factor proportional to (Δf / fₘ)², where *fₘ* is the modulating frequency (FM noise advantage).
4. Direct recording cannot record DC, and has poor low-frequency response (1 dB down at ~50 Hz), making it unsuitable for instrumentation.

(3 marks for the block diagram; 2 marks for the VCO/limiter/discriminator equations; 2 marks for the four advantages of FM over direct recording.)

**(b) Numerical problem [7 marks]:**

**Given data:**

- Tape speed, $v_{tape} = 75\ \text{mm/s} = 0.075\ \text{m/s}$
- Carrier centre frequency, $f_0 = 27\ \text{kHz} = 27000\ \text{Hz}$
- Frequency deviation, $\Delta f = 40\%\ \text{of}\ f_0 = 0.4 \times 27000 = 10800\ \text{Hz}$
- Modulating input level = ±1 V

**Step 1 — Apply Carson's rule to find the maximum modulating frequency *fₘ* (max).**

Carson's rule for the bandwidth of an FM signal is:

$$BW = 2(\Delta f + f_m)$$

The FM system is "narrowband" if $\Delta f \le f_m$ and "wideband" otherwise. For instrumentation FM, the design usually makes $f_m \le \Delta f$ (wideband FM) so that the signal-to-noise advantage is maximised. Equating the system bandwidth to the tape recorder's usable bandwidth is a common KTU assumption. Assuming the tape recorder channel can pass frequencies up to the **third harmonic of the carrier deviation-limited bandwidth** with full fidelity, the design rule of thumb often used in IRIG-standard FM instrumentation tape recorders is:

$$f_{m,\max} = \frac{\Delta f}{2}$$

Substituting:

$$f_{m,\max} = \frac{10800}{2} = 5400\ \text{Hz} = 5.4\ \text{kHz}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the wavelength of the recorded magnetisation on tape at the carrier centre frequency.**

The wavelength is the tape speed divided by the recorded frequency:

$$\lambda_0 = \frac{v_{tape}}{f_0} = \frac{0.075}{27000} \approx 2.78 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m} = 2.78\ \mu\text{m}$$

**Step 3 — Engineering interpretation [1 mark of the 7]:**

A 5.4 kHz maximum modulating frequency means the recorder can faithfully capture signals whose information bandwidth lies below ~5 kHz — perfectly adequate for **vibration, audio-frequency telemetry, biomedical signals (ECG, EEG, EMG), and process control** but not for high-frequency vibration or radar. The 2.78 μm wavelength is well within the resolution of standard instrumentation tape coatings (which support features down to ~1 μm), confirming the recording is feasible.

(2 marks for Carson's rule, 2 marks for the wavelength calculation, 1 mark for the relation between wavelength and tape coating resolution, 2 marks for the engineering interpretation.)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students *actually* lose marks on this topic (Dec 2023 / May 2024 examiner feedback):**
>
> 1. **Confusing "block diagram" with "circuit diagram".** The KTU question asking for a *block diagram* does **not** require component-level schematics (resistor values, op-amp pin numbers). A clean block diagram with named blocks and labelled signal flow earns full marks. Drawing a circuit loses time and still earns only partial credit.
> 2. **Forgetting the *Y vs. X* — *not* Y vs. time — for an X–Y recorder.** This single error can cost 2 of 7 marks because the *definition* of an X–Y recorder hinges on the fact that the independent variable is *not* time.
> 3. **Direct recording vs. FM recording confusion.** Many students write "magnetic tape recorders use AM" — wrong. Instrumentation tape recorders use **FM** almost exclusively; AM is not used in instrumentation.
> 4. **Skipping the comparator/slide-wire null-balance step** in the X–Y explanation. Without the null-balance idea, the servo motor has no defined stopping criterion and the answer is logically incomplete.
> 5. **Numerical-answer engineering interpretation.** A numerical answer without a final "interpretation" sentence forfeits the last 1 mark in every KTU numerical problem. Always close with: *"This corresponds to ... which is suitable/unsuitable for ... applications."*
> 6. **Forgetting to label axes, units, and the centre frequency** in diagrams of FM magnetic recording. The marker may deduct 0.5 – 1 mark for missing units.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-revision checklist (final 60-second read before the exam):**

- ✅ A **recorder** is a *permanent-record* instrument — that is the key distinction from a meter or CRO.
- ✅ Three principal types per KTU 2024 syllabus: **Strip-chart, X–Y, Magnetic tape**.
- ✅ Strip-chart = variable **vs. time**; X–Y = variable **vs. another variable**; Magnetic = stored magnetisation, **re-playable**.
- ✅ Strip-chart family has three sub-types: **galvanometric, potentiometric (null-balance), multi-point**.
- ✅ Galvanometric strip-chart accuracy ≈ **±2 %**; potentiometric accuracy ≈ **±0.1 %** — remember the order of magnitude difference.
- ✅ X–Y recorder uses **two independent servo loops**, each with **comparator + chopper amplifier + 2-phase servo motor + lead screw + slide-wire feedback**.
- ✅ X–Y recorder applications: **V–I, B–H, P–V, calibration, Bode, stress–strain** — pick any four.
- ✅ Magnetic tape recorders use **FM (not AM, not direct)** for instrumentation; direct recording is used for audio only.
- ✅ **VCO equation:** $f(t) = f_0 + \Delta f \cdot k \cdot v(t)$.
- ✅ **Galvanometer equation of motion:** $J\ddot\theta + D\dot\theta + K\theta = BINA$.
- ✅ **Natural frequency:** $\omega_n = \sqrt{K/J}$, in Hz: $f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{K/J}$.
- ✅ **Damping ratio:** $\zeta = \dfrac{D}{2\sqrt{KJ}}$; optimal ζ ≈ **0.6 – 0.7** for a flat recorder pass-band.
- ✅ **Wavelength on tape:** $\lambda = v_{tape}/f_{record}$.
- ✅ **Carson's rule** for FM bandwidth: $BW = 2(\Delta f + f_m)$.
- ✅ Always end a numerical answer with a **one-sentence engineering interpretation** to capture the final mark.
- ✅ Always draw **clean block diagrams** with named blocks and arrows; **never** confuse block diagrams with circuit schematics.
- ✅ X–Y recorders plot Y **vs. X**, *not* Y vs. time — getting this wrong is the single most common definitional error.
- ✅ FM recording is preferred over direct recording in instrumentation because of (i) DC response, (ii) amplitude-noise immunity, (iii) better S/N, and (iv) flat frequency response.
- ✅ **IRIG standard centre frequencies:** **27 kHz, 54 kHz, 108 kHz, 216 kHz, 432 kHz, 864 kHz** — useful for distinguishing instrumentation tape recorders.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Measurement of Angular Speed

**Angular speed** ($\omega$) is the rate of change of angular displacement with respect to time, measured in **radians per second (rad/s)** or **revolutions per minute (RPM)**. In engineering practice, the corresponding sensor is called a **tachometer** (from Greek *tachos* = speed).

$$ \omega = \frac{d\theta}{dt} \quad \text{(rad/s)} $$

$$ N = \frac{60\,\omega}{2\pi} \quad \text{(RPM)} $$

### Classification of Tachometers
- **Analog Tachometers** — Permanent Magnet DC (PMDC) Generator Tachometer, Drag-Cup AC Tachometer, Eddy-Current Tachometer.
- **Digital Tachometers** — Optical (photoelectric) tachometer, Magnetic-pickup tachometer, Stroboscopic tachometer, Inductive proximity tachometer.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A *digital tachometer* converts rotational pulses into a frequency, then into a proportional voltage or RPM reading using a frequency-to-voltage converter (FVC). The key formula is **N = (f × 60) / P**, where *f* is the pulse frequency and *P* is the number of pulses per revolution.

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine a bicycle wheel with a small bell attached to its rim. Every time the wheel turns once, the bell rings **once**. If you count **60 rings in one minute**, the wheel is rotating at exactly **60 RPM**. A digital tachometer works on the same idea — it counts *pulses* generated per revolution and converts that count into a speed display.

---

## 1.2 Measurement of Luminous Intensity

**Luminous intensity** is a photometric quantity that measures the *visible* light power emitted by a source in a particular direction per unit solid angle. The SI unit is the **candela (cd)**.

$$ I_v = \frac{d\Phi_v}{d\Omega} \quad \text{(candela, cd)} $$

Where:
- $\Phi_v$ = Luminous flux (in **lumens, lm**)
- $\Omega$ = Solid angle (in **steradians, sr**)

| Photometric Quantity | Symbol | Unit | Definition |
|---|---|---|---|
| Luminous Intensity | $I_v$ | **candela (cd)** | Flux per unit solid angle |
| Luminous Flux | $\Phi_v$ | **lumen (lm)** | Total visible light emitted |
| Illuminance | $E_v$ | **lux (lx)** | Flux per unit area ($lm/m^2$) |
| Luminance | $L_v$ | **cd/m²** | Intensity per unit projected area |

The standard instruments for measuring these quantities are **lux meters** (illuminance) and **photometers / luminance meters** (intensity). They use photosensors like:
- **Photovoltaic cells** (selenium, silicon) — generate voltage proportional to light
- **Photoconductive cells** (CdS, LDR) — change resistance with light
- **Photodiodes / Phototransistors** — fast response, used in modern meters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core KTU Point:** The human eye is most sensitive to **green light at 555 nm wavelength** (photopic vision). All photometric units are weighted by the CIE photopic luminosity function $V(\lambda)$, so a radiometric watt at 555 nm equals **683 lumens**.

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine a flashlight in a dark room. *Luminous intensity* asks: "How bright is the beam in *one specific direction*?" (cd). *Luminous flux* asks: "How much total visible light is the flashlight pouring out in *all directions*?" (lm). *Illuminance* asks: "How much of that light actually lands on a *book on the table*?" (lx). A **lux meter** placed on the book gives the illuminance directly.

---

## 1.3 Digital Data Acquisition System (DAS)

A **Data Acquisition System (DAS)** is an integrated assembly of hardware and software whose purpose is to collect, condition, digitize, store, and display real-world physical signals so they can be analyzed by a computer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** A digital DAS converts analog input signals into digital form using an **ADC** and provides the data to a digital processor for storage, display, and analysis. The fundamental block sequence is: **Sensor → Signal Conditioner → Multiplexer → ADC → Computer/Display**.

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of a hospital ICU monitor. Multiple sensors (ECG, BP, SpO₂, temperature) generate weak, noisy electrical signals. Each signal is first **filtered and amplified** (signal conditioning), then **routed one at a time** to a single **analog-to-digital converter** (multiplexing), converted to a number the computer understands (digitization), and finally **plotted on the monitor**. A DAS works exactly the same way on industrial processes.

---

## 1.4 Signal Conditioning Systems

**Signal conditioning** is the step that prepares a raw sensor output for the ADC. Without it, signals may be too weak, too noisy, or in an unusable form.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Note:** "Basic Principles Only" — focus on the *function* of each conditioner, not on derivations.

| Conditioning Function | Purpose |
|---|---|
| **Amplification** | Boost low-level signals (e.g., thermocouple mV) to ADC range |
| **Attenuation** | Reduce high-level signals (e.g., mains 230 V) to safe range |
| **Filtering** | Remove 50/60 Hz noise, EM interference |
| **Isolation** | Galvanic isolation for safety (opto-coupler, transformer) |
| **Linearization** | Convert non-linear sensor output to linear form (RTD, thermistor) |
| **Cold-Junction Compensation** | For thermocouples |
| **Excitation** | Provide stable current/voltage to active sensors (strain gauge, RTD) |
| **Range/Zero Adjustment** | Offset and span calibration |

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine trying to record a faint whisper from across a noisy room. You'd need to (1) **amplify** the whisper, (2) **filter** out the noise, and (3) maybe **shift** the pitch to make it intelligible. Signal conditioning does all three to the sensor's electrical whisper before handing it to the ADC.

---

## 1.5 Phasor Measurement Unit (PMU)

A **Phasor Measurement Unit** is a device that uses synchronized time stamps (typically from **GPS**) to measure the **voltage and current phasors** (magnitude and phase angle) of an electrical signal in real time at high speed.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus:** "Block Schematic and Description Only" — no internal algorithm derivations required.

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of a conductor leading a large orchestra. Each musician is a generator in a different substation. The conductor's baton synchronizes them. The PMU is the conductor's score — it tells every generator, "At exactly this GPS time-stamp, your voltage is X∠θ, and your current is Y∠φ." With this, the grid operator can see the *global state* of the network in real time.

---

## 1.6 Cathode Ray Oscilloscope (CRO)

A **CRO** is an electronic test instrument that visually displays the time-varying waveform of one or more electrical signals on a phosphor screen using a focused electron beam deflected by electric fields.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The CRO is the **universal laboratory tool** for observing voltage vs. time, frequency, phase, and (with Lissajous patterns) frequency ratios.

**Block components of a basic CRO:**
1. CRT (Cathode Ray Tube) with electron gun, deflection plates, phosphor screen
2. Vertical amplifier (Y-axis)
3. Horizontal amplifier (X-axis)
4. Time-base generator (sweep)
5. Trigger circuit
6. Power supply (HV & LV)

---

## 1.7 Digital Storage Oscilloscope (DSO)

A **DSO** is the modern digital equivalent of a CRO. Instead of using a CRT, it samples the input signal with an **ADC**, stores the digital samples in memory, and reconstructs the waveform on an LCD/LED display.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Advantages over CRO:** Storage capability, automatic measurements (Vpp, Vrms, frequency, rise time), cursor measurements, FFT, connectivity (USB, LAN, GPIB), and waveform math.

### Conceptual Analogy
A CRO is like a *Polaroid camera* — you see the waveform live but cannot save or post-process it. A DSO is a *DSLR camera* — you can store, zoom, filter, and analyze the captured waveform on the computer.

---

## 1.8 Harmonic Analyzers

A **harmonic analyzer** is an instrument used to measure the **harmonic content** of a periodic, non-sinusoidal waveform. The result is the **amplitude and phase of each harmonic** (fundamental, 2nd, 3rd, … nth).

Two main types:
- **Tuned-circuit (Wave Analyzer)** — uses a narrow-band filter to scan through frequencies.
- **FFT-based (Digital Spectrum Analyzer)** — uses Fast Fourier Transform on digitized samples.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Total Harmonic Distortion (THD):** The single most important metric reported by harmonic analyzers. It is the ratio of the RMS of all harmonic components to the RMS of the fundamental.

$$ \text{THD} = \frac{\sqrt{V_2^2 + V_3^2 + V_4^2 + \cdots}}{V_1} \times 100\% $$

### Conceptual Analogy
A musical chord played on a guitar is a sum of many pure tones (fundamental + overtones). A harmonic analyzer is like a *graphic equalizer* that shows you exactly how strong each individual note is — letting you judge whether the chord is "clean" or "distorted."

---

## 1.9 Virtual Instrumentation (VI)

**Virtual Instrumentation** is an approach in which the user interacts with a software-based interface (running on a PC) that controls measurement hardware (DAQ card, sensors, actuators). The "instrument" exists primarily as **software**, with the hardware acting as the data conduit.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Idea:** Replace traditional bench-top instruments (oscilloscope, function generator, multimeter) with **one PC + one DAQ card + one software** (e.g., **LabVIEW**, MATLAB, Python).

### Conceptual Analogy
A traditional instrument is a *stand-alone calculator* — fixed buttons, fixed display, fixed function. A virtual instrument is a *smartphone calculator app* — the same hardware (the phone) plus flexible software gives you a scientific calculator, a unit converter, a graph plotter, all in one device. The "instrument" lives in the software.

---

## 1.10 IoT and Data Analytics for Industrial Process — Smart Grid Case Study

The **Smart Grid** is an electricity supply network that uses **IoT sensors, digital communication, and analytics** to monitor, control, and optimize the generation, transmission, distribution, and consumption of electrical power in real time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Case Study Focus:** Show how IoT devices (smart meters, PMUs, RTUs) generate big data, and how analytics (ML, time-series forecasting) turn that data into actionable decisions for grid stability, demand response, outage management, and renewable integration.

### Conceptual Analogy
A traditional power grid is a *blindfolded delivery driver* — he drives the truck but has no GPS, no traffic updates, no customer feedback. A smart grid gives him *real-time GPS, route optimization, customer ETA feedback, and weather alerts* — so deliveries are faster, cheaper, and more reliable. IoT + analytics are the GPS, the optimizer, and the customer-feedback system of the power grid.

---

## 1.11 Snapshot of Major Instrument Categories

| Quantity Measured | Common Instrument(s) | Principle |
|---|---|---|
| Angular Speed | Tachometer (analog/digital) | EMF generation or pulse counting |
| Luminous Intensity | Lux meter, Photometer | Photoelectric effect |
| Analog Signal | DAS | Sensor → Conditioner → ADC → PC |
| Phasor (V, I) | PMU | GPS-synchronized sampling |
| Waveform vs. Time | CRO / DSO | Electron beam deflection / ADC + memory |
| Harmonic Content | Wave/Spectrum Analyzer | Tuned filter or FFT |
| Versatile Measurement | Virtual Instrument | PC + DAQ + Software |
| Grid-wide State | Smart Grid (IoT + Analytics) | Networked PMUs + cloud ML |

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Analysis: Digital Tachometer

A digital optical tachometer works on the principle of **opto-interrupter pulse counting**.

**Operating logic — step by step:**
1. A slotted disc is mounted on the rotating shaft. The number of slots = $P$ (pulses per revolution).
2. As the disc rotates, a light beam (LED) is alternately transmitted and blocked by the slots.
3. A photodiode on the other side generates a **pulse train** of frequency $f$ Hz.
4. A counter measures the number of pulses in a known time window (e.g., 1 second).
5. The RPM is computed digitally:
$$ N = \frac{f \times 60}{P} \quad \text{(RPM)} $$

**Example:** If $P = 60$ slots and the measured frequency is $f = 3000$ Hz,
$$ N = \frac{3000 \times 60}{60} = 3000 \text{ RPM} $$

### 2.1.1 Frequency-to-Voltage Converter (FVC)

For a *quasi-analog* reading, the pulse train is fed to an FVC (e.g., LM2907 / LM2917 IC). The output voltage is:
$$ V_{\text{out}} = K_{F} \cdot f $$
where $K_F$ is a constant set by external RC components. This gives a *tachogenerator-like* analog output from a digital sensor.

---

## 2.2 Detailed Analysis: PMDC Tachogenerator

A **Permanent-Magnet DC Tachogenerator** is essentially a small DC generator whose output voltage is proportional to shaft speed.

$$ E_g = K \cdot \Phi \cdot \omega $$

For a PMDC tachogenerator, $\Phi$ is **constant** (permanent magnet), so:
$$ E_g = K_g \cdot \omega \quad \text{(V)} $$

Where $K_g$ = generated EMF per rad/s. In practical units, for **N in RPM**:
$$ E_g = \frac{K_g \cdot 2\pi \cdot N}{60} $$

The voltage polarity indicates the **direction of rotation** — a key advantage of DC tachogenerators over AC types.

---

## 2.3 Detailed Analysis: Photometry and the Inverse-Square Law

For a point light source of intensity $I_v$ (in candela), the illuminance $E_v$ falling on a surface at distance $r$ (in meters) is:

$$ E_v = \frac{I_v}{r^2} \quad \text{(lux)} $$

This is the **Inverse-Square Law of Illuminance** — illuminance falls off as the square of distance. A lux meter placed at distance $r$ will read this illuminance.

**Example:** A lamp of intensity 200 cd at 2 m gives:
$$ E_v = \frac{200}{(2)^2} = 50 \text{ lux} $$

---

## 2.4 Detailed Analysis: Digital DAS

A generalized **single-channel DAS** performs these steps:

1. **Transducer/Sensor** — converts physical quantity to electrical signal.
2. **Signal Conditioner** — amplifies, filters, linearizes, isolates.
3. **Multiplexer (MUX)** — time-shares one ADC across many channels.
4. **Sample-and-Hold (S/H)** — holds the analog voltage constant during ADC conversion.
5. **ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter)** — converts held voltage to digital code.
6. **Computer / Memory / Display** — stores and displays the digital data.

**Multi-channel DAS variants:**
- **Simultaneous Sampling DAS** — one S/H + ADC per channel (expensive).
- **Multiplexed DAS** — one ADC shared via MUX (cheaper, but channels are sampled sequentially — risk of time skew between channels).
- **Pseudo-simultaneous DAS** — per-channel S/H + shared ADC (best balance).

### 2.4.1 Sampling Theorem (Nyquist–Shannon)

The sampling frequency $f_s$ of the ADC must be **at least twice** the highest frequency component in the input signal:
$$ f_s \geq 2 f_{\max} $$
If violated, **aliasing** occurs — a 900 Hz signal sampled at 1000 Hz appears as 100 Hz.

---

## 2.5 Detailed Analysis: PMU

The PMU block sequence is:
1. **Anti-aliasing filter** — limits input bandwidth.
2. **GPS-synchronized sampling** — typically **N samples per nominal cycle** (e.g., 48, 96, 128 samples/cycle for 50/60 Hz).
3. **Phasor estimation** — uses **Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT)** over one cycle window.
4. **Phasor processor** — outputs magnitude, phase, and rate-of-change-of-frequency (ROCOF).
5. **Time-stamping** — adds the GPS UTC time.
6. **Communication interface** — sends phasor to a **Phasor Data Concentrator (PDC)** using **IEEE C37.118** protocol.

The estimated phasor at fundamental frequency is:
$$ X(k) = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) \cdot e^{-j(2\pi/N)kn} $$

Where $x(n)$ are the discrete samples and $X(k)$ is the phasor at the $k$-th harmonic.

---

## 2.6 Detailed Analysis: CRO

**Key internal operations:**
- **Electron gun** emits electrons, which are accelerated and focused.
- **Y-plates** deflect the beam vertically (signal amplitude).
- **X-plates** deflect horizontally (time base, generated by a sawtooth sweep).
- The phosphor screen glows where the beam strikes.

The **sweep** is a linear **sawtooth voltage** with period $T_s$. If the input signal period is $T_{in} = m \cdot T_s$ (with $m$ integer), the display is **stationary**.

**Deflection sensitivity** is the inverse of **deflection factor**:
$$ S = \frac{1}{D} \quad \text{(mm/V)} $$

Where $D$ is the deflection factor (V/cm or V/div).

---

## 2.7 Detailed Analysis: DSO

**Block components of a DSO:**
1. **Attenuator / Amplifier** (front-end) — matches input range to ADC range.
2. **ADC** — high-speed (often 8-bit, 1–5 GS/s in modern DSOs).
3. **Acquisition Memory** — stores the digital samples.
4. **Time Base** — controls sampling rate.
5. **Trigger Circuit** — synchronizes the display.
6. **Display Processor + LCD/LED** — reconstructs and renders the waveform.
7. **Microprocessor / FPGA** — controls the entire system, runs math, FFT, etc.

**Acquisition modes:**
- **Real-time sampling** — ADC samples in one trigger event.
- **Equivalent-time sampling** — builds waveform over many triggers (for repetitive signals).
- **Peak Detect, Average, Envelope** — display processing modes.

**Key spec: Bandwidth** — must be ≥ 5× the highest frequency of interest (to preserve rise time accurately).

---

## 2.8 Detailed Analysis: Harmonic Analyzer

The output of a harmonic analyzer is a **line spectrum** showing each harmonic amplitude (or power) vs. frequency.

**Total Harmonic Distortion (THD):**
$$ \text{THD} = \frac{\sqrt{\sum_{h=2}^{\infty} V_h^2}}{V_1} = \frac{V_{\text{rms, harmonics}}}{V_1} $$

**Crest Factor:**
$$ \text{CF} = \frac{V_{\text{peak}}}{V_{\text{rms}}} $$

For a pure sine wave, CF = √2 ≈ 1.414. Distorted waveforms have higher CF.

---

## 2.9 Detailed Analysis: Virtual Instrumentation

**VI Architecture:**
1. **DAQ Hardware** — multifunction card (ADC, DAC, DIO, counter/timer).
2. **Driver Layer** — DAQmx (NI) or similar, interfaces hardware with software.
3. **Application Software** — LabVIEW, MATLAB, Python (with PyDAQmx).
4. **Front Panel (UI)** — graphical user interface, often resembles traditional instruments (knobs, dials, graphs).

**Advantages:** flexibility, scalability, remote access, lower cost (one DAQ + software replaces many instruments).

**Disadvantages:** real-time performance is limited by OS scheduling; calibration drift.

---

## 2.10 Detailed Analysis: Smart Grid

The Smart Grid layers are:
1. **Physical Layer** — generation, transmission, distribution, load.
2. **Sensing & Measurement Layer** — smart meters, PMUs, IoT temperature/vibration sensors, weather stations.
3. **Communication Layer** — RF mesh, cellular (4G/5G), fiber, PLC, LoRaWAN.
4. **Data Analytics Layer** — SCADA, EMS, big-data platforms, ML/AI for load forecasting, fault detection, theft detection.
5. **Application Layer** — demand response, automated outage management, renewable integration, dynamic pricing, EV smart charging.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **PMU is to the smart grid what a thermometer is to a hospital** — it provides the real-time vital signs that the entire system relies on for diagnosis and treatment.

---

## 2.11 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** Memorize the units in the third column — KTU frequently tests "what is the SI unit of luminous intensity?" type questions.

| # | Formula | Quantity | Unit | Where Used |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $N = (f \times 60) / P$ | RPM from pulse frequency | RPM | Digital Tachometer |
| 2 | $E_g = K_g \cdot \omega$ | EMF in DC Tachogenerator | Volt | PMDC Tachometer |
| 3 | $E_v = I_v / r^2$ | Illuminance (Inverse-Square) | lux | Photometry |
| 4 | $I_v = d\Phi_v / d\Omega$ | Luminous Intensity | candela | Photometry |
| 5 | $E_v = \Phi_v / A$ | Illuminance from flux | lux | Photometry |
| 6 | $f_s \geq 2 f_{\max}$ | Nyquist Rate | Hz | DAS / DSO / PMU |
| 7 | $V_{\text{out}} = K_F \cdot f$ | FVC output | Volt | Tachometer |
| 8 | $\text{THD} = \frac{\sqrt{\sum V_h^2}}{V_1}$ | Total Harmonic Distortion | % | Harmonic Analyzer |
| 9 | $X(k) = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{N}\sum x(n) e^{-j 2\pi kn/N}$ | DFT Phasor Estimate | p.u. | PMU |
| 10 | $V = A \sin(\omega t + \phi)$ | Phasor in time domain | Volt | PMU / CRO |
| 11 | $E = (P \times t)$ | Energy in a pulse | J | Stroboscope |
| 12 | $D = V / (\text{deflection})$ | Deflection Factor | V/div | CRO |
| 13 | $S = 1/D$ | Deflection Sensitivity | div/V | CRO |

| 15 | $f = 1/T$ | Frequency | Hz | General |
| 16 | $\omega = 2\pi f$ | Angular Frequency | rad/s | General |
| 17 | $P_{\text{avg}} = V_1 I_1 \cos\phi$ | Active Power (fundamental) | W | PMU / Power Quality |
| 18 | $\eta = V_{\text{out}} / V_{\text{in}}$ | Transducer Efficiency | — | General |
| 19 | $\tau = RC$ | Filter Time Constant | s | Signal Conditioning |
| 20 | $G = 20 \log_{10}(A)$ | Gain (dB) | dB | Amplifier / Filter |

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Relationship between Angular Speed in RPM and rad/s

**Given:** A shaft rotates at $N$ revolutions per minute (RPM).

**Step 1:** Number of revolutions per second = $N / 60$ rev/s.

**Step 2:** One revolution = $2\pi$ radians. So angular displacement per second = $(N/60) \times 2\pi$ rad/s.

**Step 3:** This is exactly the angular speed $\omega$:

$$ \omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{\pi N}{30} \quad \text{(rad/s)} $$

**Step 4:** Solving for $N$:

$$ N = \frac{60\,\omega}{2\pi} $$

**Step 5:** Converting to **rps** (revolutions per second) for completeness:

$$ f_{\text{rot}} = \frac{N}{60} = \frac{\omega}{2\pi} \quad \text{(rps, Hz)} $$

**Verification with a numerical example:**
If $N = 1500$ RPM, then:
$$ \omega = \frac{2\pi \times 1500}{60} = 50\pi \approx 157.08 \text{ rad/s} $$

And the rotational frequency is:
$$ f_{\text{rot}} = \frac{1500}{60} = 25 \text{ Hz} $$

This matches the fact that a 4-pole, 50 Hz induction motor runs at 1500 RPM (synchronous speed).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: PMDC Tachogenerator Output EMF

**Given:** A PMDC generator has a permanent-magnet field of constant flux $\Phi$ Wb per pole, with $Z$ total armature conductors, $A$ parallel paths, and $P$ poles.

**Step 1 — Faraday's Law for a rotating machine:** The induced EMF per parallel path is the rate of change of flux linkage. The number of conductors per parallel path is $Z/A$. Each conductor cuts flux $\Phi \cdot P$ in one revolution.

**Step 2 — Flux cut per second:** With shaft speed $N$ rev/s = $N/60$ rev/s, flux cut per second per conductor is $\Phi \cdot P \cdot (N/60)$.

**Step 3 — EMF per parallel path:**
$$ E_g = \frac{Z}{A} \cdot \Phi \cdot P \cdot \frac{N}{60} $$

**Step 4 — Define machine constant:**
$$ K = \frac{Z P}{60 A} $$

So:
$$ E_g = K \cdot \Phi \cdot N $$

**Step 5 — For a PMDC tachogenerator, $\Phi$ is constant**, so we can write:
$$ E_g = K_g \cdot N \quad \text{(V)} $$

Where $K_g$ is the **tachogenerator constant** (V per RPM).

**Step 6 — Expressing in terms of $\omega$ (rad/s):**
$$ N = \frac{60\,\omega}{2\pi} \implies E_g = K_g \cdot \frac{60\,\omega}{2\pi} = K_g' \cdot \omega $$

So the output voltage is **linear in angular speed** — the very property that makes the PMDC tachogenerator useful as a speed transducer.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Inverse-Square Law of Illuminance

**Given:** A point source of luminous intensity $I_v$ candela emits flux uniformly into the full solid angle $4\pi$ sr.

**Step 1 — Flux emitted by the source:**
$$ \Phi_v = I_v \cdot 4\pi \quad \text{(lm)} $$

**Step 2 — At a distance $r$, the flux is spread uniformly over the surface of a sphere of area:**
$$ A = 4\pi r^2 $$

**Step 3 — Illuminance is flux per unit area:**
$$ E_v = \frac{\Phi_v}{A} = \frac{I_v \cdot 4\pi}{4\pi r^2} = \frac{I_v}{r^2} $$

$$ \boxed{E_v = \frac{I_v}{r^2} \quad \text{(lux)}} $$

**Step 4 — Lambert's cosine law for non-perpendicular incidence:** If the surface is tilted by angle $\theta$ from the normal to the source, the projected area increases and the illuminance falls:

$$ E_v = \frac{I_v \cos\theta}{r^2} $$

**Numerical verification:**
Source: 100 cd lamp, distance 4 m, perpendicular surface:
$$ E_v = \frac{100}{16} = 6.25 \text{ lux} $$

At 2 m: $E_v = 100/4 = 25$ lux — confirms the inverse-square law (4× brighter at half the distance).

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Maximum Permissible Sampling Frequency (Nyquist)

**Given:** A signal $x(t)$ has a maximum frequency component $f_{\max}$.

**Step 1 — Shannon's Sampling Theorem:** A band-limited signal can be perfectly reconstructed from its samples **if and only if** the sampling frequency is strictly greater than twice the highest frequency:

$$ f_s > 2 f_{\max} $$

**Step 2 — The Nyquist Rate** is the *minimum* sampling frequency that *just* satisfies the theorem:
$$ f_{\text{Nyquist}} = 2 f_{\max} $$

**Step 3 — In practice**, engineers use a **safety factor of 5× to 10×** to allow for filter roll-off and guard bands:
$$ f_s = (5 \text{ to } 10) \times f_{\max} $$

**Step 4 — Aliasing Example:** Let $f_{\text{signal}} = 900$ Hz, $f_s = 1000$ Hz. The aliased frequency is:
$$ f_{\text{alias}} = \vert f_s - f_{\text{signal}} \vert = \vert 1000 - 900 \vert = 100 \text{ Hz} $$

So a 900 Hz signal is misinterpreted as 100 Hz — a serious measurement error if the antialiasing filter is missing.

---

## 3.5 Derivation: PMU Phasor Estimate via DFT

**Given:** A discretized voltage signal $v(n)$ sampled at $f_s$, with $N$ samples per fundamental cycle.

**Step 1 — The fundamental phasor** is obtained by correlating the samples with a reference sine and cosine at the fundamental frequency $f_0 = f_s / N$:

$$ V_c = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} v(n) \cdot \cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi n}{N}\right) $$

$$ V_s = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} v(n) \cdot \sin\!\left(\frac{2\pi n}{N}\right) $$

**Step 2 — Magnitude and Phase:**

$$ \vert V \vert = \sqrt{V_c^2 + V_s^2} $$

$$ \angle V = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{V_s}{V_c}\right) $$

**Step 3 — The complex phasor** combines both:

$$ \mathbf{V} = V_c - j V_s = \vert V \vert \angle\phi $$

**Step 4 — Time-stamping:** Each phasor is tagged with the **GPS time at the center of the DFT window**, with sub-microsecond accuracy. This is what enables **synchrophasor** measurements across the grid.

> [!NOTE]
> The factor $\sqrt{2}/N$ converts the peak value of the sine to its RMS equivalent — that is why the PMU reports phasors in RMS, the engineering standard.

---

## 3.6 Derivation: Total Harmonic Distortion (THD)

**Given:** A non-sinusoidal periodic signal $v(t)$ expressed as a Fourier series:

$$ v(t) = V_0 + \sum_{h=1}^{\infty} V_h \sin(h\omega t + \phi_h) $$

**Step 1 — RMS value of the total signal:**

$$ V_{\text{rms}}^2 = V_0^2 + \sum_{h=1}^{\infty} \frac{V_h^2}{2} $$

**Step 2 — RMS value of the fundamental:**

$$ V_{1,\text{rms}} = \frac{V_1}{\sqrt{2}} $$

**Step 3 — RMS of all harmonics (excluding fundamental):**

$$ V_{H,\text{rms}} = \sqrt{\sum_{h=2}^{\infty} \frac{V_h^2}{2}} $$

**Step 4 — Define THD:**

$$ \boxed{\text{THD} = \frac{V_{H,\text{rms}}}{V_{1,\text{rms}}} = \frac{\sqrt{\sum_{h=2}^{\infty} V_h^2}}{V_1}} $$

**Numerical Example:** A waveform has fundamental $V_1 = 230$ V, 3rd harmonic $V_3 = 12$ V, 5th harmonic $V_5 = 8$ V, 7th harmonic $V_7 = 4$ V. Then:

$$ \text{THD} = \frac{\sqrt{12^2 + 8^2 + 4^2}}{230} = \frac{\sqrt{144+64+16}}{230} = \frac{\sqrt{224}}{230} \approx 6.51\% $$

For IEEE 519 compliance, individual harmonic voltages and THD are tightly regulated at the point of common coupling (PCC).

---

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Instrument

| Measurement Need | Best Choice | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Continuous analog speed in feedback loop | **DC Tachogenerator** | Direct EMF proportional to speed |
| Discrete RPM reading on a display | **Digital Optical Tachometer** | Direct digital readout, no ADC needed |
| Stroboscopic inspection of rotating machinery | **Stroboscope** | "Freezes" motion visually |
| Illuminance of a workspace | **Lux meter with cosine-corrected sensor** | Direct lux readout, calibrated to $V(\lambda)$ |
| Many sensors monitored from one PC | **Multiplexed DAS** | Cost-effective, single ADC |
| High-speed transient capture | **DSO in real-time mode** | ADC + memory + trigger |
| Power quality audit | **Harmonic Analyzer / Power Quality Meter** | THD, individual harmonics, flicker |
| Synchrophasors across the grid | **PMU + GPS + PDC** | Time-synchronized, IEEE C37.118 |
| Replacing many instruments with one platform | **Virtual Instrumentation (LabVIEW)** | Software-defined, flexible |
| Wide-area grid monitoring | **Smart Grid (PMU + AMI + analytics)** | Scalable, real-time, predictive |

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: General Digital Data Acquisition System (DAS)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Physical Quantity] --> S2[Sensor or Transducer]
    S2 --> S3[Signal Conditioner]
    S3 --> S4[Analog Multiplexer]
    S4 --> S5[Sample and Hold]
    S5 --> S6[Analog to Digital Converter]
    S6 --> S7[Digital Processor and Memory]
    S7 --> S8[Display and Storage]
    S7 --> S9[Communication Interface]
    CLK[Clock and Trigger Circuit] -.-> S4
    CLK -.-> S5
    CLK -.-> S6
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Signals flow left-to-right from the physical world to the digital computer. The dashed arrows from the clock/trigger circuit show that the multiplexer, S/H, and ADC are all **time-synchronized** by a single timing source — essential for accurate sampling.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Phasor Measurement Unit (PMU)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    VT[Voltage Transformer] --> AAF1[Anti Aliasing Filter]
    CT[Current Transformer] --> AAF2[Anti Aliasing Filter]
    AAF1 --> ADC1[High Speed ADC]
    AAF2 --> ADC2[High Speed ADC]
    GPS[GPS Receiver and Clock] --> TS1[Time Stamp Unit]
    GPS --> ADC1
    GPS --> ADC2
    ADC1 --> DFT[DFT Phasor Estimator]
    ADC2 --> DFT
    DFT --> PP[Phasor Processor Magnitude Angle and ROCOF]
    PP --> COM[Communication Port IEEE C37.118]
    TS1 --> COM
    COM --> PDC[Phasor Data Concentrator PDC]
    PDC --> EMS[Energy Management System]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The GPS receiver provides two things: (1) **a precise 1-PPS timing pulse** used to synchronize the ADC sampling, and (2) a **time-stamp** attached to each phasor estimate so the PDC can align phasors from PMUs across the entire grid.

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Cathode Ray Oscilloscope (CRO)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN[Input Signal] --> ATT[Attenuator]
    ATT --> VA[Vertical Amplifier]
    VA --> YP[Y Deflection Plates]
    TBG[Time Base Generator] --> HA[Horizontal Amplifier]
    HA --> XP[X Deflection Plates]
    TRG[Trigger Circuit] --> TBG
    CRT[CRT Electron Gun and Phosphor Screen] --> YP
    CRT --> XP
    HVT[High Voltage Power Supply] --> CRT
    LVT[Low Voltage Power Supply] --> VA
    LVT --> HA
    LVT --> TBG
    LVT --> TRG
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **time-base generator** produces a **linear sawtooth voltage** that pulls the beam across the screen at a constant horizontal speed — converting the time axis into a spatial axis on the CRT screen.

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Digital Storage Oscilloscope (DSO)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    IN[Analog Input] --> ATT[Attenuator and Impedance Match]
    ATT --> AA[Programmable Amplifier]
    AA --> TRG[Trigger Circuit]
    AA --> ADC[High Speed ADC]
    CLK[Time Base and Clock] --> ADC
    TRG --> MEM[Acquisition Memory]
    ADC --> MEM
    MEM --> PROC[MICROPROCESSOR OR FPGA]
    PROC --> DISP[LCD or LED Display]
    PROC --> MATH[Math and FFT Engine]
    PROC --> CURS[Cursors and Measurements]
    PROC --> IO[USB LAN GPIB Interface]
    IO --> PC[External Computer]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Modern DSOs use **FPGA-based** processing pipelines for real-time math, FFT, protocol decoding (I²C, SPI, UART, CAN), and mask testing. This is what makes a DSO far more powerful than a CRO for embedded-system debugging.

---

## 4.5 Block Diagram: Harmonic Analyzer (FFT-Based Spectrum Analyzer)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN[Input Signal] --> ATT[Attenuator]
    ATT --> AA[Low Noise Amplifier]
    AA --> BPF[Bandpass Filter]
    BPF --> ADC[High Resolution ADC]
    ADC --> FFT[FFT Processor]
    FFT --> DSP[DSP Engine for Windowing and Averaging]
    DSP --> DISP[Spectrum Display Frequency vs Amplitude]
    DSP --> METER[THD and Crest Factor Meter]
    METER --> READ[Numerical Readout]
    CLK[Frequency Reference] --> ADC
    CLK --> FFT
```

> [!NOTE]
> Windowing (Hann, Hamming, Blackman) is applied **before** the FFT to reduce spectral leakage — a critical step when analyzing non-synchronous waveforms.

---

## 4.6 Block Diagram: Virtual Instrumentation System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SENSOR[Physical Sensors] --> DAQ[DAQ Hardware ADC DAC DIO Counter]
    DAQ --> DRV[DAQmx Driver Layer]
    DRV --> SW[Application Software LabVIEW MATLAB Python]
    SW --> UI[Front Panel UI on PC]
    SW --> PROC[Signal Processing Math FFT Filter]
    PROC --> DISP[Graphical Display]
    UI --> CTRL[User Control and Automation]
    CTRL --> SW
    SW --> DAQ
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice the **bi-directional arrows**: the user interacts with the front panel, the software sends commands to the DAQ hardware, the hardware acquires data, and the cycle continues. The "instrument" is fully defined by software.

---

## 4.7 Block Diagram: Smart Grid (IoT + Analytics Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    GEN[Power Generation Conventional and Renewable] --> TX[Transmission Network]
    TX --> DX[Distribution Network]
    DX --> LOAD[Industrial Commercial and Residential Loads]
    PMU1[PMU at Substation 1] --> PDC
    PMU2[PMU at Substation 2] --> PDC
    PMU3[PMU at Substation 3] --> PDC
    SM1[Smart Meter Industrial] --> AMI
    SM2[Smart Meter Commercial] --> AMI
    SM3[Smart Meter Residential] --> AMI
    WST[Weather Station IoT] --> CLOUD
    DGI[DG and Inverter Sensors] --> CLOUD
    PDC[Phasor Data Concentrator] --> CLOUD
    AMI[Advanced Metering Infrastructure Head End] --> CLOUD
    CLOUD[Cloud Analytics Platform] --> ML[Machine Learning and Forecasting]
    CLOUD --> BIG[Big Data Storage]
    ML --> EMS[Energy Management System]
    EMS --> DR[Demand Response and Dynamic Pricing]
    EMS --> OMS[Outage Management System]
    EMS --> RI[Renewable Integration Control]
    EMS --> EV[EV Smart Charging]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Smart Grid is essentially a **cyber-physical system** — physical power flow on the top row, and data/decision flow on the bottom. IoT devices are the "sensory organs" and analytics is the "brain" that keeps the system stable and efficient.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the principle of operation of a digital optical tachometer with a neat block diagram.**

**Model Answer:**

A digital optical tachometer measures the rotational speed of a shaft in RPM by counting light pulses.

**Principle of operation:**
1. A **slotted disc** is mounted on the rotating shaft. The number of slots equals $P$ (pulses per revolution).
2. An **LED** is placed on one side of the disc and a **photodiode** on the other.
3. As the disc rotates, the slots alternately **transmit and block** the light beam.
4. The photodiode generates a **pulse train** of frequency $f$ Hz.
5. A **counter** measures the pulses in a fixed time window (typically 1 second).
6. The RPM is computed using:
$$ N = \frac{f \times 60}{P} $$

**Block diagram:**
Sensor (Slotted disc + LED + Photodiode) → Amplifier → Schmitt Trigger → Counter → Display.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define luminous intensity. State the inverse-square law of illuminance and explain its significance.**

**Model Answer:**

**Luminous Intensity ($I_v$):** The luminous flux emitted per unit solid angle by a point source in a given direction. SI unit is the **candela (cd)**.

$$ I_v = \frac{d\Phi_v}{d\Omega} \quad \text{(cd)} $$

**Inverse-Square Law of Illuminance:** The illuminance $E_v$ on a surface, due to a point source of intensity $I_v$ at distance $r$, is:

$$ E_v = \frac{I_v}{r^2} \quad \text{(lux)} $$

**Significance:**
- Doubling the distance reduces the illuminance to **one-fourth**.
- The law is valid only for a **point source** and a **perpendicular** surface.
- For tilted surfaces, multiply by $\cos\theta$: $E_v = I_v \cos\theta / r^2$.
- It forms the basis of lux-meter calibration and lighting design.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question — **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

### (a) Draw the block diagram of a generalized Digital Data Acquisition System (DAS) and explain the function of each block. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Block diagram** (as in Section 4.1 above):
Physical Quantity → Sensor → Signal Conditioner → Multiplexer → Sample & Hold → ADC → Processor/Memory → Display/Storage.

**Function of each block:**

1. **Sensor / Transducer:** Converts the physical quantity (temperature, pressure, strain) into an analog electrical signal (voltage or current). Example: Thermocouple (mV), RTD (Ω), LVDT (V ac).

2. **Signal Conditioner:** Performs amplification, attenuation, filtering, isolation, linearization, and excitation of the sensor output. Its goal is to produce a clean, scaled signal matched to the ADC's input range.

3. **Multiplexer (MUX):** Selects one of many input channels in a time-shared manner and routes it to a single ADC — saving cost on the most expensive component.

4. **Sample-and-Hold (S/H):** Samples the multiplexer output and **holds** the voltage constant during the ADC's conversion time. This prevents errors caused by signal variation mid-conversion.

5. **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC):** Converts the held analog voltage to a digital code (binary number). Resolution is typically 12, 16, or 24 bits. Sampling rate must obey $f_s \geq 2 f_{\max}$.

6. **Digital Processor / Memory:** Stores the digital samples, applies scaling (engineering units), and performs any on-board calculations (averaging, RMS, FFT).

7. **Display / Storage:** Presents the data to the user — LCD, computer screen, or stored in disk for later analysis.

---

### (b) With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a Phasor Measurement Unit (PMU). Mention the role of GPS in PMU. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Block diagram** (as in Section 4.2 above).

**Working:**
1. The voltage and current signals are stepped down by **VT and CT** to safe levels.
2. **Anti-aliasing filters** limit the input bandwidth to below half the sampling rate.
3. A **GPS-synchronized clock** triggers the **high-speed ADC** at a precise rate (e.g., 48 samples/cycle for 50 Hz → $f_s = 2400$ Hz).
4. The **DFT-based phasor estimator** computes the magnitude and phase of the fundamental component for each cycle window.
5. The **phasor processor** also computes the **Rate of Change of Frequency (ROCOF)** for stability studies.
6. Each phasor is **time-stamped** with the GPS UTC time and sent via the **IEEE C37.118** protocol to a **Phasor Data Concentrator (PDC)**, which then forwards to the Energy Management System (EMS).

**Role of GPS:**
- Provides a **1-PPS (pulse per second)** timing reference with sub-microsecond accuracy.
- All PMUs across the grid sample synchronously at the same instant.
- This enables direct **phase-angle comparison** between distant substations, which is the heart of **Wide-Area Monitoring Systems (WAMS)**.
- Without GPS, phasors from different PMUs would have unknown relative time references and could not be compared.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often describe PMU as just "an ADC with a clock." That is **incomplete**. A correct answer must mention (i) GPS synchronization, (ii) DFT-based phasor estimation, (iii) ROCOF, and (iv) the IEEE C37.118 communication protocol. Marks are lost if any of these four are missing.

---

## 5.3 Part B Question — **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

### (a) Draw the block diagram of a Digital Storage Oscilloscope (DSO) and explain its working. Compare it with a CRO. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Block diagram** (as in Section 4.4 above).

**Working of a DSO:**
1. The input signal passes through an **attenuator** and **programmable amplifier** to scale it to the ADC's input range.
2. A **trigger circuit** detects a user-defined event (rising edge, threshold, pulse width) and starts the acquisition.
3. The **time-base clock** drives the **high-speed ADC** at the chosen sampling rate.
4. Samples are stored in **acquisition memory**.
5. The **microprocessor / FPGA** reads the memory and reconstructs the waveform on the **LCD/LED display**.
6. Optional **math, FFT, cursors, automatic measurements, and protocol decoding** are applied.

**Comparison: DSO vs CRO**

| Feature | CRO | DSO |
|---|---|---|
| Display | Phosphor CRT | LCD / LED |
| Storage | No (transient only) | Yes (digital memory) |
| Trigger | Analog edge | Digital, advanced (pulse, runt, logic) |
| Measurements | Manual (graticule) | Automatic (Vpp, Vrms, freq, rise time) |
| Bandwidth | Limited by CRT | Limited by ADC (much higher) |
| Connectivity | None | USB, LAN, GPIB |
| Post-processing | None | Math, FFT, decode |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |
| Portability | Bulky, heavy | Compact, lightweight |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** A common answer to this question lists DSO features without comparing with CRO. The question explicitly asks for a **comparison** — you must present at least 5–6 contrasting points in a table or bullet form. Marks are lost for a one-sided answer.

---

### (b) What is a Harmonic Analyzer? Explain FFT-based spectrum analyzer with block diagram. Define THD and compute it for a waveform with V₁ = 230 V, V₃ = 12 V, V₅ = 8 V, V₇ = 4 V. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Harmonic Analyzer:** An instrument that measures the amplitude (and sometimes phase) of each harmonic component in a periodic, non-sinusoidal waveform. The output is a **line spectrum** (frequency vs. amplitude).

**Types:**
- **Tuned-circuit / Wave Analyzer** (analog, narrow-band filter sweeps through frequencies).
- **FFT-based Spectrum Analyzer** (digital, uses Fast Fourier Transform on digitized samples).

**Block diagram of FFT-based spectrum analyzer** (as in Section 4.5 above).

**Working:**
1. The input signal is attenuated and amplified to a suitable level.
2. A **bandpass filter** limits out-of-band noise.
3. A **high-resolution ADC** (16–24 bit) samples the signal at a rate obeying Nyquist's theorem.
4. A **window function** (Hann, Hamming) is applied to reduce spectral leakage.
5. The **FFT processor** converts the time-domain samples into frequency-domain bins.
6. The **DSP engine** computes magnitude, applies averaging, and displays the spectrum.
7. **THD, SNR, and crest factor** are calculated from the spectrum.

**THD Definition:**

$$ \text{THD} = \frac{\sqrt{V_2^2 + V_3^2 + V_4^2 + \cdots}}{V_1} \times 100\% $$

**Numerical Computation:**
Given: $V_1 = 230$ V, $V_3 = 12$ V, $V_5 = 8$ V, $V_7 = 4$ V.

Sum of squares of harmonics:
$$ V_H^2 = 12^2 + 8^2 + 4^2 = 144 + 64 + 16 = 224 $$

$$ V_H = \sqrt{224} \approx 14.967 \text{ V} $$

$$ \text{THD} = \frac{14.967}{230} \times 100\% = 6.507\% $$

$$\boxed{\text{THD} \approx 6.51\%}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often include $V_1$ (the fundamental) in the numerator square root. It must be **excluded** — THD is the distortion *relative to the fundamental*. Marks lost for this one mistake. Also, units in the final answer (in %) must be shown explicitly.

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 7 ways students lose marks in this module (consolidated):**
> 1. Confusing **luminous intensity (cd)** with **luminous flux (lm)** — they are different quantities.
> 2. Forgetting the **cosine factor** in Lambert's cosine law for illuminance on tilted surfaces.
> 3. Writing the digital-tachometer formula as $N = 60 f$ instead of $N = (60 f)/P$, missing the **slots per revolution** $P$.
> 4. Confusing **PMU (Phasor Measurement Unit)** with a generic power-quality meter — PMU is **GPS-synchronized**; that's its defining feature.
> 5. Describing a DSO block diagram but **forgetting the acquisition memory** and **time-base** blocks.
> 6. Using the **fundamental voltage in the numerator** of THD instead of the harmonic sum.
> 7. Describing Smart Grid as just "IoT + Internet" — it must include **analytics, control, and demand response** to be a complete answer.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Final 60-second recap — memorize these bullets before entering the exam hall.**

- **Angular Speed Unit Conversions:** $\omega = \pi N / 30$ rad/s; $N = 60 f_{\text{rot}}$ RPM; $f_{\text{rot}} = \omega / (2\pi)$ Hz.
- **Digital Tachometer formula:** $N = (f \times 60) / P$, where $P$ = pulses/revolution, $f$ = pulse frequency.
- **PMDC Tachogenerator:** $E_g = K_g \cdot \omega$ — linear with speed; polarity indicates direction.
- **Luminous Intensity:** candela (cd) = lumen/steradian.
- **Illuminance:** $E_v = I_v / r^2$ (lux). Lambert's cosine: $E_v = I_v \cos\theta / r^2$.
- **Digital DAS order:** Sensor → Conditioner → MUX → S/H → ADC → Processor → Display.
- **Nyquist Rate:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{\max}$; aliasing occurs if violated.
- **Signal Conditioning functions:** Amplify, Filter, Isolate, Linearize, Excite, Compensate.
- **PMU:** GPS-synchronized DFT-based phasor estimator; uses IEEE C37.118; outputs magnitude, phase, ROCOF.
- **CRO:** Analog, uses CRT + sawtooth time base. Deflection factor $D$ (V/div), Sensitivity $S$ (div/V).
- **DSO:** Digital ADC + memory + LCD; auto measurements, FFT, math, connectivity.
- **Harmonic Analyzer:** FFT-based preferred; outputs frequency spectrum.
- **THD formula:** $\text{THD} = \sqrt{\sum_{h \geq 2} V_h^2} / V_1 \times 100\%$ — fundamental in **denominator**.
- **Virtual Instrumentation:** PC + DAQ card + software (LabVIEW); software-defined instrument.
- **Smart Grid layers:** Physical → Sensing (PMU, smart meters) → Communication (4G/5G/fiber) → Analytics (ML) → Application (DR, OMS, renewable).
- **Synchrophasor = Phasor + GPS time-tag** — the foundation of wide-area monitoring.
- **Photopic peak:** Human eye most sensitive at 555 nm; 1 W at 555 nm = 683 lm.
- **Real-world example to remember:** 4-pole, 50 Hz induction motor synchronous speed = 1500 RPM — useful for tachometer numericals.
